verse_bank.am_7_7: Amos describes a visible manifestation of the Lord standing on a wall, holding a physical object (an adamant) in His hand. Because a strict unitarian view holds that God cannot be seen or contained in a physical form, this localized, visible appearance of Yahweh demonstrates God manifesting tangibly. Christians recognize this visible Lord as the pre-incarnate Word (the Son).
verse_bank.am_9_1: Amos explicitly states, 'I saw the Lord standing on the altar.' Here, the Prophet physically sees Yahweh standing in a specific, localized place. Since God the Father is an invisible Spirit, this visible person of Yahweh interacting with the prophet on earth is a Christophany—the pre-incarnate Christ, showing God has always had a way to manifest visibly to His creation.
verse_bank.am_4_11: The Lord (Yahweh) speaks in the first person ('I overthrew you... yet not even thus did you return to me, says the Lord') but simultaneously refers to God in the third person ('as God overthrew Sodoma'). This grammatically demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead where one divine Person (the Lord speaking) distinguishes Himself from another divine Person (God in heaven), interacting as the one God.
verse_bank.am_4_13: In the Septuagint, God describes His fundamental acts of creation ('creates the wind', 'forming the morning') and in the very same breath says He 'proclaims to men his Christ'. This places the Messiah (Christ) in the context of the eternal, foundational acts of the universe alongside God the Father, establishing the pre-existence and cosmic, divine significance of the Christ.
verse_bank.gn_19_24: The text explicitly states that the LORD (Yahweh) rained fire and brimstone from the LORD (Yahweh) out of heaven. This describes two distinct persons acting simultaneously who both share the identical divine name, demonstrating a multi-personal Godhead acting together (Two Powers in Heaven).
verse_bank.gn_21_18: The 'angel of God' (identified in verse 17) speaks as God in the first person, saying, 'I will make him a great nation.' Promising to create a great nation is an exclusive divine prerogative and perfectly echoes the promise God Himself made in Genesis 17:20, showing this Angel exercises the authority of God.
verse_bank.gn_16_10: The Angel of the LORD speaks in the first person, claiming the divine prerogative to multiply Hagar's seed ('I will surely multiply...'), an authority and creative power that belongs exclusively to God.
verse_bank.gn_16_13: The Angel of the LORD who appeared to Hagar is explicitly identified by the text and by Hagar herself as 'the Lord God' and 'God who sees me.'
verse_bank.gn_18_1: The text states that 'God appeared' to Abraham, manifesting visibly as one of three 'men' visiting his tent, demonstrating God taking a physical form prior to the Incarnation.
verse_bank.gn_18_13: The physical 'man' who visited Abraham and promised Sarah a son is explicitly identified by the narrator as 'the Lord' (Yahweh), showing the Lord in a localized, human form.
verse_bank.gn_18_22: After two of the men depart for Sodom (later revealed to be created angels), Abraham remains standing before the third person, who is explicitly identified by the text as 'the Lord' (Yahweh).
verse_bank.gn_18_25: Abraham intercedes and directly addresses the visible, physical person standing before him as the one 'that judges the whole earth,' a title and role reserved solely for Almighty God.
verse_bank.gn_15_1: The 'word of the Lord' (Logos) comes to Abram in a vision as a distinct personal presence, speaking directly in the first person as God ('I shield you'). Later in the same conversation (v. 7), this personified Word explicitly identifies Himself by saying, 'I am God.' This establishes that God's Word is not merely a spoken sound, but a divine Person who interacts with humanity and is fully God, laying the explicit foundation for the pre-incarnate Christ.
verse_bank.gn_48_16: Jacob explicitly equates God (mentioned in the previous verse) with 'the angel who delivers me from all evils' by invoking them together to 'bless these boys'. By praying to this Angel for a divine blessing and equating Him with the God of his fathers, Jacob reveals that this Angel is an uncreated, divine person (the pre-incarnate Christ) capable of granting divine favor.
verse_bank.gn_11_7: The LORD (Yahweh) speaks using plural pronouns ('Come, and having gone down let us there confound their tongue'), which demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead deliberating and acting together in unity.
verse_bank.gn_22_12: The Angel of the LORD (who called from heaven in v.11) speaks in the first person as God, telling Abraham, 'for my sake you have not spared your beloved son.' The Angel explicitly identifies Himself as the very God to whom the sacrifice was being offered.
verse_bank.gn_22_15: The Angel of the LORD calls to Abraham a second time and speaks as Yahweh, stating, 'I have sworn by myself, says the Lord.' A created messenger cannot swear by his own name as God; this demonstrates the Angel is a divine Person possessing the authority of Yahweh.
verse_bank.gn_1_26: God uses the plural pronouns 'us' and 'our' ('Let us make man according to our image'), revealing a plurality of distinct Persons communicating and acting as one Creator within the singular Godhead.
verse_bank.gn_2_18: God again speaks in the plural, saying 'let us make for him a help', demonstrating the multi-personal nature of God conversing within the Divine Counsel during creation.
verse_bank.gn_3_22: God explicitly refers to Himself in the plural, stating that Adam has become 'as one of us', which clearly shows distinct, co-equal Persons communicating within the one God.
verse_bank.gn_31_13: Although introduced as the 'angel of God' in verse 11, the speaker explicitly declares 'I am God that appeared to you...' The Angel (Messenger) identifies as God Himself, demonstrating a distinct Person who is sent as a messenger but possesses full divine nature.
verse_bank.gn_32_28: The 'man' who wrestled with Jacob (introduced in v. 24) exercises divine authority by renaming Jacob to Israel and declares that Jacob has 'prevailed with God,' equating His own physical person with God.
verse_bank.gn_32_30: After wrestling with a physical 'man', Jacob names the place 'Face of God' because he explicitly declares, 'I have seen God face to face, and my life was preserved.' This is a clear Christophany showing God appearing and interacting in a tangible human form.
verse_bank.jb_19_25: Job speaks of his coming Deliverer (often translated as Redeemer), explicitly identifying Him as 'eternal'—a strictly divine attribute. He further attributes the power of bodily resurrection to this eternal Deliverer, establishing a prophecy of a divine Messiah.
verse_bank.mi_5_2: This prophecy predicts the coming Messiah (Ruler of Israel) will be born in Bethlehem, yet explicitly states His origins are 'from the beginning, even from eternity.' Since only God is eternal and uncreated, this demonstrates the pre-existent, divine nature of the Messiah before His human birth.
verse_bank.jo_5_16: The 'man' appearing as the Captain of the Lord's host accepts Joshua falling on his face in reverence (which created angels strictly refuse in scripture) and commands Joshua to remove his shoes because the ground is holy. This exactly mirrors Yahweh's divine self-revelation to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:5), demonstrating that this Commander carries the uncreated Divine presence.
verse_bank.jo_6_2: Continuing the uninterrupted encounter with the Captain of the Lord's host from the previous chapter, the text now explicitly identifies this physical 'man' with a drawn sword as 'the Lord' (Yahweh). He speaks in the first person with absolute divine authority ('I deliver Jericho into your power'), proving that this distinct Commander is God Himself appearing in human form prior to the Incarnation.
verse_bank.ex_33_11: The LORD speaks to Moses 'face to face' in a visible, personal interaction. However, in verse 20, God states 'no man shall see my face, and live.' This apparent paradox points to the multi-personal nature of God: Moses spoke with the visible Person of Yahweh (the pre-incarnate Word/Son), who is distinct from the unapproachable, invisible Person of the Father.
verse_bank.ex_23_21: God commands Israel to obey His Angel because 'my name is on him' and the Angel has the authority to not forgive their disobedience ('he will not give way to you'). Possessing the divine Name signifies sharing God's exact essence, and the authority to forgive or withhold forgiveness of sins is a prerogative that belongs to God alone.
verse_bank.ex_24_1: While speaking directly to Moses, the Lord commands him to 'Go up to the Lord'. God is speaking as the commander, yet directs Moses to ascend to the Lord as if referring to a second distinct person. This reveals a multi-personal Godhead where two distinct interacting persons are both identified as the Lord.
verse_bank.ex_34_5: The text explicitly states that 'the Lord' (Yahweh) descended and stood by Moses, and then this same Lord 'called by the name of the Lord.' This grammatically demonstrates the Lord invoking or proclaiming the Lord, revealing a multi-personal Godhead where distinct Persons interact while sharing the one divine name.
verse_bank.ex_14_19: The 'Angel of God' is described as the one leading the camp of Israel and moving alongside the pillar of cloud. Contextually, Exodus 13:21 and 14:24 explicitly state that it is God (Yahweh) Himself who resides in the pillar and leads the people. This equates the Angel of God directly with Yahweh, demonstrating a distinct Divine Person who acts as the visible presence and messenger of the Godhead.
verse_bank.ex_3_4: The figure in the burning bush, introduced in verse 2 as 'an angel of the Lord' (a messenger), is explicitly identified here as 'the Lord' calling out to Moses. This demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead where a distinct Person acts as a messenger of the Lord while being the Lord Himself.
verse_bank.ex_3_6: The 'angel of the Lord' who appeared in the bush (v. 2) speaks in the first person as God, declaring, 'I am the God of your father...' Moses then turns his face away because he is afraid to gaze at God, confirming that this Messenger is truly the one God.
verse_bank.jgs_6_14: The Angel of the LORD speaks with divine authority, taking the direct prerogative of God by personally commissioning Gideon to save Israel and declaring, 'behold, I have sent you.'
verse_bank.jgs_6_21: The Angel of the LORD accepts and miraculously consumes Gideon's sacrificial offering with fire. Since created angels strictly refuse worship and sacrifices, this demonstrates the Angel possesses divine status.
verse_bank.jgs_6_22: Gideon fears for his life because he has seen the Angel of the LORD face to face, a reaction strictly associated with the belief that no man can see God and live. He also addresses Him as 'Lord my God.'
verse_bank.jgs_6_23: Immediately after Gideon realizes he was speaking with the Angel of the LORD and fears for his life, the text identifies the one who responds to comfort him directly as 'the Lord' (Yahweh), inextricably linking the Angel's identity to Yahweh.
verse_bank.jgs_13_18: When asked for His name, the Angel of the LORD states that His name is 'wonderful' (incomprehensible or beyond human understanding). This exact divine title is later used prophetically to describe the coming Divine Messiah in Isaiah 9:6 ('His name shall be called Wonderful... Mighty God'), identifying this pre-incarnate figure as the divine Son.
verse_bank.jgs_13_22: Upon realizing the identity of the 'man' (the Angel of the LORD) who had appeared to them and ascended in the altar's flame, Manoah explicitly exclaims, 'We shall surely die, because we have seen God.' This identifies the distinct, visible messenger sent by the LORD as being God Himself, directly proving a multi-personal Godhead where God can interact with humanity in a visible form.
verse_bank.jgs_2_1: The Angel of the LORD physically appears and speaks in the first person as the God who brought Israel out of Egypt, explicitly claiming ownership of the divine covenant ('my covenant'). A created angel cannot claim God's covenant as his own, identifying this Angel as a visible, distinct person of Yahweh (the pre-incarnate Christ).
verse_bank.prv_30_4: This prophetic riddle describes the Creator performing exclusively divine actions (gathering the winds, binding the waters, having dominion over the earth). It then asks, 'what is his name? or what is the name of his children?' (often translated 'Son' in the MT/other manuscripts). For a Muslim, this text explicitly challenges strict unitarianism by revealing that God has offspring associated with His divine, incomprehensible nature, and points to the divine person who 'comes down' from heaven.
verse_bank.prv_8_23: Wisdom speaks as a distinct, self-aware person who was established 'before time [was] in the beginning.' This reveals the eternal, pre-existent nature of God's Wisdom (the Son) as a distinct person, challenging strict unitarianism.
verse_bank.prv_8_25: Wisdom states that before the physical world existed, 'he begets me.' This demonstrates the eternal generation of the Son, showing that God has eternally had a begotten Word/Wisdom, meaning begetting is an eternal relational reality, not a physical one.
verse_bank.prv_8_27: Wisdom states 'I was present with him' when God prepared the heavens. This reveals a multi-personal Godhead where God and His Wisdom are distinct subjects co-present at the act of creation.
verse_bank.prv_8_30: Wisdom is described as being 'by him' and 'daily I rejoiced in his presence.' This depicts an eternal, interpersonal relationship of joy and communion within God before any creatures existed, proving a plurality of persons within the one God.
verse_bank.is_44_6: The text coordinates two divine titles and figures—'God the King of Israel' and 'the God of hosts'—who then speak together as a single unified entity ('I am the first... beside me there is no God'). This dual-subject/singular-voice structure demonstrates a plurality of Persons united as the one God.
verse_bank.is_45_14: The nations make supplication and bow down to a distinct Person, declaring 'God is in you' and addressing this Person as 'O Lord.' This demonstrates one divine Person receiving worship while containing another ('God is in you'), establishing a clear multi-personal Godhead where God the Father dwells in God the Son.
verse_bank.is_45_15: Continuing the address from verse 14, the Person who has God 'in' Him is now explicitly called 'God' and 'the God of Israel, the Saviour' who was previously hidden ('yet we knew it not'). This is a direct declaration of the pre-incarnate Christ as fully God, sharing the exact divine identity while being distinct from the Father.
verse_bank.is_40_3: This prophecy foretells a forerunner who will prepare the way for the coming of the Messiah. The coming Messiah, whose path is being prepared in the wilderness, is explicitly identified with the divine titles 'the Lord' and 'our God.'
verse_bank.is_40_9: Announcing the arrival of the coming Savior who will shepherd His flock (as detailed in the following verses), the text commands the messenger to proclaim 'Behold your God!', explicitly attributing absolute deity to the coming Messiah.
verse_bank.is_41_22: The Lord God (identified in verse 21) challenges false idols to prove themselves and speaks using plural pronouns ('tell us', 'we will apply', 'we shall know'). This use of grammatical plurality by the one true God points to a multi-personal Godhead.
verse_bank.is_41_23: Continuing His divine challenge to the idols, God repeatedly refers to Himself in the plural ('tell us', 'declare you to us', 'we shall wonder'), revealing a plurality of persons speaking as the singular Godhead.
verse_bank.is_49_7: Yahweh speaks to His chosen Messianic Servant, who is despised by men, and prophesies that kings and princes will 'arise, and shall worship him.' Since true religious worship is strictly reserved for God alone, the fact that the Messiah is given worship from the rulers of the earth points directly to His divine nature and equality in glory.
verse_bank.is_50_6: The speaker in this continuous passage first identifies Himself as the omnipotent Creator who dries up the sea and clothes the sky with darkness (verses 2-3). Without changing speakers, this exact same divine 'I' then says, 'I gave my back to scourges, and my cheeks to blows.' This explicitly prophesies the Incarnation, showing the Creator God taking a physical form capable of suffering, being beaten, and being spat upon.
verse_bank.is_50_7: Continuing the same discourse, the divine Creator who just stated He would suffer physical scourging (verses 2-6) now says, 'the Lord God became my helper.' This reveals a multi-personal Godhead: one divine person (the Suffering Creator) is distinct from and interacts with another divine person ('the Lord God').
verse_bank.is_25_9: A prophetic declaration of the Messianic era ('in that day') where the Savior who comes to deliver His people is explicitly identified as 'our God' and 'the Lord' (Yahweh), demonstrating that the expected coming Savior is God Himself.
verse_bank.is_52_10: The 'holy arm' of the Lord is personified as the source of salvation for all nations. The Messiah is later identified as this 'arm' (Isa 53:1), demonstrating that He is not merely a human agent, but the direct manifestation of God's own divine power and essence.
verse_bank.is_53_1: The Messiah is explicitly referred to as the 'arm of the Lord.' In biblical theology, God's arm represents His inseparable power and presence, indicating that the coming Messiah shares in the divine nature rather than being a separate created entity.
verse_bank.is_53_8: The prophetic question 'who shall declare his generation?' highlights the Messiah's incomprehensible and unsearchable origins. While a mere human's lineage is easily traced, this points to the Messiah's eternal generation and divine pre-existence.
verse_bank.is_53_11: The Servant is given the authority to 'justify the just one who serves many well; and he shall bear their sins.' The absolute authority to inherently justify sinners and expiate their sins belongs exclusively to God, revealing the Servant's divine authority.
verse_bank.is_34_16: The text distinguishes between the Lord who commands and His Spirit who gathers, demonstrating distinct but unified, co-working agencies within the multi-personal Godhead.
verse_bank.is_35_4: The prophet explicitly declares that 'our God' will come and save us. The subsequent verses (35:5-6) describe this coming with the exact Messianic miracles Jesus performed (the blind seeing, the deaf hearing), identifying the coming Savior as God Himself.
verse_bank.is_28_16: God establishes the Messianic cornerstone and promises that 'he that believes [on him] shall by no means be ashamed.' Since the Old Testament explicitly forbids and curses placing saving trust in a mere creature (e.g., Jeremiah 17:5), presenting this Messianic figure as the object of saving faith demonstrates His divine nature.
verse_bank.is_30_27: 'The name of the Lord' is described as a distinct personal entity that 'comes' from afar, possessing 'lips' and 'breath.' This hypostasis (personification) of God's Name acting as a physical, divine person points to a plurality within the Godhead, where the Name/Word of God is a distinct conscious agent.
verse_bank.is_6_1: Isaiah physically sees the Lord sitting on a high and exalted throne. Because God the Father is invisible, this visible manifestation of God is a Theophany of the pre-incarnate Son, demonstrating that God can take a visible form.
verse_bank.is_6_3: The seraphim praise God with a threefold repetition of 'Holy' (the Trisagion). This threefold declaration points to the plurality of Persons in the Godhead (the Trinity), all united as the one 'Lord of hosts.'
verse_bank.is_6_5: Isaiah explicitly declares that he has seen 'with my eyes' the King, the Lord of hosts. This reinforces the Theophany, proving that Yahweh manifested in a form that could be physically seen by human eyes, which points to the pre-incarnate Christ.
verse_bank.is_7_14: The prophesied child to be born of a virgin is named 'Emmanuel,' which means 'God with us.' This serves as a direct prophetic claim that the coming Messiah will be the presence of God Himself among His people.
verse_bank.is_8_8: The land of Judah is directly addressed as belonging to 'God with us' (Emmanuel). This identifies the prophesied child of Isaiah 7:14 as the divine Lord and rightful owner of the promised land.
verse_bank.is_9_6: The child born to rule eternally on David's throne is given the divine title 'Messenger of great counsel' (often translated as the 'Angel of Great Counsel' from the LXX). In Orthodox theology, this explicitly identifies the coming Messiah as the pre-incarnate divine Word, the heavenly Angel of the LORD who shares and reveals the eternal counsel of the Godhead.
verse_bank.is_48_16: The speaker in this passage has just identified Himself as the eternal Creator who founded the earth and the sky (verses 12-13). Yet in this verse, this same eternal Creator explicitly states, 'and now the Lord, [even] the Lord, and his Spirit, has sent me.' This demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead: one eternal, divine Person (the pre-incarnate Word/Son) is sent by the Lord (the Father) and His Spirit, revealing the Trinity.
verse_bank.is_11_4: The Messianic 'rod out of the root of Jesse' is prophesied to exercise the absolute authority of God by judging and striking the earth. He destroys the ungodly simply by the 'word of his mouth' and 'breath of his lips'—demonstrating omnipotent power characteristic of Yahweh.
verse_bank.is_11_10: The Messiah (the Root of Jesse) is described as the one in whom the Gentiles will put their trust. Since the Old Testament repeatedly commands that mankind must trust in God alone and curses those who trust in mere men, the Gentiles placing their religious trust in the Messiah points to His divine nature.
verse_bank.is_61_1: The speaker (the Messiah) declares He is anointed by the Lord and has the Spirit of the Lord upon Him. This clearly reveals three distinct, co-acting persons: the Lord (Father), the Spirit, and the Anointed One (Son), establishing plurality in the Godhead.
verse_bank.is_62_11: The coming 'Saviour' is described as bringing His reward with Him. This is an explicit divine prerogative attributed directly to the Lord God (Yahweh) earlier in Isaiah 40:10, equating the coming Saviour's authority with God Himself.
verse_bank.is_63_10: The text states the Israelites rebelled and 'provoked his Holy Spirit', attributing a personal emotional response (being provoked) to the Holy Spirit as a distinct person from the Lord who saved them, demonstrating plurality within the Godhead.
verse_bank.dn_7_13: The 'Son of Man' is seen coming 'with the clouds of heaven' to the Ancient of Days. Throughout the Old Testament, riding the clouds is an exclusively divine attribute belonging to Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 104:3, Isaiah 19:1). This establishes a 'Two Powers' framework where a distinct person shares God's unique prerogatives.
verse_bank.dn_7_14: The Son of Man is given an 'everlasting dominion' that will never be destroyed, and all nations and languages are commanded to 'serve' Him. To an absolute unitarian, universal service and an eternal kingdom belong solely to God; yet here, they are permanently given to the Messiah, proving His divine nature.
verse_bank.dn_8_16: Daniel hears the 'voice of a man' who commands the archangel Gabriel to explain the vision. Since angels take their commands and decrees directly from God, this 'Man' exhibiting the supreme authority to dispatch and order an archangel is a Christophany—God appearing in the form of a man before the Incarnation.
verse_bank.dn_3_25: A fourth figure miraculously appears in the fiery furnace to protect the three youths. The king explicitly describes this supernatural figure's appearance as 'like the Son of God' (who is then identified as God's sent angel or messenger in v. 28). This demonstrates a visible, pre-incarnate divine person—the Son—manifesting physically to save God's servants, aligning with the Angel of the LORD Christophanies.
verse_bank.1sm_3_10: The text states 'the Lord came, and stood' to call Samuel. This describes Yahweh manifesting in a localized, physical manner. If a unitarian skeptic argues this was merely an angel or a created manifestation to preserve strict incorporeality, they must concede the text explicitly calls this standing, visible figure 'the Lord' (Yahweh). This establishes a Christophany: God taking a visible, approachable form before the Incarnation.
verse_bank.sir_24_3: Wisdom (identified by the Apostles as the pre-incarnate Word/Logos) speaks as a distinct person coming forth from the mouth of the Most High, demonstrating a multi-personal nature within the Godhead.
verse_bank.sir_24_4: Wisdom states 'my throne is in a cloudy pillar', directly identifying this distinct, pre-existent person with the divine presence of Yahweh that led the Israelites in the wilderness.
verse_bank.sir_24_9: Wisdom declares her existence 'from the beginning before the world', proving that this distinct person was pre-existent with God before creation.
verse_bank.sir_24_18: Wisdom explicitly claims to be eternal ('being eternal'), which is an attribute belonging exclusively to God, establishing this distinct person as fully divine.
verse_bank.sir_51_10: The speaker explicitly addresses God as 'the Lord, the Father of my Lord'. This reveals a multi-personal Godhead by distinguishing between God the Father and a second divine person whom the speaker calls 'my Lord', strongly paralleling the divine plurality found in Psalm 110:1.
verse_bank.hos_9_12: Speaking in the first person, God mysteriously declares 'my flesh is of them' (an exclusive reading in the Septuagint). This serves as a direct prophecy of the Incarnation, indicating that God would take human flesh from the lineage of Israel.
verse_bank.hos_11_10: God is the speaker (identifying Himself in the previous verse as 'God, and not man') and says 'I will go after the Lord.' This shows one divine Person speaking about interacting with another divine Person ('the Lord'), demonstrating multi-personality in the Godhead.
verse_bank.hos_12_4: The text states that Jacob 'prevailed with the angel'. The preceding verse identifies this figure explicitly as 'God', and the following verse (12:5) identifies Him as 'the Lord God Almighty'. This equates the Angel whom Jacob wrestled with directly to Yahweh, revealing a distinct visible Person of the Godhead.
verse_bank.hos_1_7: The LORD is speaking in the first person ('I will have mercy...') and declares that He will save the house of Judah 'by the LORD their God.' This demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead, where one divine Person interacts with and acts through another who is equally called the LORD (Yahweh) their God.
verse_bank.zec_4_9: The 'Word of the Lord' (identified as the speaker in verse 8) speaks and says, 'you shall know that the Lord Almighty has sent me to you.' This explicitly demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead where one divine Person (the Word of the Lord) is sent by another (the Lord Almighty).
verse_bank.zec_6_15: Continuing His speech from verse 9, the 'Word of the Lord' states, 'you shall know that the Lord Almighty has sent me to you.' This reinforces the reality of distinct Persons within the one God, as the divine Word is sent as a distinct messenger by the Lord Almighty.
verse_bank.zec_14_4: This prophecy explicitly states that the LORD (Yahweh) will have 'feet' and physically stand on the Mount of Olives, demonstrating that God will take on a physical, incarnate form on earth.
verse_bank.zec_9_9: The promised Messianic King is explicitly described as a 'Saviour' (and the following verse says He will rule to the ends of the earth). In the Old Testament, Yahweh repeatedly declares that He alone is the Savior and there is no other (e.g., Isaiah 43:11). The Messiah bearing this exclusive divine title and universal authority demonstrates His divine nature.
verse_bank.zec_2_8: The speaker is explicitly introduced as 'the Lord Almighty,' yet He states that 'he has sent me.' This demonstrates a multi-personal Godhead where one divine Person (the Lord Almighty) is sent by another.
verse_bank.zec_2_11: The Lord declares that He will dwell in the midst of the people and then says 'you shall know that the Lord Almighty has sent me to you.' This explicitly shows Yahweh being sent by Yahweh on earth, establishing distinct persons within the Godhead.
verse_bank.zec_3_2: The speaker is identified as 'the Lord' (contextually the Angel of the Lord from verse 1), and He says 'The Lord rebuke you.' This shows one divine Person (the Lord on earth) invoking another distinct divine Person (the Lord in heaven).
verse_bank.jer_23_6: The Messianic 'righteous branch' of David is given the name 'Josedec', which is a transliteration of the Hebrew 'Yahweh our Righteousness' (YHWH Tzidkenu). Bestowing the actual divine name of Yahweh upon the coming Davidic King establishes the Messiah's divinity.
verse_bank.jer_27_40: The LORD (Yahweh) is speaking and refers to 'God' overthrowing Sodom in the third person ('As God overthrew Sodom... says the Lord'). This echoes Genesis 19:24 where Yahweh on earth rained fire from Yahweh in heaven, demonstrating distinct persons within the Godhead being referenced by Yahweh Himself.
verse_bank.ez_8_6: The divine figure who visibly appeared to Ezekiel in the 'likeness of a man' (v. 2) speaks directly as God, referring to the temple in Jerusalem as 'my sanctuary.' This demonstrates Yahweh appearing in human form and exercising His divine prerogatives prior to the Incarnation.
verse_bank.ez_43_7: A figure explicitly described as a 'man' (in verse 6) stands beside Ezekiel and speaks in the first person as God Almighty. This 'man' claims the temple as the place of 'my throne', speaks of 'my holy name', and exercises divine judgment, providing a clear Old Testament example of God appearing and speaking directly in human form.
verse_bank.ez_1_26: Ezekiel sees a vision of the ultimate divine throne of heaven, and seated upon it is a figure with the 'appearance of a man'. This explicitly demonstrates that God can and does reveal Himself in human form, laying the Old Testament foundation for the Incarnation of Christ and refuting the Islamic objection that God cannot take human likeness.
verse_bank.ez_2_1: The human-like figure seated on the throne from the preceding verses is explicitly identified here as the 'glory of the Lord' (Yahweh). Ezekiel falls on his face in worship before this Man, who then speaks with the absolute, first-person authority of God. No created angel or prophet can sit on God's throne or accept this level of divine worship.
verse_bank.ez_3_23: The 'glory of the Lord', who previously appeared to Ezekiel with the likeness of a man (1:26), physically 'stood there' before him in the plain. Ezekiel again falls on his face in worship, proving that Yahweh manifested visibly and locally on earth in a recognizable form to interact directly with His prophet.
verse_bank.lam_4_20: The Septuagint refers to the Messiah as the 'anointed Lord' (Christos Kyrios), explicitly granting the Anointed One the divine title of 'Lord' and prophesying that He would be captured and suffer.
verse_bank.dt_32_9: Read in context with verse 8, early Church Fathers noted a distinction between 'the Most High' (the Father) who divides the nations, and 'the Lord' (the pre-incarnate Son) who receives Israel as His specific inheritance, demonstrating a multi-personal Godhead where one divine person receives a portion from another.
verse_bank.dt_32_43: The text commands, 'let all the angels of God worship him'. The New Testament explicitly quotes this exact Septuagint phrasing in Hebrews 1:6, where the Father commands the angels to worship the Son. This proves the Son's distinct personhood and His absolute deity, as He rightfully receives worship.
verse_bank.dt_33_16: Moses specifically invokes the divine favor and blessing of 'him that lived in the bush'. This refers to the Angel of the LORD who appeared in the burning bush (Exodus 3), recognizing this distinct pre-incarnate figure as a divine Person with the sovereign power to bestow heavenly blessings.
verse_bank.ps_44_6: The Messianic King is explicitly addressed as 'O God' whose throne is forever and ever, demonstrating the divine nature of the promised Messiah.
verse_bank.ps_44_7: The text states 'therefore God, your God, has anointed you.' Continuing from the previous verse which called the Messiah 'God', this reveals God anointing God—explicit evidence of multiple distinct Persons sharing the divine nature.
verse_bank.ps_101_25: In the Septuagint structure, the preceding verses frame a dialogue ('He answered him...') which the New Testament (Hebrews 1:10) identifies as the Father speaking to the Son. The Father addresses the Son as 'Lord' (Yahweh) and the very Creator who laid the foundation of the earth, demonstrating a multi-personal Godhead and the eternal divinity of the Son.
verse_bank.ps_101_26: Continuing the Father's address to the Son (as quoted in Hebrews 1:11), this verse contrasts the fading nature of creation with the eternal nature of the Son. It proves the Messiah is not a created being, but the uncreated God who remains while creation perishes.
verse_bank.ps_101_27: Concluding the address to the Son (quoted in Hebrews 1:12), the text explicitly grants the Messiah the attribute of divine immutability ('you are the same') and eternality ('your years shall not fail'), which are exclusive prerogatives of Yahweh.
verse_bank.ps_2_7: The Lord (Yahweh) addresses the Messiah as His 'begotten' Son. Unlike created beings, one who is uniquely 'begotten' of God shares the exact same divine, uncreated nature as the Father, demonstrating a distinct Father-Son relationship within the Godhead.
verse_bank.ps_117_27: Following the Messianic prophecy of the One who 'comes in the name of the Lord' (v. 26), this verse declares 'God is the Lord, and he has shined upon us' (or 'appeared to us' from the Greek 'epephanen'). This directly links the arriving Messiah with God the Lord manifesting Himself to humanity.
verse_bank.ps_142_10: David prays to God and mentions 'your good Spirit' as an active, guiding agent ('shall guide me'). By ascribing personal actions like teaching and guiding to the Spirit of God alongside God Himself, this verse establishes a multi-personal Godhead where the Holy Spirit is a distinct, active divine presence rather than a strict, solitary monad.
verse_bank.ps_71_5: A Messianic prophecy declaring that the King will exist eternally, 'before the moon for ever.' This demonstrates a divine, eternal nature beyond that of any mere human monarch.
verse_bank.ps_71_11: The Messianic King receives universal worship and service from all kings and nations. In strict monotheism, absolute worship and universal service belong exclusively to God, yet here they are commanded for the Messiah.
verse_bank.ps_71_17: The Messiah's name is described as eternal, enduring 'longer than the sun,' and He is identified as the source of blessing for all tribes of the earth. These eternal attributes and the ability to bless all nations point to a divine figure.
verse_bank.ps_103_30: God is described as sending His 'Spirit' to perform the act of creation ('they shall be created'). For a strict unitarian (like a Muslim), creation is exclusively the prerogative of God alone and cannot be done by a created entity. The fact that the Spirit is distinctly sent, yet directly executes the divine act of creating life and renewing the earth, establishes a plurality within the Godhead where the Spirit shares the divine nature.
verse_bank.ps_23_8: In this prophetic dialogue regarding the Ascension, the 'king of Glory' who enters the heavenly gates is explicitly identified as 'the Lord (Yahweh) strong and mighty', establishing that the ascending Messiah is the Lord Himself.
verse_bank.ps_23_10: Concluding the prophetic description of the ascending King of Glory, He is given the ultimate divine title 'The Lord of hosts' (Yahweh Sabaoth), explicitly identifying the entering Messiah with the supreme title of God.
verse_bank.ps_106_20: God is described as actively sending 'His Word' (the Logos) to heal and deliver the people from destruction. This reveals the Word of God not merely as a spoken utterance, but as a distinct, living saving agent sent by God. This establishes the Old Testament foundation for a multi-personal Godhead where the pre-existent divine Word (Christ) is a distinct Person sent by the Father to save humanity.
verse_bank.ps_138_7: David speaks of God's Spirit and His presence as being omnipresent. By distinguishing God from His Spirit, while attributing the absolute divine characteristic of omnipresence to that Spirit, this verse establishes plurality within the one divine nature.
verse_bank.ps_83_7: The Psalmist prophesies explicitly that the 'God of gods shall be seen in Sion.' Since God the Father is invisible and no one has seen Him, this visible manifestation of God on earth in Zion is a direct prophecy of the Incarnation, where God the Son takes on flesh and becomes visible to humanity.
verse_bank.ps_109_1: King David, who is the highest human authority in Israel, refers to the Messiah as 'my Lord'. Here, 'The Lord' (Yahweh) speaks to another 'Lord' (Adonai) and invites Him to sit at His right hand, a position that indicates shared, absolute divine authority over creation. This clearly shows a multi-personal Godhead where God interacts with another distinct, co-ruling divine Person.
verse_bank.ps_109_3: God tells the Messiah, 'I have begotten you from the womb before the morning' (often translated as 'before the morning star'). This explicitly states the eternal generation and pre-existence of the Messiah from the essence of God Himself prior to creation, establishing His divine, uncreated origin.
verse_bank.ps_109_5: The text refers to 'The Lord at your right hand' who will dash kings to pieces in the day of His wrath. The Messiah, seated at the right hand of God, is Himself called 'Lord' and exercises the eschatological judgment and divine wrath over the nations, prerogatives that belong to God alone.
verse_bank.ps_39_6: A clear prophetic reference to the Incarnation where the Messiah addresses God. The phrase 'a body have you prepared me' demonstrates that the Messiah pre-existed as a distinct, divine Person before taking on physical, human flesh.
verse_bank.ps_39_7: The Messiah declares 'Behold, I come', confirming His pre-incarnate, eternal existence and His voluntary, sovereign entry into the world to fulfill God's will as a distinct Person of the Godhead.
verse_bank.ps_46_5: The psalm declares 'God is gone up' (ascended). Since the omnipresent God does not spatially move, this is understood as a prophecy of the Incarnate God the Son's bodily Ascension into heaven, demonstrating that the ascending Messiah is Yahweh Himself.
verse_bank.ps_48_7: The text explicitly declares that no mere man can redeem another or give a ransom to God for his soul. This establishes that the Messiah who provides the ultimate redemption for humanity cannot be just a human prophet, but must be divine.
verse_bank.ps_48_15: Following the absolute rule that no man can redeem a soul from death (v. 7-8), the psalmist reveals that 'God shall deliver my soul from the power of Hades.' This prophesies that the Savior who descends to conquer Hades and redeem humanity is God Himself.
verse_bank.2sm_23_2: Through poetic parallelism, David equates the speaking of the 'Spirit of the Lord' (v. 2) with the speaking of the 'God of Israel' (v. 3). This demonstrates that the Holy Spirit is a distinct, personal agent who speaks and reveals, yet acts with the exact authority and identity of God, establishing plurality within the Godhead.
verse_bank.nm_22_31: Balaam falls on his face and worships the Angel of the LORD. The Angel accepts this reverence, which created angels strictly forbid (as seen in Revelation), pointing to the Angel's identity as a pre-incarnate divine Person of the Godhead.
verse_bank.nm_22_32: The Angel of the LORD speaks with divine authority, declaring that Balaam's disobedience was a direct offense against Him ('your way was not seemly before me').
verse_bank.nm_22_34: Balaam confesses his sin directly to the Angel of the LORD ('I have sinned'). Because sins are ultimately committed against God, this shows Balaam recognizing the divine authority of this visible manifestation.
verse_bank.nm_22_35: The Angel of the LORD claims the absolute prerogative of Yahweh by telling Balaam, 'the word which I shall speak to you, that you shall take heed to speak.' This equates the Angel directly with God, who earlier told Balaam the exact same thing regarding His divine prophetic word (v. 20).
verse_bank.nm_6_27: In the Aaronic blessing (vv. 24-26), 'The Lord' (Yahweh) is invoked three distinct times to impart blessing, grace, and peace. Yet in verse 27, God refers to these three distinct invocations as placing 'my name' (singular) upon the people. This grammatical marker—a threefold invocation of God united under one singular Name—demonstrates plurality within the Godhead, revealing three distinct Persons sharing one indivisible divine Name and essence.
verse_bank.bar_3_37: Referring back to verse 35 ('This is our God'), this verse explicitly prophesies the Incarnation, stating that the one God would later show Himself on earth and live among humans.
verse_bank.tb_8_6: In his prayer, Tobias quotes God's words from Genesis saying, 'let us make to him an aid like to himself.' This reaffirms the use of divine plural pronouns by God when speaking of His own actions, demonstrating a plurality of Persons within the one Creator.
verse_bank.mal_3_1: Yahweh ('the Lord Almighty') declares that a messenger will prepare the way 'before me', yet states that 'the Lord' and 'the angel of the covenant' will suddenly come to 'his temple'. Since the temple belongs exclusively to God, identifying this coming Angel of the Covenant as 'the Lord' entering His own temple explicitly proves the divine nature of the promised Messiah.
verse_bank.ws_7_22: Wisdom is described as the 'worker of all things' (the Creator), establishing this figure as the active, divine agent of creation.
verse_bank.ws_7_25: Wisdom is described as a 'pure influence flowing from the glory of the Almighty,' demonstrating a distinct Person eternally emanating from the Father while sharing His divine glory.
verse_bank.ws_7_26: Wisdom is called the 'brightness of the everlasting light' and the 'image of his goodness.' This explicitly establishes a distinct, co-eternal Person who perfectly mirrors God's essence, a description directly applied to the Son in the New Testament (Hebrews 1:3).
verse_bank.ws_8_3: Wisdom is described as being 'conversant with God' and loved by the Lord. This demonstrates an interpersonal relationship between God and His Wisdom, showing distinct persons within the Godhead.
verse_bank.ws_9_1: God is said to have 'made all things with your word' (Logos). This establishes God's Word as the active, pre-existent agent of creation rather than merely a spoken command.
verse_bank.ws_9_4: Solomon asks for 'wisdom, that sits by your throne.' A distinct figure sharing the very throne of God demonstrates a second Person sharing Yahweh's sovereign authority.
verse_bank.ws_9_9: Wisdom 'was with you' and 'was present when you made the world.' This mirrors John 1:1, revealing a distinct Person eternally co-existing with God and co-creating the universe.
verse_bank.ws_9_17: This verse explicitly lists God, His 'wisdom' (the Son), and His 'Holy Spirit sent from above,' revealing a multi-personal (Trinitarian) operation of God in human salvation.
verse_bank.ws_18_15: God's 'Almighty Word' (Logos) is explicitly personified as a distinct divine figure who leaps down from God's royal throne in heaven to earth as a 'fierce man of war.' This establishes the Word as a distinct, omnipotent Person who shares the divine throne.
verse_bank.ws_18_16: Continuing the description of the Almighty Word, the Word is described as a person who 'touched the heaven, but it stood upon the earth.' This portrays the Word as a distinct divine entity possessing the divine attribute of omnipresence, spanning heaven and earth simultaneously.

verse_bank.jas_5_7: James exhorts believers to be patient 'until the coming of the Lord' (Jesus). In the Old Testament, the eschatological 'coming' to judge and save is exclusively the 'Day of Yahweh' (e.g., Zechariah 14:1-5; Isaiah 13:9). Applying the expectation of Yahweh's arrival to the return of Jesus identifies Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.jas_5_8: James states 'the coming of the Lord is at hand.' The Old Testament prophets eagerly anticipated the arrival of Yahweh. Here, that long-awaited arrival is explicitly the return of Jesus, equating His coming with the arrival of Yahweh.
verse_bank.jas_5_9: James warns that 'the Judge is standing at the door,' referring to the returning Lord (Jesus). In the Old Testament, being the Judge of all the earth is exclusively the prerogative of Yahweh (Genesis 18:25, Psalm 50:6). Jesus is given the title and role of the divine Judge.
verse_bank.jas_5_14: The elders are instructed to anoint the sick 'in the name of the Lord' (Jesus). In the Old Testament, healing is uniquely Yahweh's domain ('I am the Lord, your healer', Exodus 15:26). Calling upon Jesus' name to perform divine healing demonstrates His inherent divine authority.
verse_bank.jas_5_15: The verse states 'the Lord will raise him up' and 'if he has committed sins, he will be forgiven.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the exclusive healer (Deuteronomy 32:39) and the only one who can forgive sins (Isaiah 43:25). James attributes both of these exclusive divine actions directly to Jesus.
verse_bank.jas_4_12: Isaiah 33:22 establishes that Yahweh alone is the lawgiver, judge, and savior. Here, James states there is 'only one lawgiver and judge, he who is able to save and to destroy.' Because James subsequently identifies Jesus as 'the Judge' who is returning (James 5:9), he is directly applying an exclusive, singular divine role to Christ.
verse_bank.jas_1_1: James introduces himself as a 'servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ'. In the Old Testament, the prophets and leaders are exclusively known as the 'servant of the LORD' (Yahweh). By placing Jesus on equal footing with God as his divine Master, James applies a relationship reserved exclusively for Yahweh to Jesus, which would otherwise be idolatry.
verse_bank.jas_2_1: James refers to Jesus as 'the Lord of glory'. In the Old Testament (Psalm 24:8, 10), the title 'King of glory' or 'Lord of glory' is a title exclusively reserved for Yahweh. By applying this unique title to Jesus, James directly equates Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.jas_2_7: James speaks of 'the honorable name by which you were called'. This phrase is an exact Old Testament idiom (e.g., Deut 28:10, Jer 14:9) used exclusively to describe Yahweh's name being placed upon His people Israel. By applying this divine prerogative to the name of Christ being called upon believers, James identifies Jesus in the role of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1tm_1_1: Paul calls Christ Jesus 'our hope.' In the Old Testament, being the ultimate hope of believers is a title and role reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Jeremiah 17:13 calls Yahweh 'the hope of Israel', and Psalm 71:5 says 'For you, O Lord, are my hope').
verse_bank.1tm_1_2: Paul cites Jesus alongside God the Father as the direct source of 'grace, mercy, and peace.' In the Old Testament, bestowing divine grace and peace is a prerogative of Yahweh, as seen in the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26).
verse_bank.1tm_1_12: Paul thanks Jesus for inherently giving him strength. In the Old Testament, empowering and giving strength to His people is an action characteristic of Yahweh alone (e.g., Psalm 29:11, Isaiah 40:29).
verse_bank.1tm_1_15: Paul states that Jesus came to 'save sinners.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh emphatically declares that He is the only Savior (Isaiah 43:11: 'besides me there is no savior'). Jesus coming to bring ultimate salvation identifies Him with this exclusive divine prerogative.
verse_bank.1tm_1_16: Paul speaks of those who 'believe in him [Jesus] for eternal life.' The Old Testament strictly warns against trusting in man (Jeremiah 17:5) and commands trusting only in Yahweh for salvation (Psalm 2:12). Making Jesus the direct object of saving faith demonstrates His divinity.
verse_bank.1tm_2_6: The preceding verse identifies Jesus, and here He gives Himself as a 'ransom for all.' In the Old Testament, Psalm 49:7-8 declares that no mere man can ransom another's soul, but Psalm 49:15 reveals that God Himself will ransom souls. By acting as the ransom for all humanity, Jesus accomplishes what the Old Testament says only God can do.
verse_bank.1tm_5_21: Paul solemnly charges Timothy 'in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus and of the elect angels.' In the Old Testament, calling upon heavenly witnesses is an invocation of Yahweh and His divine council (e.g., Deuteronomy 4:26). Placing Jesus within this triad, distinct from the created angels, points to His uncreated nature and role as an omnipresent, divine judge alongside the Father.
verse_bank.1tm_6_14: The text looks forward to the 'appearing' of the Lord Jesus Christ. In the Old Testament, the ultimate eschatological event is the coming or 'appearing' of Yahweh Himself to bring salvation and judgment (e.g., Malachi 3:1-2, Zechariah 14:5). By making the eschatological hope the appearing of Jesus, Paul equates Christ's return with the promised arrival of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1tm_6_15: The title 'King of kings and Lord of lords' is an exclusive title for Yahweh in the Old Testament (Deuteronomy 10:17). Some grammatical readings of this passage identify Jesus directly with this title in relation to His appearing. Even if the immediate antecedent is understood as the Father, the New Testament applies this exact, exclusive Yahweh-title directly to Jesus elsewhere (Revelation 17:14), demonstrating that He shares the unique identity of God.
verse_bank.1tm_3_13: The verse speaks of 'the faith that is in Christ Jesus.' The Old Testament strictly forbids placing ultimate religious faith in a mere mortal (Jeremiah 17:5) and reserves this absolute trust for Yahweh alone (Jeremiah 17:7). By making Jesus the ultimate object of faith, the text places Him in the position of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1tm_3_16: Christ is described as being 'believed on in the world.' In the Old Testament, being the universal object of saving trust is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 45:22). Furthermore, the phrase 'manifested in the flesh' implies a pre-existent divine nature, as God is the one who 'manifests' or appears, rather than simply being born.
verse_bank.1jn_1_2: Jesus is described as 'the eternal life'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the sole fountain and source of life (e.g., Psalm 36:9), making this a divine title.
verse_bank.1jn_2_2: Jesus is the 'propitiation for our sins... of the whole world'. In the Old Testament, it is explicitly stated that no mere man can redeem a brother or give to God a ransom for a soul (Psalm 49:7-8); this scale of salvation is an action only God can perform.
verse_bank.1jn_2_12: John states that sins are forgiven for Jesus' 'name's sake'. In the Old Testament, sins are forgiven exclusively for Yahweh's 'name's sake' (e.g., Psalm 25:11, Psalm 79:9, Isaiah 43:25).
verse_bank.1jn_2_13: Jesus is described as 'him who is from the beginning'. In the Old Testament, eternity and being 'from the beginning' or 'from everlasting' is an attribute strictly belonging to Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 101:25, Habakkuk 1:12).
verse_bank.1jn_2_23: The verse makes having a relationship with the Father intrinsically and inseparably tied to confessing the Son. In the Old Testament, Yahweh shares His glory with no one (Isaiah 42:8); the Son sharing this equal honor and necessity is a claim to shared divine essence.
verse_bank.1jn_2_27: The verse speaks of the 'anointing' (the Holy Spirit) received from Him (Jesus). In the Old Testament, pouring out the Spirit is an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., Joel 2:28).
verse_bank.1jn_2_28: This verse refers to believers having confidence when Jesus 'appears' at 'his coming' (judgment). In the Old Testament, the one who comes to judge the earth in glory is Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 96:13).
verse_bank.1jn_2_29: Believers are said to be 'born of him', with the preceding context referring to Jesus. Throughout Scripture, believers are only 'born of God' (as explicitly stated later in 1 John 3:9), equating Jesus' life-giving role with God's.
verse_bank.1jn_3_5: Jesus is said to have appeared to 'take away sins'. In the Old Testament, the authority to completely remove and forgive sins is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Micah 7:18, Exodus 34:7). Jesus performing this action demonstrates His inherent divine authority.
verse_bank.1jn_3_8: Jesus appeared to 'destroy the works of the devil.' In the Old Testament, defeating the ultimate spiritual tyrant and rescuing captives is an action attributed to Yahweh Himself (e.g., Isaiah 49:24-25). Jesus autonomously executes this divine victory.
verse_bank.1jn_4_9: The verse states we 'live through him' (Jesus). In the Old Testament, Yahweh alone is the source and fountain of life (e.g., Psalm 36:9, Deuteronomy 30:20). Attributing the source of spiritual and eternal life to Jesus is an attribution of a uniquely divine prerogative.
verse_bank.1jn_4_14: In the Old Testament, Yahweh repeatedly declares that He is the only Savior and there is no savior apart from Him (e.g., Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4). Here, Jesus is given the exclusive divine title 'Savior of the world', directly identifying Him with Yahweh's unique role.
verse_bank.1jn_5_11: The verse states that eternal life is located 'in his Son.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is exclusively described as the fountain and source of life (e.g., Psalm 36:9). For the Son to possess and be the source of eternal life means He shares an attribute reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.1jn_5_13: The author tells believers to put their faith 'in the name of the Son of God' to possess eternal life. In the Old Testament, placing absolute trust in a name for spiritual salvation is an act of devotion reserved strictly for the name of Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 50:10, Joel 2:32).
verse_bank.1jn_5_20: The text explicitly calls Jesus Christ 'the true God and eternal life.' The specific title 'true God' is used exclusively for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., 2 Chronicles 15:3, Isaiah 65:16). Applying this title to Jesus is a direct and unambiguous claim to His identity as Yahweh.
verse_bank.2jn_1_3: Jesus is uniquely called 'the Father's Son' and is presented alongside God the Father as the co-equal source of divine grace, mercy, and peace. In the Old Testament, bestowing these divine blessings is the exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26, Psalm 29:11).
verse_bank.2jn_1_7: The phrase 'coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh' describes the Incarnation. It implies that Jesus pre-existed in a divine state before taking on human nature, as a mere human does not choose to 'come in the flesh' but is simply created.
verse_bank.2jn_1_9: John inextricably links possessing God with possessing the Son, stating that abiding in Christ's teaching means having 'both the Father and the Son'. Placing the Son on the exact same level of necessity and divine possession as God the Father demonstrates their equality of essence.
verse_bank.rom_1_1: In the Old Testament, the title 'servant of the LORD (Yahweh)' was a distinct honor for prophets acting under God's direct authority (e.g., Joshua 1:2, Amos 3:7). By introducing himself as a 'servant of Christ Jesus,' Paul directs an absolute religious devotion exclusively reserved for God toward Jesus.
verse_bank.rom_1_5: God declares in the Old Testament that He acts 'for His name's sake' and will not share His glory with another (e.g., Psalm 23:3, Isaiah 42:8). Paul states his global mission is to bring about obedience 'for the sake of his [Jesus's] name,' ascribing a unique divine prerogative and glory to Christ.
verse_bank.rom_1_7: In the Old Testament, the bestowal of divine grace and peace is a blessing that comes exclusively from Yahweh (e.g., the priestly blessing in Numbers 6:24-26). Paul places Jesus on the exact same level as God the Father as the co-equal source of this divine grace and peace.
verse_bank.rom_2_16: In the Old Testament, searching hearts and judging the secret thoughts of men is an action requiring omniscience, exclusively reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Jeremiah 17:10, 1 Kings 8:39). Paul states that this ultimate divine judgment of secrets will be executed by Christ Jesus. Since no mere creature can know the secrets of all men, Jesus executing this judgment shows He shares in God's exclusive omniscience and authority.
verse_bank.rom_9_5: Paul explicitly identifies Christ as 'God over all, blessed forever,' directly ascribing the title of God to Him.
verse_bank.rom_9_33: Paul applies the title 'stone of stumbling' to Jesus. In Isaiah 8:13-14, this 'stumbling stone' is explicitly identified as the Lord (Yahweh) Himself.
verse_bank.rom_10_13: The preceding verses (Rom 10:9-12) establish Jesus as the 'Lord' to be called upon. Paul then quotes Joel 2:32, which states that whoever calls on the name of Yahweh will be saved, applying this exclusively divine prerogative to Jesus.
verse_bank.rom_7_4: Paul uses a marriage analogy, stating that believers have died to the law to 'belong to another,' namely Christ. In the Old Testament, Yahweh exclusively claims the title of Husband to His people (e.g., Isaiah 54:5, Hosea 2:16). By presenting Christ as the divine Bridegroom to whom believers are united, Paul assigns a strictly Yahweh-exclusive relationship to Jesus.
verse_bank.rom_8_9: Paul interchangeably uses 'Spirit of God' and 'Spirit of Christ.' In the Old Testament, the Spirit that gives life and dwells within believers belongs exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., Ezekiel 36:27). By possessing and giving this same Spirit, Jesus is shown to share the divine identity.
verse_bank.rom_8_10: In the Old Testament, it is exclusively God (Yahweh) who promises to dwell within the hearts of His people (e.g., Zechariah 2:10). Here, Paul states that 'Christ is in you,' showing Jesus fulfilling the divine action of indwelling believers.
verse_bank.rom_8_34: Christ is described as being 'at the right hand of God,' drawing from Psalm 110:1. In the biblical context, sitting at the right hand of Yahweh is not merely a place of honor, but signifies sharing in His sovereign, divine rule over all creation.
verse_bank.rom_8_36: In the preceding verse, Paul speaks of believers facing persecution and the sword to separate them from the 'love of Christ.' He then justifies this by quoting Psalm 44:22, which describes Israelites being killed for Yahweh's sake. By applying a psalm about suffering for Yahweh directly to suffering for Christ, Paul equates Jesus with Yahweh.
verse_bank.rom_8_39: Paul states that nothing 'in all creation' can separate us from God's love which is 'in Christ Jesus.' By explicitly contrasting 'all creation' with Christ, who eternally mediates this love, Paul demonstrates that Christ Himself is uncreated and therefore shares the divine nature.
verse_bank.rom_14_6: Paul states that believers observe holy days and eat or abstain 'in honor of the Lord' (referring to Jesus, as the context in verses 8-9 shows). In the Old Testament, religious devotion and the observance of holy days were dedicated exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., Leviticus 23). Directing this religious devotion to Jesus shows He receives the worship due only to God.
verse_bank.rom_14_8: Paul states that 'whether we live or whether we die, we are the Lord's' (referring to Jesus, as explicitly stated in verse 9). In the Old Testament, the ultimate ownership of all souls belongs exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., Ezekiel 18:4, 'All souls are mine'). Believers belonging entirely to Jesus in life and death demonstrates His divine ownership over humanity.
verse_bank.rom_14_9: Paul declares that Christ is 'Lord both of the dead and of the living.' In the Old Testament, absolute sovereignty over life, death, and the afterlife is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Deuteronomy 32:39, 1 Samuel 2:6). By asserting Jesus is the universal Lord over the dead and the living, Paul identifies Him with God's unique authority.
verse_bank.rom_14_14: Paul states his conviction 'in the Lord Jesus' that no food is unclean in itself. In the Old Testament, the dietary laws were established directly by Yahweh (Leviticus 11). For Jesus to have the inherent authority to abrogate divine dietary laws shows He exercises the authority of the divine Lawgiver Himself.
verse_bank.rom_14_18: Paul states that whoever 'serves Christ' is acceptable to God. In the Old Testament, religiously serving anyone other than Yahweh as Lord is idolatry and brings condemnation (e.g., Exodus 20:3-5). The fact that serving Jesus as Lord is the very thing that makes one acceptable to God proves that Jesus shares the divine identity.
verse_bank.rom_5_8: Paul states that God demonstrates His own love by the death of Christ. If Christ were merely a human prophet, his sacrifice would not inherently demonstrate God's own personal love. By identifying Christ's sacrifice as the ultimate proof of God's love, the text portrays a profound divine unity.
verse_bank.rom_5_9: Jesus is presented as the Savior who eternally saves believers from God's wrath. In the Old Testament, Yahweh emphatically declares 'besides me there is no savior' (Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4). Jesus fulfilling the role of ultimate, eternal Savior exercises an authority reserved strictly for God.
verse_bank.rom_5_17: Believers receive the 'abundance of grace' through Jesus Christ. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the ultimate source who bestows grace and favor (Psalm 84:11). Jesus acting as the sovereign conduit of divine grace is a prerogative of God.
verse_bank.rom_5_21: Eternal life is granted 'through Jesus Christ.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the sole fountain of life who alone says, 'I kill and I make alive' (Deuteronomy 32:39, Psalm 36:9). Dispensing eternal life is an action exclusive to God.
verse_bank.rom_6_23: The free gift of eternal life is found 'in Christ Jesus.' Similar to Romans 5:21, the Old Testament strictly reserves the power over eternal life to Yahweh alone (1 Samuel 2:6), showing Jesus acts in a uniquely divine capacity.
verse_bank.rom_3_22: Paul states that God's righteousness comes through 'faith in Jesus Christ'. The Old Testament explicitly curses trusting in man for salvation and reserves saving faith exclusively for Yahweh (Jeremiah 17:5-7, Psalm 146:3). By making Jesus the necessary object of saving faith, Paul assigns Him a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.rom_3_24: Paul writes that believers are justified through 'the redemption that is in Christ Jesus'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh emphatically declares that He is the exclusive Redeemer and Savior, and there is no other (Isaiah 43:11, 44:6). Attributing the source of humanity's redemption to Jesus identifies Him with the exclusive role of Yahweh.
verse_bank.rom_3_26: The verse states God justifies 'the one who has faith in Jesus'. Similar to verse 22, requiring faith to be placed in Jesus for justification demonstrates His divinity, as the Old Testament strictly forbids trusting in anyone but Yahweh for salvation (Psalm 146:3).
verse_bank.rom_4_24: Paul calls Jesus 'our Lord' (Kyrios). In the Greek Old Testament (Septuagint), Kyrios is the direct translation of the divine name Yahweh, a usage Paul applies to God earlier in this chapter (v. 8). By giving Jesus the sovereign title of 'Lord' in a religious context, He is identified with the Yahweh of the Old Testament.
verse_bank.rom_15_12: Paul quotes Isaiah 11:10, stating that the Gentiles will place their 'hope' in the root of Jesse (Christ). In the Old Testament, placing ultimate spiritual hope in a mere man is explicitly cursed (Jeremiah 17:5, Psalm 146:3), and true hope must be in Yahweh (Jeremiah 17:7). Being the valid object of the nations' hope identifies Christ as divine.
verse_bank.rom_15_19: The preceding verse establishes that Christ is the active agent bringing the Gentiles to obedience, and here Paul states this is done 'by the power of signs and wonders.' In the Old Testament, performing miraculous signs and wonders to deliver a people is an action exclusively attributed to Yahweh (Deuteronomy 4:34). Here, Christ exercises this divine prerogative.
verse_bank.rom_16_16: Paul refers to the congregations as the 'churches of Christ.' In the Old Testament, God's people are exclusively identified as the 'assembly of Yahweh' or 'congregation of the Lord' (Numbers 16:3, Micah 2:5). Claiming ownership of God's assembly is a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.rom_16_18: Paul notes that divisive people 'do not serve our Lord Christ.' In the Old Testament, religious service and devotion are exclusively commanded for Yahweh alone (Deuteronomy 6:13, 'him only shall you serve'). Applying this exclusive religious service to Christ shows He is receiving the devotion due to God.
verse_bank.rom_16_20: Paul prays for the 'grace of our Lord Jesus Christ' to be with the believers. In the Old Testament, bestowing grace and spiritual blessing upon God's people is a priestly blessing reserved to be given by Yahweh (Numbers 6:24-25). Jesus bestows this divine grace by His own authority.
verse_bank.rom_11_26: Paul refers to Jesus as 'The Deliverer,' quoting Isaiah 59:20. In Isaiah, this Redeemer who comes to bring salvation is explicitly Yahweh. By applying this exclusive Old Testament divine title to Jesus, Paul identifies Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.rom_12_11: Paul commands believers to 'serve the Lord' (Christ). In the Old Testament, the command to offer this kind of absolute religious devotion and service was strictly reserved for Yahweh alone (e.g., Deuteronomy 6:13). Directing this service to Jesus places Him in the position of God.
verse_bank.1cor_3_13: The 'Day' that will test works by fire refers to the 'Day of our Lord Jesus Christ' (as established contextually in 1 Cor 1:8). In the Old Testament (e.g., Malachi 4:1, Zephaniah 1:18), the eschatological 'Day of the Lord' characterized by fiery judgment is exclusively the day of Yahweh's judgment. Jesus here assumes the role of Yahweh as the final judge.
verse_bank.1cor_4_5: Paul states that when the Lord (Jesus) comes, He will disclose the purposes of the heart to judge. The preceding verse establishes Jesus as this judge. In the Old Testament, knowing and judging the hidden secrets of the human heart is a prerogative explicitly reserved for Yahweh alone (e.g., 1 Kings 8:39 states God 'alone' knows the heart of all the children of men).
verse_bank.1cor_7_17: Paul uses parallel structure to say 'the Lord' (Jesus) assigns life paths while 'God' calls. In the Old Testament, sovereignly apportioning one's lot or station in life is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 16:5).
verse_bank.1cor_7_23: The preceding verse establishes believers as bondservants of Christ. Here, Paul states they were 'bought with a price.' In the Old Testament, purchasing or redeeming a people from slavery to be His servants is an action reserved strictly for Yahweh (e.g., Exodus 15:16, Psalm 74:2).
verse_bank.1cor_7_35: Paul encourages 'undivided devotion to the Lord' (Christ). In the Old Testament, demanding undivided devotion and singular allegiance is the exclusive right of Yahweh (e.g., Deuteronomy 6:5). Directing this ultimate devotion to Christ places Him in the position of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1cor_8_6: Paul reworks the Jewish Shema (Deut 6:4, 'The LORD our God, the LORD is one'), inserting Jesus into the unique divine identity as the 'one Lord'. Furthermore, he attributes the creation of 'all things' to Jesus, an action Isaiah 44:24 states Yahweh performed completely alone.
verse_bank.1cor_8_12: Paul states that wounding a brother's conscience is a sin against Christ. In the Old Testament, all sin is ultimately an offense committed uniquely against God as the supreme moral lawgiver and judge (e.g., Psalm 51:4). By making Christ the ultimate offended party, Paul equates Him with God.
verse_bank.1cor_10_4: In the Old Testament, Yahweh is identified as the one who provided water from the rock in the wilderness. Paul identifies this pre-existent provider and "Rock" of Israel as Christ.
verse_bank.1cor_10_9: Numbers 21 shows the Israelites testing Yahweh in the wilderness, which resulted in them being bitten by serpents. Paul explicitly states that the one they were putting to the test was Christ, identifying Jesus as the Yahweh of the Exodus.
verse_bank.1cor_10_22: The preceding verse establishes the context of the Eucharist as the table and cup of the Lord Jesus. In Deuteronomy 32:21, Yahweh says He is provoked to jealousy by idolatry. Here, Paul warns that partaking with demons provokes the Lord (Jesus) to jealousy, placing Him in the exact role of Yahweh who demands exclusive devotion.
verse_bank.1cor_5_5: The phrase 'day of the Lord' in the Old Testament exclusively refers to the Day of Yahweh's judgment (e.g., Isaiah 13:6, Joel 2:1). The preceding verse (1 Cor 5:4) explicitly identifies 'the Lord' as Jesus. By applying the day of Yahweh's judgment to Jesus, Paul identifies Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.1cor_6_11: In the Old Testament, justification is an action performed exclusively by Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 45:25 'By the Lord shall they be justified'). Here, believers are washed, sanctified, and justified 'in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ,' identifying Jesus with the divine prerogative to justify and save.
verse_bank.1cor_6_17: The Old Testament commands believers to 'cleave' or 'join' themselves exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., Deuteronomy 10:20). Here, Paul applies this spiritual union to Jesus, stating that whoever is 'joined to the Lord' becomes one spirit with Him.
verse_bank.1cor_1_2: In the Old Testament, 'calling upon the name of the LORD (Yahweh)' is an act of prayer and worship exclusive to God (e.g., Joel 2:32). Here, Paul defines Christians as those who 'call upon the name of our Lord Jesus Christ,' thereby directing divine worship exclusively reserved for Yahweh to Jesus.
verse_bank.1cor_1_8: The Old Testament repeatedly warns of the 'Day of the LORD (Yahweh)' as the final day of divine judgment (e.g., Amos 5:18, Zechariah 14:1). Paul takes this exclusive, apocalyptic event of Yahweh and calls it 'the day of our Lord Jesus Christ,' placing Jesus in the role of the final Divine Judge.
verse_bank.1cor_1_31: Paul quotes Jeremiah 9:24, where God commands that if anyone boasts, they must boast in knowing Yahweh. The preceding verse establishes Christ as the source of our redemption, and Paul applies this Old Testament command about Yahweh to Jesus, meaning we are to boast in Jesus just as Israel boasted in Yahweh.
verse_bank.1cor_2_8: Paul calls Jesus the 'Lord of glory.' In the Old Testament, this specific attribution of glory is a divine title, with Psalm 24:8-10 defining Yahweh as the 'King of glory.' Furthermore, God states in Isaiah 42:8 that He shares His glory with no one. Claiming this title for Jesus is a direct attribution of Yahweh's exclusive glory to Him.
verse_bank.1cor_2_16: Paul quotes Isaiah 40:13, which rhetorically asks who can know or instruct the mind of Yahweh. He then immediately states, 'But we have the mind of Christ.' By substituting Christ into a passage explicitly about Yahweh's unsearchable mind, Paul equates the mind of Christ with the mind of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1cor_15_45: Jesus is described as a 'life-giving spirit.' In the Old Testament, the power to give life (to make alive) is a prerogative explicitly and exclusively reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Deut. 32:39, 1 Sam. 2:6). Attributing this to Jesus means He exercises an exclusive divine power.
verse_bank.1cor_16_22: Paul utters a direct prayer to Jesus ('Our Lord, come!' or 'Maranatha'). In the Old Testament, prayer and supplication are acts of worship exclusively directed to Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 5:2), showing early Christians worshiped Jesus as God.
verse_bank.1cor_16_23: Paul blesses the church with the 'grace of the Lord Jesus.' In the Old Testament, the bestowal of divine grace is an action of God (e.g., Psalm 83:11 in the LXX). Paul identifies Jesus as the source of this divine grace.
verse_bank.1cor_11_24: In Exodus 12:14, the Passover is established as a sacred memorial to remember Yahweh's deliverance. By commanding 'Do this in remembrance of me,' Jesus places Himself at the center of this divine memorial, claiming the reverence and focus exclusively reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.1cor_11_25: In Jeremiah 31:31, it is exclusively Yahweh who declares, 'I will make a new covenant.' Here, Jesus personally institutes this New Covenant by His own blood, fulfilling a promise and performing an action that the Old Testament attributes solely to Yahweh.
verse_bank.1cor_11_32: The 'Lord' in this context refers to Christ. In the Old Testament, being the ultimate Judge and disciplining His people are exclusive prerogatives of Yahweh (Psalm 50:6; Proverbs 3:11-12). Paul attributes these divine actions of judging and disciplining directly to Jesus.
verse_bank.1cor_12_3: The Greek title 'Lord' (Kyrios) used here is the same term used throughout the Septuagint to translate God's unutterable divine name, Yahweh. By stating that the Holy Spirit leads believers to confess 'Jesus is Lord,' Paul identifies Jesus with the Yahweh of the Old Testament.
verse_bank.acts_19_17: In the Old Testament, the magnifying or extolling of a name is an act of reverence reserved for Yahweh alone (e.g., Psalm 34:3). In this verse, supernatural fear falls upon the people and they explicitly extol the name of the Lord Jesus, offering Him the worship and reverence that belongs exclusively to God.
verse_bank.acts_20_21: The Old Testament strictly warns against putting trust in mere mortals (Jeremiah 17:5) and commands that absolute faith be placed in Yahweh. Here, Paul preaches 'faith in our Lord Jesus Christ' as parallel to repentance toward God, treating Jesus as the proper object of saving faith—a position belonging only to God.
verse_bank.acts_20_24: In the Old Testament, true prophets receive their sacred commission and ministry directly from Yahweh (e.g., Jeremiah 1:4-10). Paul explicitly states that he received his ministry directly from the Lord Jesus, showing that Jesus exercises the divine prerogative of Yahweh in commissioning His servants.
verse_bank.acts_20_28: In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the divine shepherd who purchases and redeems His flock (e.g., Psalm 74:2). Here, Paul refers to the believers as the 'church of God' and states that God obtained it 'with his own blood.' Since it was Jesus who shed His blood on the cross, this text identifies Jesus as God purchasing His flock.
verse_bank.acts_5_31: Jesus is given the title 'Savior' and grants 'forgiveness of sins'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly states that He is the only Savior (Isaiah 43:11, 45:21) and the only one who can forgive sins (Isaiah 43:25). By attributing these exclusive divine prerogatives to Jesus, the text identifies Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_5_41: The apostles rejoice that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for 'the name'. In the Old Testament and Jewish tradition, 'the Name' (HaShem) was used absolutely as a reverent substitute for the divine name of Yahweh (e.g., Leviticus 24:11). Applying this absolute title to Jesus demonstrates that He is given the reverence due strictly to God.
verse_bank.acts_23_11: Jesus ('the Lord') appears to Paul in the night to comfort him and commission him as His witness. In the Old Testament, appearing to prophets to give divine commissions and claiming them as witnesses is exclusive to Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 43:10, where Yahweh says 'You are my witnesses'). Jesus acts in the role of Yahweh by making Paul His own witness.
verse_bank.acts_24_24: Paul preaches 'faith in Christ Jesus.' In the Old Testament, placing ultimate spiritual trust and faith in anyone other than God is heavily condemned (e.g., Jeremiah 17:5-7). By making Jesus the direct object of religious faith, the text gives Him a position exclusively reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_26_15: The preceding verses describe Paul encountering a blinding light from heaven, a hallmark of divine Theophanies reserved for God's glory (e.g., Ezekiel 1:28, Psalm 103:2 LXX). In this verse, the Lord speaking from the divine glory identifies Himself as Jesus, claiming the unapproachable light of Yahweh for Himself.
verse_bank.acts_26_16: Jesus directly appoints Paul as His servant and witness from heaven. In the Old Testament, only Yahweh directly commissions prophets from heaven (e.g., Moses, Isaiah, Ezekiel). Jesus performs this divine commissioning by His own inherent authority.
verse_bank.acts_26_18: Jesus states that forgiveness of sins and sanctification come through faith in Him. In the Old Testament, only God can forgive sins, and He alone is the sanctifier (Exodus 31:13). Furthermore, placing faith for salvation in anyone other than Yahweh is idolatry unless Jesus is Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_15_11: Isaiah 43:11 explicitly states that Yahweh is the only Savior. Peter declares here that salvation is achieved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, attributing an exclusive prerogative of God directly to Jesus.
verse_bank.acts_16_7: The preceding verse (v. 6) refers to the guiding 'Holy Spirit', while this verse refers to Him as the 'Spirit of Jesus'. In the Old Testament, the Holy Spirit is strictly the Spirit of God (Yahweh). Jesus possessing the Divine Spirit as His own demonstrates a shared divine nature.
verse_bank.acts_16_18: Old Testament prophets performed miracles and cast out spirits by calling upon the name of Yahweh. Here, Paul commands the demonic spirit to leave using the name of Jesus Christ, demonstrating that Jesus' name carries the same supreme, divine authority as Yahweh's.
verse_bank.acts_16_34: In verse 31, Paul instructs the jailer to 'believe in the Lord Jesus' to be saved. Here in verse 34, the jailer's resulting faith in Jesus is explicitly described as having 'believed in God'. Placing one's faith in someone for salvation is an act of worship reserved exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., Genesis 15:6).
verse_bank.acts_21_13: Paul declares his willingness to die 'for the name of the Lord Jesus'. In the Old Testament, absolute devotion, living, and suffering for 'the name' is directed solely toward Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 44:22). Rendering this ultimate devotion to Jesus' name places Him in the position of God.
verse_bank.acts_22_8: The preceding verses (v. 6-7) establish a blinding heavenly light and a voice speaking to Paul. In the Old Testament, such manifestations of blinding glory are theophanies exclusively reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Ezekiel 1:28). Here, Jesus identifies Himself as the one speaking from this divine glory.
