verse_bank.acts_22_16: Ananias instructs Paul to wash away his sins by calling on Jesus' name. In the Old Testament, forgiving and washing away sins is God's exclusive prerogative. Furthermore, Joel 2:32 establishes that salvation comes specifically by calling on the name of Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_22_20: Paul refers to the martyr Stephen as 'your [Jesus'] witness'. In the Old Testament, God emphatically declares that His prophets and people are exclusively His witnesses (Isaiah 43:10). Claiming the prophets and martyrs as His own personal witnesses is a prerogative of Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_22_21: While Paul is praying in the temple (v. 17), Jesus appears in a vision and commissions him, saying, 'I will send you'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh alone appears to prophets in the temple to commission and send them (e.g., Isaiah 6:8-9). Jesus exercises this sovereign divine authority as His own.
verse_bank.acts_11_17: Salvation and receiving the Holy Spirit are granted upon believing 'in the Lord Jesus Christ.' In the Old Testament, placing salvific faith and belief is exclusively reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Genesis 15:6, Isaiah 28:16).
verse_bank.acts_11_21: The preceding verse establishes that they were 'preaching the Lord Jesus.' Ascribing the 'hand of the Lord' to Jesus applies an Old Testament idiom used exclusively for the active, saving power of Yahweh (e.g., Exodus 9:3, Isaiah 59:1). Furthermore, the people 'turned to the Lord' (Jesus), paralleling the OT command to turn to Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_11_23: Barnabas exhorted the believers to 'remain faithful to the Lord' (Jesus). In the Old Testament, the command to remain steadfast and cleave to the Lord is directed specifically towards Yahweh (e.g., Deuteronomy 10:20).
verse_bank.acts_11_24: The text states that people were 'added to the Lord' (Jesus). This is a direct fulfillment and textual parallel to Old Testament prophecies such as Zechariah 2:11, where it is prophesied that many nations shall be 'joined to Yahweh' in the last days.
verse_bank.acts_9_5: Jesus appears to Paul from heaven in a blinding light (v. 3). In the Old Testament, this kind of glorious, overwhelming manifestation (theophany) is reserved for Yahweh alone (Ezekiel 1:28). The voice speaking from this divine glory identifies itself as Jesus.
verse_bank.acts_9_10: Jesus appears in a vision and calls Ananias by name, who responds 'Here I am, Lord.' This perfectly parallels how Yahweh called prophets in the Old Testament (like Samuel in 1 Samuel 3:4), showing Jesus interacting with believers exactly as Yahweh does.
verse_bank.acts_9_14: Ananias identifies believers as those who 'call on your name' (Jesus' name). In the Old Testament, believers 'call on the name of Yahweh' for salvation and worship (Joel 2:32). Praying directly to Jesus shows the early church worshiped Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_9_20: Paul immediately begins preaching that Jesus is the 'Son of God', a divine title claiming a unique, shared nature and equality with the Father.
verse_bank.acts_9_34: Old Testament prophets performed miracles by praying to Yahweh (1 Kings 17:20). Here, Peter does not pray to God for healing, but declares that Jesus Christ is actively performing the healing from heaven, showing Jesus exercises inherent divine power.
verse_bank.acts_10_36: Peter explicitly calls Jesus 'Lord of all.' In the Old Testament, sovereign lordship over all creation is a title exclusively belonging to Yahweh (Joshua 3:11, Psalm 97:5).
verse_bank.acts_10_42: Jesus is appointed to be the 'judge of the living and the dead.' In the Old Testament, being the ultimate judge of all the earth is a role exclusively belonging to Yahweh (Genesis 18:25).
verse_bank.acts_10_43: The forgiveness of sins is an exclusive prerogative of God (Isaiah 43:25). Peter declares that this divine forgiveness is received directly through the name of Jesus.
verse_bank.acts_7_56: Stephen identifies Jesus as the 'Son of Man' at the right hand of God, directly referencing Daniel 7:13-14. In Daniel, the Son of Man is a divine figure who is given everlasting dominion and is served (worshiped) by all nations.
verse_bank.acts_7_59: Stephen prays directly to Jesus, asking Him to receive his spirit. In the Old Testament (Psalm 31:5), committing one's spirit is an act of trust directed exclusively to Yahweh. Furthermore, offering prayer to anyone other than God is forbidden.
verse_bank.acts_7_60: Stephen kneels and prays directly to Jesus, asking Him not to hold the sin of his murderers against them. Forgiving sins is an authority and prerogative reserved strictly for God.
verse_bank.acts_17_31: Paul states that the world will be judged in righteousness by the appointed man, Jesus. In the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 96:13, Psalm 98:9), the act of 'judging the world in righteousness' is an action explicitly and exclusively reserved for Yahweh. By executing this judgment, Jesus occupies the divine role of the final judge.
verse_bank.acts_18_10: In the preceding verse, the Lord (Jesus) appears to Paul in a vision. Here, Jesus promises His divine presence by saying 'I am with you,' echoing the exact promise Yahweh gave to reassure prophets (e.g., Joshua 1:9, Jeremiah 1:8). Furthermore, Jesus refers to the believers as 'my people,' a phrase exclusively used by Yahweh in the Old Testament to claim ownership over Israel (e.g., Exodus 3:7).
verse_bank.acts_28_5: Paul suffers no harm from a venomous snake, fulfilling the authority Jesus personally granted His disciples (Luke 10:19, Mark 16:18). In the Old Testament, supernatural protection from deadly creatures is a promise exclusively given by Yahweh (Psalm 91:13). Because Jesus grants this protection by His own inherent authority ('I have given you authority'), the fulfillment of this promise in Paul shows Jesus exercising the prerogative of God.
verse_bank.acts_28_8: When Paul heals Publius' father, he must first pray to perform the miracle. This contrasts sharply with Jesus, who consistently heals by His own inherent authority and command without needing to pray (e.g., Mark 1:41, 5:41). Following the methodology, this contrast demonstrates that while the greatest apostles act merely as dependent servants, Jesus possesses the independent, divine authority of God Himself.
verse_bank.acts_28_20: Paul states he is in chains for 'the hope of Israel', which refers to Jesus Christ. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is exclusively given the title 'Hope of Israel' (Jeremiah 14:8, 17:13). By applying this exclusive divine title to Jesus, for whom he is imprisoned, Paul identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_28_28: Paul declares that 'this salvation of God' has been sent to the Gentiles, referring to Jesus and His gospel. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly claims to be the only Savior and the literal salvation of His people (Isaiah 43:11, Psalm 27:1). Identifying Jesus as the very 'salvation of God' aligns Him directly with the exclusive saving role of Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_1_8: In Isaiah 43:10, Yahweh declares 'You are my witnesses.' In this verse, Jesus claims this divine prerogative by telling the apostles 'you will be my witnesses,' placing Himself in the position of Yahweh commissioning His servants.
verse_bank.acts_1_9: Jesus ascends and is taken up by a cloud. In the Old Testament, the cloud (Shekinah) represents the manifest presence and glory of Yahweh (e.g., Exodus 13:21, Psalm 104:3). Being enveloped by the divine cloud is an attribute of deity.
verse_bank.acts_1_11: The angels declare Jesus will return in the same way He left (on the clouds). In the Old Testament, riding the clouds is exclusively an action of Yahweh (Isaiah 19:1, Psalm 104:3) or the divine Son of Man (Daniel 7:13).
verse_bank.acts_1_24: The apostles pray to the 'Lord' (Jesus, who originally chose them in verse 2) calling Him the one 'who knows the hearts of all'. In 1 Kings 8:39, Solomon declares that Yahweh 'alone knows the hearts of all the children of men.' They directly attribute an exclusive divine attribute to Jesus.
verse_bank.acts_2_21: Peter quotes Joel 2:32, which states 'everyone who calls upon the name of Yahweh shall be saved.' Throughout his sermon, Peter applies this directly to Jesus, making it clear that salvation comes by calling on the name of Jesus, effectively identifying Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_2_33: Peter states that Jesus 'has poured out' the Holy Spirit. In the Old Testament, pouring out the Spirit is an action strictly reserved for Yahweh (Joel 2:28: 'I will pour out my Spirit'). Jesus performing this act shows He wields the exact authority of Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_2_34: Peter quotes Psalm 110:1 to show that Jesus is David's 'Lord' who sits at the right hand of God. Sitting at God's right hand denotes sharing in God's unique sovereign rule and divine authority over the cosmos.
verse_bank.acts_2_36: Peter concludes that God made Jesus 'Lord' (Kyrios). This is the exact same Greek word used in the Septuagint for Yahweh. Since Peter just quoted Joel 2:32 referring to Yahweh as 'Lord,' he is applying the divine title to Jesus.
verse_bank.acts_2_38: Peter commands baptism 'in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.' As established in Mark 2:7 and Isaiah 43:25, only God can forgive sins. Peter's instruction shows that Jesus' name carries the inherent divine authority to grant forgiveness.
verse_bank.acts_13_23: Jesus is explicitly given the title 'Savior'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh states absolutely that there is no Savior besides Him (Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4). By giving this exclusive divine title to Jesus, the text identifies Him with the prerogatives of Yahweh.
verse_bank.acts_13_33: By applying Psalm 2:7 to Jesus, He is claimed to be the uniquely begotten 'Son'. To a skeptic: begetting implies sharing the exact same nature as the begetter. Just as a human begets a human, God begetting a Son implies Jesus shares the exact uncreated, divine nature of God the Father.
verse_bank.acts_13_38: Paul proclaims that the forgiveness of sins is granted through Jesus. In the Old Testament, forgiving sins is an exclusive right and action of Yahweh alone (e.g., Isaiah 43:25). Jesus is acting as the source of what only God can provide.
verse_bank.acts_13_39: The preceding verse mentions forgiveness through Jesus, and here believers are 'freed' (justified) by Him. The Old Testament states that it is 'in Yahweh' that people shall be justified (Isaiah 45:25). Here, Jesus is the one performing this purely divine action of justification.
verse_bank.acts_14_3: Jesus ('the Lord') is described as actively working from heaven to grant 'signs and wonders' to be done by the apostles' hands. In the Old Testament, performing great wonders by one's inherent power is an action attributed solely to Yahweh (Psalm 136:4), distinct from prophets who must ask God to perform them.
verse_bank.acts_14_23: The disciples are committed to 'the Lord in whom they had believed' (Jesus). Placing one's ultimate religious faith, total trust, and soul into the hands of a person for salvation is an act of worship reserved for God alone in the Old Testament (e.g., Proverbs 3:5).
verse_bank.acts_3_6: Peter heals the lame man by invoking the name of Jesus. In the Old Testament, prophets performed miracles by calling upon the name of Yahweh. Peter using Jesus' name as the ultimate authority and source of power demonstrates that Jesus possesses divine prerogative.
verse_bank.acts_3_14: Jesus is given the title the 'Holy and Righteous One'. In the Old Testament, being the 'Holy One' is a title uniquely reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 43:15). Applying this exclusive title to Jesus is a claim to His divine nature.
verse_bank.acts_3_15: Jesus is called the 'Author of life'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh alone is the fountain and source of all life (Psalm 35:10 LXX / 36:9 MT). Identifying Jesus as the very author of life equates Him with the Creator God.
verse_bank.acts_4_12: Peter declares that salvation is found in no one else but Jesus. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly states, 'beside me there is no Saviour' (Isaiah 43:11, 45:21). Stating that Jesus is the exclusive source of salvation directly identifies Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.phlm_1_3: Jesus is placed on the exact same level as God the Father as the direct source of divine grace and peace. In the Old Testament, bestowing divine blessing and peace is a prerogative exclusive to Yahweh (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26).
verse_bank.phlm_1_5: Paul commends Philemon's religious faith being directed toward the Lord Jesus. The Old Testament explicitly curses placing spiritual trust for salvation in any mere man, commanding that faith be placed exclusively in Yahweh (Jeremiah 17:5-7, Psalm 146:3).
verse_bank.phlm_1_25: Jesus is directly invoked in prayer as the source of grace for the human spirit. Bestowing divine grace and spiritual blessing is an action reserved strictly for God in the Old Testament.
verse_bank.eph_5_5: The kingdom is described as belonging to 'Christ and God' (or 'Christ, who is God' based on Greek grammar). In the Old Testament, the everlasting kingdom is a prerogative belonging exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 145:13).
verse_bank.eph_5_14: Paul adapts the imagery of Isaiah 60:1-2, where the glory of Yahweh rises and shines upon His people, and instead states that it is Christ who shines upon the sleeper. Christ takes the place of Yahweh as the divine light.
verse_bank.eph_5_21: Believers are instructed to submit out of 'reverence' (Greek 'phobos', or 'fear') for Christ. In the Old Testament, this absolute religious fear and reverence is exclusively commanded for Yahweh.
verse_bank.eph_5_23: Christ is explicitly called the 'Savior' of the church. In Isaiah 43:11, Yahweh emphatically declares that He is God and that besides Him there is no Savior.
verse_bank.eph_5_27: Christ is depicted as presenting the church to Himself as a bridegroom to his bride (established in v. 25). In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 54:5), being the Husband and Bridegroom of God's people is a relational role exclusively reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.eph_6_8: Believers are told they will receive their reward back from 'the Lord' (Christ, as established in verses 5-7). In the Old Testament, rewarding and rendering to each person according to their work is exclusively the action of God (e.g., Psalm 62:12).
verse_bank.eph_6_9: Christ, the Master in heaven, is said to judge with 'no partiality.' In Deuteronomy 10:17, showing no partiality is highlighted as a defining characteristic of Yahweh as the ultimate Judge.
verse_bank.eph_1_2: Paul places Jesus Christ as the co-equal source of divine grace and peace alongside God the Father. In the Old Testament, the giving of such divine grace and peace is an exclusive action of Yahweh (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26).
verse_bank.eph_1_20: The preceding verse establishes God raising Christ, and here Christ is seated at His 'right hand in the heavenly places.' This invokes Psalm 110:1. Sitting on the cosmic throne at God's right hand signifies sharing in the exclusive sovereign rule of Yahweh.
verse_bank.eph_1_21: Christ is described as being exalted 'above every name that is named.' In the Old Testament, it is explicitly declared that Yahweh's name alone is exalted (Psalm 148:13), indicating Christ shares this exclusive supreme divine authority.
verse_bank.eph_1_23: Christ is described as the one who 'fills all in all.' In the Old Testament, omnipresence—filling heaven and earth—is a trait exclusively reserved for Yahweh alone (Jeremiah 23:24).
verse_bank.eph_2_17: Christ came preaching peace to those 'far off' and 'near.' This directly parallels Isaiah 57:19, where Yahweh is the one who declares 'peace, peace, to the far and to the near.' Christ is performing the very action of Yahweh.
verse_bank.eph_4_5: Paul calls Jesus the 'one Lord'. In the Old Testament, the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) declares that Yahweh is the 'one Lord', establishing this as an exclusive title for God. Paul identifies Jesus directly with this unique divine title.
verse_bank.eph_4_8: Paul quotes Psalm 68:18, a verse that originally describes Yahweh ascending on high in triumph and leading captives. By applying this exact divine action to Christ's ascension, Paul identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.eph_4_10: Christ is described as ascending 'that he might fill all things' (omnipresence). In the Old Testament (Jeremiah 23:24), only Yahweh claims this exclusive attribute, declaring 'Do I not fill heaven and earth?'.
verse_bank.gal_5_1: In Leviticus 26:13, Yahweh states He broke the 'yoke of slavery' to free His people. Paul attributes this exact divine action of liberation from the yoke of slavery to Christ.
verse_bank.gal_5_24: The Old Testament repeatedly states that God's people belong exclusively to Yahweh as His possession (Deuteronomy 7:6). Paul claims that believers 'belong to Christ Jesus,' transferring God's exclusive ownership to Jesus.
verse_bank.gal_6_2: The Old Testament uniformly refers to the divine commandments as the 'Law of Yahweh' (Psalm 19:7). By calling it the 'law of Christ', Paul identifies Jesus as the divine Lawgiver, assuming a title belonging only to God.
verse_bank.gal_6_14: Jeremiah 9:23-24 commands that one must boast in absolutely nothing except in knowing Yahweh. Paul parallels this exclusive devotion by stating he will boast in nothing except the cross of the Lord Jesus Christ.
verse_bank.gal_6_18: Bestowing divine grace and blessing the human spirit is an action exclusively reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament (Numbers 6:24-25). Paul invokes Jesus to perform this divine action by granting grace to the spirits of the believers.
verse_bank.gal_1_1: Paul explicitly contrasts his commission with human origins ('not from men nor through man'), grouping Jesus Christ directly with God the Father as the divine source of his apostleship. In the Old Testament, only Yahweh has the authority to commission prophets and apostles (e.g., Jeremiah 1:5, Isaiah 6:8).
verse_bank.gal_1_3: Paul identifies Jesus Christ alongside God the Father as the co-equal source of divine grace and peace, applying the title 'Lord' (the Greek 'Kyrios', used to translate Yahweh in the Septuagint). In the Old Testament, bestowing such spiritual blessing is the exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26).
verse_bank.gal_1_12: Paul asserts his gospel is not of human origin ('not from any man') but came through a 'revelation of Jesus Christ.' In the Old Testament, revealing divine mysteries, laws, and truth to prophets is exclusively the action of God (e.g., Daniel 2:28).
verse_bank.gal_2_16: Paul states that a person is justified through faith in Jesus Christ. In the Old Testament, Yahweh alone is the source of justification and righteousness for His people (e.g., Isaiah 45:25). By making Jesus the direct object of justifying faith, Paul places Him in the divine role of Yahweh.
verse_bank.gal_2_20: Paul declares that 'Christ lives in me,' attributing divine omnipresence and spiritual indwelling to Jesus. In the Old Testament, making a dwelling place within His people is an action exclusively reserved for God (e.g., Leviticus 26:11-12; Ezekiel 37:27).
verse_bank.gal_3_13: In the Old Testament, redeeming God's people from their iniquities and spiritual curses is an action explicitly and exclusively attributed to Yahweh (Psalm 130:8, Hosea 13:14). Christ personally redeeming us from the curse of the law performs a saving action reserved solely for God.
verse_bank.gal_3_22: The Old Testament explicitly curses those who put their primary trust in man for salvation (Jeremiah 17:5) and reserves saving faith exclusively for Yahweh (Isaiah 43:11, Jeremiah 17:7). By making Jesus Christ the direct object of saving faith, Paul attributes to Him a prerogative reserved only for God.
verse_bank.gal_4_6: In the Old Testament, sending the omnipresent Spirit to dwell within the hearts of believers is an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh (Ezekiel 36:27, Joel 2:28). Here, the divine Spirit sent into our hearts is explicitly called the 'Spirit of his Son,' showing that the Son possesses and shares the omnipresent Spirit of Yahweh.
verse_bank.gal_4_19: Paul speaks of Christ being spiritually 'formed in' the Galatian believers. The ability to spiritually indwell and be simultaneously present within believers across the world requires omnipresence, an attribute exclusively reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament (Jeremiah 23:24).
verse_bank.phil_3_20: Jesus is called 'Savior'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly states that this title belongs exclusively to Him, declaring 'besides me there is no savior' (Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4).
verse_bank.phil_3_21: Jesus is said to transform our bodies (resurrection) by the power that enables Him to subject all things to Himself. In the Old Testament, the power over life and resurrection is strictly reserved for God (1 Samuel 2:6). Unlike prophets who had to pray to Yahweh to raise the dead, Jesus performs this global resurrection by His own inherent power.
verse_bank.phil_4_5: Paul states 'The Lord is at hand', referring to Jesus. This uses the exact language of the Old Testament regarding Yahweh's presence and the impending 'Day of the Lord' (e.g., Psalm 145:18 'The Lord is near', Zephaniah 1:7).
verse_bank.phil_1_2: In the Old Testament, blessing and giving peace is a prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26). Paul places Jesus on the same level as the Father as the co-source of divine grace and peace.
verse_bank.phil_1_6: The Old Testament refers to the final day of judgment as the 'Day of Yahweh' (e.g., Amos 5:18). By calling it the 'Day of Jesus Christ', Paul identifies Jesus as Yahweh the ultimate judge.
verse_bank.phil_1_10: Similar to 1:6, the 'Day of Christ' appropriates the Old Testament 'Day of Yahweh' (e.g., Zephaniah 1:14), establishing Jesus as the final judge.
verse_bank.phil_1_19: The Old Testament speaks exclusively of the 'Spirit of God' or 'Spirit of Yahweh' (e.g., Judges 3:10). Calling the Holy Spirit the 'Spirit of Jesus Christ' shows Jesus shares Yahweh's nature and ownership of the Spirit.
verse_bank.phil_2_6: Jesus is described as existing in the 'form of God' and possessing 'equality with God'. This is an explicit claim to his pre-existent, uncreated divine nature.
verse_bank.phil_2_9: God bestows on Jesus the 'name that is above every name'. In the Jewish Old Testament context, the highest, ultimate name is Yahweh, indicating Jesus possesses the divine name and authority.
verse_bank.phil_2_10: This directly quotes Isaiah 45:23, where Yahweh swears that to Him alone every knee shall bow. Paul takes this exclusive act of worship reserved for Yahweh and applies it to Jesus.
verse_bank.phil_2_11: Continuing the quotation from Isaiah 45:23, where Yahweh states every tongue shall swear allegiance to Him. Confessing Jesus as Lord here fulfills that exclusive divine prerogative.
verse_bank.jude_1_4: Jude refers to Jesus as our 'only Master and Lord'. In the Old Testament, the title of singular, exclusive Master and Lord over the believers belongs strictly to God (Yahweh). By calling Jesus the 'only' Master, Jude equates His authority with God's.
verse_bank.jude_1_5: Jude explicitly states that Jesus saved the Israelites out of the land of Egypt and later destroyed the unbelieving in the wilderness. In the Old Testament (e.g., Exodus 20:2), delivering Israel from Egypt is the defining redemptive act exclusively attributed to God (Yahweh). Jude directly identifies Jesus as the Yahweh of the Exodus.
verse_bank.jude_1_15: The preceding verse states 'the Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones', and here it states He comes to execute judgment on all. In the Old Testament (e.g., Zechariah 14:5), it is exclusively God (Yahweh) who comes with His heavenly host to judge the earth. The New Testament uniformly identifies this coming Lord as Jesus Christ (e.g., Matthew 25:31), attributing the final judgment—a divine prerogative—to Him.
verse_bank.1pt_1_11: The Old Testament prophets were inspired by the Spirit of Yahweh (e.g., Zechariah 7:12). Peter identifies this divine Spirit that was in the prophets as the 'Spirit of Christ,' demonstrating that Christ shares the same Spirit and divine prerogative as Yahweh.
verse_bank.1pt_2_3: Peter quotes Psalm 33:8 (LXX), which says 'taste and see that the Lord is good' concerning Yahweh, and directly applies it to Jesus in the following verse ('As you come to him'), identifying Jesus as the Yahweh of the Psalms.
verse_bank.1pt_2_8: Peter quotes Isaiah 8:14, where Yahweh of Armies is described as a 'stone of stumbling and a rock of offense,' and applies this description directly to Jesus, identifying Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.1pt_2_25: In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly claims to be the ultimate Shepherd who seeks out His straying sheep (Ezekiel 34:11, Psalm 22:1 in LXX). Peter applies this uniquely divine role to Jesus as the Shepherd and Overseer of our souls.
verse_bank.1pt_5_4: Jesus is given the title 'chief Shepherd'. In the Old Testament (e.g., Ezekiel 34:11-15 and Psalm 23:1), Yahweh explicitly claims the role of being the ultimate Shepherd of His people. By assigning this supreme title to Jesus, the text identifies Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.1pt_3_15: Peter directly adapts Isaiah 8:12-13. While Isaiah commands the people not to be troubled but to 'sanctify the LORD of hosts' (Yahweh), Peter uses the exact phrasing but replaces Yahweh with Jesus, instructing believers to 'honor Christ the Lord as holy.' This directly equates Jesus with Yahweh.
verse_bank.1pt_3_22: Jesus is described as ruling from heaven with 'angels, authorities, and powers having been subjected to him.' This sovereign, universal dominion over all heavenly and spiritual beings is a prerogative strictly reserved for God alone in the Old Testament.
verse_bank.1pt_4_11: Peter attributes a doxology to Jesus Christ, stating 'To him belong glory and dominion forever and ever.' In the Old Testament, God emphatically declares He will not share His glory with another (Isaiah 42:8), demonstrating that Christ shares in the divine nature to receive such worship.
verse_bank.1pt_4_13: Peter looks forward to the day 'when his [Christ's] glory is revealed.' In the Old Testament, the great eschatological hope is specifically the revelation of Yahweh's glory (e.g., Isaiah 40:5). Peter identifies the final revelation of God's glory with the revelation of Christ.
verse_bank.rv_16_15: The preceding verse (v. 14) establishes the coming final judgment as 'the great day of God the Almighty.' Jesus immediately interjects here, 'Behold, I am coming like a thief!' By applying the arrival of God's great day to His own return, Jesus claims the Old Testament 'Day of Yahweh' (e.g., Zechariah 14:1-5, Joel 2:1) as His own, identifying Himself as the God who comes in judgment.
verse_bank.rv_17_14: The Lamb (Jesus) is explicitly given the title 'Lord of lords'. In the Old Testament (e.g., Deuteronomy 10:17 and Psalm 135:3 in the LXX), the title 'Lord of lords' is exclusively reserved for Yahweh. By claiming this title, the text identifies the Lamb as Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_11_8: Verse 4 states the two witnesses stand before the 'Lord of the earth,' a title explicitly reserved for Yahweh in Zechariah 4:14. Here in verse 8, 'their Lord' is identified as the one who was crucified, directly identifying Jesus as the 'Lord of the earth' from the Old Testament.
verse_bank.rv_11_15: The verse declares that the kingdom belongs to both 'our Lord' (the Father) and 'his Christ,' but uses the singular 'he shall reign forever and ever,' uniting them in a single eternal reign. In the Old Testament (e.g., Exodus 15:18, Psalm 146:10), reigning eternally is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_12_5: The male child (Jesus) is caught up to share the very throne of God. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 6:1, Psalm 103:19), the heavenly throne belongs exclusively to Yahweh, who declares He shares His glory with no one (Isaiah 42:8). Jesus ascending to sit on God's heavenly throne demonstrates His divine equality.
verse_bank.rv_7_9: The great multitude stands in worship and victory before both God's throne and the Lamb. In the Old Testament, standing before the heavenly throne is a posture of worship and reverence reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_7_10: The multitude proclaims, 'Salvation belongs to our God... and to the Lamb.' In the Old Testament, salvation is exclusively the prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 3:8). Ascribing salvation equally to the Lamb gives Him divine status.
verse_bank.rv_7_14: The believers wash their robes and make them white in the blood of the Lamb, indicating ultimate spiritual purification and atonement. No mere prophet's blood has the inherent divine power to universally cleanse sins.
verse_bank.rv_7_17: The Lamb is 'in the midst of the throne', occupying the very seat of God. He acts as the divine shepherd guiding His people to living water, directly fulfilling Isaiah 49:10 and Ezekiel 34 where Yahweh Himself is the shepherd.
verse_bank.rv_8_1: The Lamb opens the seventh seal of the divine scroll. Sovereignty over the decrees of history belongs exclusively to God, yet the Lamb uniquely possesses the inherent authority to open the seals and execute God's plan.
verse_bank.rv_19_7: The verse speaks of the 'marriage of the Lamb,' where Jesus is the bridegroom of His people. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 54:5, Hosea 2), Yahweh exclusively holds the title and role of the husband to His people. By taking this role, Jesus claims a position reserved only for Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_19_11: Jesus is described as the one who 'in righteousness he judges and makes war.' In the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 9:8, Psalm 96:13), judging the world in righteousness is an action explicitly and exclusively attributed to Yahweh. Jesus acts with this divine prerogative.
verse_bank.rv_19_15: Jesus is shown to 'tread the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God.' In Isaiah 63:3, Yahweh explicitly declares that He treads the winepress of His wrath 'alone' and that no man is with Him. Since Yahweh performs this action alone, Jesus doing so portrays Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_19_16: Jesus bears the title 'Lord of lords.' In the Old Testament, specifically Deuteronomy 10:17 and Psalm 136:3, this title is uniquely and explicitly applied to Yahweh to demonstrate His supreme sovereignty over all powers. Jesus claiming this exact title is a direct claim to deity.
verse_bank.rv_20_6: The saints are described as being 'priests of God and of Christ.' In the Old Testament, priests exclusively serve and are consecrated to Yahweh alone; serving anyone else is idolatry. By being the co-recipient of priestly service, Christ shares in the exclusive worship due to Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_21_9: The angel shows John the 'Bride, the wife of the Lamb.' In the Old Testament (e.g., Hosea 2:16), Yahweh is exclusively portrayed as the husband of His people. The Lamb assumes the position of the divine husband.
verse_bank.rv_21_22: The Lamb shares the status of the temple equally with the Lord God Almighty. The temple is the unique dwelling place of Yahweh, indicating the Lamb shares His divine presence.
verse_bank.rv_21_23: In Isaiah 60:19, Yahweh declares He will be the everlasting light of His people. Here, the Lamb acts as the lamp of the city, sharing the divine prerogative of illuminating the New Jerusalem alongside God's glory.
verse_bank.rv_21_27: The book of life is described as the 'Lamb's book of life'. In Exodus 32:32-33, the book of the living is explicitly described as belonging exclusively to the Lord ('my book'). The Lamb claims ownership of God's book.
verse_bank.rv_22_1: The river flows from the single 'throne of God and of the Lamb'. The Lamb shares the supreme, singular throne of God, whereas in the Old Testament, Yahweh's throne is exclusively His own.
verse_bank.rv_22_3: The throne is shared by God and the Lamb, and the following pronoun 'his servants will worship him' is singular, indicating absolute unity. Furthermore, they receive worship, which is exclusively reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_22_12: Jesus states He is coming soon bringing His recompense to repay each one. In Isaiah 40:10, it is the Lord God who comes with His reward to repay. Jesus assumes this divine action.
verse_bank.rv_22_13: Jesus (identified as the speaker in v. 16) claims the title 'the first and the last'. In Isaiah 44:6 and 48:12, Yahweh explicitly claims 'I am the first, and I endure for ever' as exclusively His own title.
verse_bank.rv_22_16: Revelation 22:6 states that 'the Lord, the God of the spirits of the prophets, has sent his angel'. In this verse, Jesus says 'I, Jesus, have sent my angel'. Jesus exercises the exact authority and action attributed to the Lord God.
verse_bank.rv_22_20: John prays directly to Jesus saying 'Come, Lord Jesus!' Praying to someone and calling upon their name is an act of worship reserved for God in the Old Testament (e.g., Joel 2:32).
verse_bank.rv_3_5: In Exodus 32:33, Yahweh explicitly states that He is the one who blots people out of His book. Jesus claims this exact, exclusive authority to determine whose name remains in or is removed from the book of life.
verse_bank.rv_3_7: Jesus assumes the title 'the holy one'. In the Old Testament, 'The Holy One' is a supreme title reserved exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., Hosea 11:9, Isaiah 43:15).
verse_bank.rv_3_9: Jesus says He will make enemies bow down at the feet of the church and know that He loved them. This mirrors Isaiah 60:14 and Isaiah 43:4, where Yahweh is the one who makes oppressors bow down and declares His exclusive love for His people. Jesus is taking the direct role of Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_3_19: Proverbs 3:12 says, 'For the LORD [Yahweh] reproves him whom he loves.' Jesus adopts this exact action and applies it to Himself, exercising the loving, providential discipline that the Old Testament attributes to Yahweh alone.
verse_bank.rv_3_21: Jesus declares that He sits with the Father on His throne. In the Old Testament, God states He will not share His sovereign glory with another (Isaiah 42:8). Sitting on the supreme throne of the universe indicates absolute divine equality.
verse_bank.rv_5_6: The Lamb is described as having seven eyes sent out into all the earth. In the Old Testament (Zechariah 4:10), these seven eyes that range through the whole earth are exclusively identified as the eyes of Yahweh. By possessing these eyes, the Lamb shares Yahweh's omniscience.
verse_bank.rv_5_8: The Lamb receives worship from the elders who offer golden bowls of incense, representing the prayers of the saints. In the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 141:2), prayers are offered as incense exclusively to Yahweh, and worship is reserved solely for God (Exodus 34:14). Receiving the prayers of the saints means the Lamb is functioning as Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_5_13: Every created thing in the universe gives the exact same blessing, honor, glory, and might to the Lamb as they do to 'him who sits on the throne' (the Father). In the Old Testament (Isaiah 42:8), Yahweh explicitly states He will not share His glory with another. The Lamb receiving equal eternal glory from all creation indicates He is Yahweh.
verse_bank.rv_6_8: The preceding verses establish that the Lamb is opening the seals. The opening of the fourth seal releases four specific judgments: sword, famine, pestilence, and wild beasts. In the Old Testament (Ezekiel 14:21), Yahweh explicitly identifies these exact four as 'my four sore judgments' which He alone sends. By initiating these specific judgments, the Lamb exercises Yahweh's sovereign authority.
verse_bank.rv_6_16: The kings of the earth hide in caves and call to the mountains to hide them from the 'wrath of the Lamb.' In the Old Testament (Isaiah 2:10, 19), hiding in the rocks and caves from impending judgment is exclusively 'for fear of the Lord (Yahweh).' By making the Lamb the co-source of this terrifying cosmic wrath, the text equates the Lamb's judgment with Yahweh's.
verse_bank.rv_13_8: The 'book of life' is identified as belonging to the Lamb. In the Old Testament (e.g., Exodus 32:32-33, Psalm 69:28), the book of the living belongs exclusively to Yahweh, who writes names in it or blots them out. The Lamb possessing the book of life shows He exercises this divine prerogative.
verse_bank.rv_14_1: The 144,000 have the name of the Lamb and His Father written on their foreheads. Placing a mark of ownership on the foreheads of His servants is an action of Yahweh in the Old Testament (Ezekiel 9:4). The Lamb's name is placed equivalently alongside the Father's as the seal of divine ownership, showing they share the same divine authority.
verse_bank.rv_14_4: The redeemed are described as 'firstfruits for God and the Lamb.' In the Old Testament, firstfruits are an offering reserved exclusively for Yahweh (Exodus 23:19). The Lamb receiving firstfruits alongside God demonstrates His equality and divine status as a recipient of worship.
verse_bank.rv_14_14: Jesus is described as 'one like a son of man' seated on a white cloud. Riding on the clouds is an exclusively divine prerogative in the Old Testament, belonging only to Yahweh (Psalm 104:3, Isaiah 19:1). This also fulfills Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man is a divine figure given everlasting dominion.
verse_bank.rv_14_16: The preceding verse 14 establishes Jesus as the Son of Man with a sickle. Here He swings His sickle to reap the earth. In the Old Testament, the eschatological harvesting and judgment of the earth is an action performed by Yahweh (Joel 3:12-13). Jesus executing this final judgment demonstrates He has Yahweh's authority.
verse_bank.rv_1_7: Jesus is described as 'coming with the clouds' (Daniel 7:13) and as the one they 'pierced', referencing Zechariah 12:10 where Yahweh says 'they shall look upon me... and they shall make lamentation'.
verse_bank.rv_1_13: Jesus is described as 'one like a son of man', referencing the divine figure in Daniel 7:13-14 who is given everlasting dominion, glory, and kingship alongside the Ancient of Days.
verse_bank.rv_1_14: Jesus is described with 'hairs of his head... white, like white wool', which directly parallels the physical description of the Ancient of Days (God) in Daniel 7:9.
verse_bank.rv_1_15: Jesus' voice is described 'like the roar of many waters', which directly parallels the description of the voice of the God of Israel in Ezekiel 43:2.
verse_bank.rv_1_17: Jesus claims the title 'the first and the last'. In Isaiah 44:6, Yahweh explicitly states 'I am the first, and I am hereafter: beside me there is no God', reserving this title exclusively for Himself.
verse_bank.rv_1_18: Jesus claims to hold 'the keys of Death and Hades', asserting ultimate authority over life and death. In 1 Samuel 2:6, this authority belongs exclusively to Yahweh: 'The Lord kills and makes alive; he brings down to the grave, and brings up.'
verse_bank.rv_2_7: Jesus claims the authority to 'grant to eat of the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God'. In Genesis 3:22-24, it is God who planted the garden and exclusively controls access to the tree of life.
verse_bank.rv_2_8: Jesus again claims the title 'the first and the last', directly equating Himself with Yahweh's exclusive claim from Isaiah 44:6.
verse_bank.rv_2_23: Jesus says 'I am he who searches mind and heart, and I will give to each of you according to your works.' This is an exact parallel to Jeremiah 17:10 where Yahweh alone says 'I the Lord try the hearts... to give to every one according to his ways'.
verse_bank.3jn_1_7: The believers went out 'for the sake of the name' (referring to Jesus). In the Old Testament, 'The Name' (HaShem) is used as an absolute, standalone title reserved exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., Leviticus 24:11), and acting 'for His name's sake' is a motif reserved for God alone (e.g., Psalm 23:3). By applying 'the Name' absolutely to Jesus, the author gives Him a uniquely divine title.
verse_bank.2cor_13_3: Paul speaks of 'Christ speaking in me.' In the Old Testament, true prophets only spoke by the indwelling voice and authority of Yahweh (e.g., 2 Samuel 23:2; Deuteronomy 18:18). By identifying Christ as the divine voice speaking through him as an apostle, Paul attributes to Jesus the role exclusively held by God in inspiring His messengers.
verse_bank.2cor_13_5: Paul tells the Corinthians that 'Jesus Christ is in you.' In the Old Testament, dwelling universally among and within His people is an action exclusive to Yahweh (e.g., Leviticus 26:11-12; Zechariah 2:10). Furthermore, for Christ to dwell within all believers simultaneously requires omnipresence, an attribute belonging only to God.
verse_bank.2cor_13_10: Paul asserts that his apostolic authority was given to him by 'the Lord' (Jesus) for 'building up and not for tearing down.' This echoes Jeremiah 1:10, where Yahweh gives the prophet authority to 'tear down' and 'build.' Jesus is shown exercising the divine prerogative of commissioning and authorizing His prophets.
verse_bank.2cor_13_14: In this trinitarian benediction, Paul invokes the 'grace of the Lord Jesus Christ' alongside the love of God. In the Old Testament, bestowing divine grace and blessing upon the people is an action strictly reserved for Yahweh (as seen in the Aaronic Blessing, Numbers 6:24-26). By placing Jesus as a co-equal source of divine grace, He is equated with Yahweh.
verse_bank.2cor_5_8: The Old Testament believer's ultimate hope was to dwell eternally in the presence of Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 23:6). Paul describes the heavenly afterlife simply as being 'at home with the Lord' (Christ), making Jesus the divine presence that fulfills the believer's eternal rest.
verse_bank.2cor_5_10: In the Old Testament, judging humanity is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh, who sits on the divine throne of judgment (e.g., Psalm 9:7-8, Psalm 50:6). Paul states that all must stand before the 'judgment seat of Christ,' directly placing Jesus in the seat and role reserved exclusively for God. (Paul even calls this same seat the 'judgment seat of God' in Romans 14:10).
verse_bank.2cor_11_2: In the Old Testament, the covenant between Yahweh and His people is explicitly described as a marriage, with Yahweh as the exclusive husband (e.g., Isaiah 54:5, Hosea 2:16). By stating that the church is betrothed to 'one husband'—Christ—Paul assigns a covenantal role and title to Jesus that is strictly reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.2cor_12_9: The preceding verse establishes that Paul prayed directly to 'the Lord' for deliverance from his affliction, which is an act of worship and reliance reserved for God (e.g., Psalm 50:15). Here, the Lord answers by dispensing His own divine grace and power, and Paul explicitly identifies this Lord as Christ ('the power of Christ'). In the Old Testament, answering prayer and giving strength to the weak is the exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 40:29).
verse_bank.2cor_3_3: In Jeremiah 31:33, Yahweh promises the New Covenant by saying, 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts.' In this verse, Paul describes the believers as a letter 'from Christ' written 'on tablets of human hearts'. Jesus is the one performing the exclusive divine action that Yahweh promised He Himself would fulfill.
verse_bank.2cor_3_16: The preceding verse 14 establishes that the spiritual veil is taken away only through Christ. In verse 16, Paul directly quotes the scenario of Exodus 34:34, where Moses removes his veil when he turns to Yahweh. By stating the veil is removed when one turns to 'the Lord', Paul places Jesus in the exact role of Yahweh from the Old Testament narrative.
verse_bank.2cor_3_18: In Exodus 34, Moses is physically transformed (his face shines) simply by being in the presence of Yahweh's glory. Here, Paul states that believers are transformed by beholding 'the glory of the Lord' (Christ). Jesus' glory possesses the identical divine, transforming power as Yahweh's glory.
verse_bank.2cor_4_4: In Isaiah 42:8, Yahweh explicitly declares, 'my glory I give to no other.' However, this verse specifically highlights 'the glory of Christ.' Because God absolutely refuses to share His glory with any created being, Christ inherently possessing this divine glory signifies He is one with Yahweh.
verse_bank.2cor_4_6: In Exodus 33:20, Yahweh warns Moses that no human can see His face and live, as His full glory is unapproachable. This verse boldly asserts that the very 'glory of God' is perfectly revealed and safely seen 'in the face of Jesus Christ', identifying Jesus as the exact physical manifestation of Yahweh's divine presence.
verse_bank.2cor_10_8: In the Old Testament (e.g., Jeremiah 1:10, 24:6), it is exclusively Yahweh who commissions His prophets and delegates the authority to 'build up' and 'tear down' His people. Here, Paul asserts that his apostolic authority for this exact purpose was given by 'the Lord' (Jesus Christ). By commissioning Paul with this specific authority, Jesus is performing a divine action reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.2cor_10_17: Paul directly quotes Jeremiah 9:24, an Old Testament command strictly requiring people to boast only in Yahweh. He applies this to 'the Lord', which the surrounding context (verses 7, 8, and 14) identifies as Jesus Christ. By taking a verse about the exclusive honor and devotion due to Yahweh and applying it to Jesus, Paul demonstrates that Christ shares the divine identity.
verse_bank.2cor_1_2: In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the sole source of divine grace and peace (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26). By declaring that grace and peace come equally from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, Paul places Jesus on the same level as the Father as the co-author and dispenser of divine blessings reserved for God.
verse_bank.2cor_1_14: The Old Testament repeatedly refers to the eschatological time of judgment and vindication exclusively as the 'Day of the Lord' (meaning Yahweh) (e.g., Isaiah 13:9, Joel 2:1). Paul takes this exclusive event and title reserved for Yahweh's judgment and applies it directly to Jesus, calling it 'the day of our Lord Jesus.'
verse_bank.2cor_8_9: Jesus is described as having been 'rich' prior to His incarnation (becoming 'poor'). This asserts His pre-existent state of divine glory and ownership of all creation, attributes belonging exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., Haggai 2:8, Psalm 50:10).
verse_bank.2cor_8_19: Paul states the ministry is carried out 'for the glory of the Lord himself' (referring to Jesus). In the Old Testament, doing things for divine glory is exclusively reserved for Yahweh, who declares He will not share His glory with another (Isaiah 42:8).
verse_bank.2cor_8_21: Paul directly quotes Proverbs 3:4 (LXX), an instruction about living honestly in the sight of Yahweh, and applies 'the Lord' to Jesus. Applying an Old Testament text about how one must stand before Yahweh directly to Jesus identifies Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.2cor_8_23: Paul refers to the messengers as 'the glory of Christ.' Attributing divine glory to Christ demonstrates He is Yahweh, since Yahweh emphatically states He will not give His glory to another (Isaiah 42:8, 48:11).
verse_bank.mk_9_4: Moses and Elijah appear on a mountain communing with Jesus. In the Old Testament, Moses (Exodus 24) and Elijah (1 Kings 19) were the only two who climbed a mountain to commune directly with God (Yahweh).
verse_bank.mk_9_7: A cloud overshadows the mountain. In the Old Testament, the cloud (Shekinah glory) represents the direct, physical presence of Yahweh (Exodus 40:34). God speaks from it, directly sharing His glory with Jesus.
verse_bank.mk_9_25: Jesus casts out an unclean spirit by His own direct authority ('I command you'). Old Testament prophets had to cry out to Yahweh for miracles, but Jesus exercises inherent divine power over the spiritual realm.
verse_bank.mk_9_39: Jesus validates exorcisms and miracles being performed 'in my name'. In the Old Testament, acts of divine power and blessing were performed strictly in the name of Yahweh. Jesus places His own name on that same divine level.
verse_bank.mk_10_18: Jesus says, 'No one is good except God alone.' He is not denying His own goodness, but challenging the man's logic: if you recognize me as inherently good, you must recognize me as God.
verse_bank.mk_10_45: Jesus claims the title 'Son of Man' (the divine, eternal ruler from Daniel 7) and states He will give His life as a 'ransom'. In the Old Testament (Psalm 49:7-8), only God can provide the ransom to redeem a soul.
verse_bank.mk_7_19: By declaring all foods clean, Jesus overrides the dietary laws given by Yahweh to Moses in the Old Testament. Only the divine Lawgiver has the inherent authority to abrogate or modify God's established law.
verse_bank.mk_7_37: Jesus heals the deaf and mute by His own authority. In the Old Testament, making the deaf hear and the mute speak is an action and eschatological sign exclusively attributed to Yahweh coming to save His people (Exodus 4:11; Isaiah 35:4-6).
verse_bank.mk_8_8: Jesus miraculously provides food to satisfy the multitudes in the desolate wilderness. This directly parallels Yahweh supernaturally providing manna in the wilderness for His people so they could be full (Exodus 16).
verse_bank.mk_8_38: Jesus uses the divine title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7:13-14 (a heavenly figure given eternal dominion) and claims He will return in divine glory with holy angels to act as the final judge of humanity—a role strictly reserved for God.
verse_bank.mk_11_3: Jesus refers to Himself simply as 'The Lord' to commandeer a donkey. This aligns with Zechariah 9:9, where the divine King comes to Jerusalem riding on a donkey, showing Jesus claiming this sovereign title and fulfilling the prophecy by His own authority.
verse_bank.mk_11_15: Jesus violently cleanses the temple by His own authority. In the Old Testament, Malachi 3:1 prophesies that the Lord (Yahweh) will suddenly come to His temple to purify it. Jesus is acting out the prerogative of Yahweh arriving at His own house.
verse_bank.mk_12_6: In the Parable of the Tenants, Jesus explicitly distinguishes Himself from all previous Old Testament prophets. He labels the prophets as mere 'servants', but identifies Himself as the one 'beloved son' and heir, claiming a unique, divine nature above any human prophet.
verse_bank.mk_12_36: Jesus quotes Psalm 110:1, applying it to Himself. He shows that the Messiah is invited to sit at the 'right hand' of God—an Old Testament idiom for sharing in God's eternal throne, divine authority, and rule.
verse_bank.mk_12_37: Jesus points out that King David, speaking by the Holy Spirit, calls the Messiah 'Lord'. If the Messiah were merely a human descendant (a prophet), David would never call his own descendant 'Lord'. This proves the Messiah's pre-existence and divine superiority.
verse_bank.mk_15_33: The darkness over the land starting at the sixth hour (noon) mirrors Amos 8:9, where Yahweh explicitly declares that He will make the sun go down at noon and darken the earth on the Day of the Lord. The Gospel directly identifies the crucifixion of Jesus with this exclusive cosmic action of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mk_16_19: Jesus is taken to heaven and sits at the right hand of God, directly fulfilling Psalm 110:1. Sharing God's throne and actively exercising His universal dominion is not a position granted to a mere prophet, but is a prerogative of divine authority equal with Yahweh.
verse_bank.mk_16_20: The text states that as the disciples preached everywhere, 'the Lord' (Jesus) worked with them and confirmed their message with signs. This demonstrates divine omnipresence (acting everywhere simultaneously from heaven) and performs the action of Yahweh seen in Isaiah 44:26, where God alone confirms the word of His messengers.
verse_bank.mk_5_6: The Gerasene man runs and falls on his knees to worship Jesus. In the biblical tradition, accepting religious worship without rebuking the worshipper is an exclusive prerogative of God.
verse_bank.mk_5_19: Jesus instructs the healed man to tell his friends how much 'the Lord' (a title for Yahweh) has done for him. In verse 20, the man goes and proclaims what Jesus did, drawing a direct parallel between Jesus and Yahweh.
verse_bank.mk_5_41: Unlike Old Testament prophets (like Elijah or Elisha) who had to pray and cry out to Yahweh to perform resurrections, Jesus raises Jairus's daughter simply by His own inherent spoken command.
verse_bank.mk_6_41: Jesus miraculously multiplies food to feed the multitudes in a desolate wilderness, acting in the exact role of Yahweh providing manna to the Israelites in the desert.
verse_bank.mk_6_48: Jesus walks on the water. This is a direct parallel to Job 9:8, which states that God alone stretches out the heavens and walks on the waves of the sea.
verse_bank.mk_6_50: Jesus speaks to the terrified disciples saying 'It is I' (in Greek, 'ego eimi', which literally translates to 'I AM'), intentionally invoking the divine name of Yahweh revealed in Exodus 3:14.
verse_bank.mk_1_1: Mark introduces Jesus as the 'Son of God', establishing His divine nature from the very first verse, drawing upon Old Testament royal and divine sonship imagery (e.g., Psalm 2:7).
verse_bank.mk_1_2: Quotes Malachi 3:1 where Yahweh says a messenger will prepare the way 'before Me'. Mark applies this to Jesus, substituting 'ahead of you', showing Jesus shares Yahweh's identity.
verse_bank.mk_1_3: Quotes Isaiah 40:3, which commands preparing the way for Yahweh. By identifying John the Baptist as the voice preparing the way for Jesus, Mark explicitly identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.mk_1_24: Demons identify Jesus as 'the Holy One of God'. In the Old Testament, 'The Holy One' is a frequent title for Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah 43:15), indicating absolute divine purity.
verse_bank.mk_1_41: Jesus heals a leper by His own sovereign will and command ('I will; be clean'). Unlike Old Testament prophets who had to pray to Yahweh for healing miracles, Jesus heals with inherent divine authority.
verse_bank.mk_2_5: Jesus forgives the sins of the paralytic. In the Old Testament, transgressions are ultimately against God, and forgiving them is an exclusively divine prerogative.
verse_bank.mk_2_7: The scribes correctly state 'Who can forgive sins but God alone?'. Jesus does not correct their theology; by proceeding to heal the man to prove His authority, He embraces the implication that He is God.
verse_bank.mk_2_8: Jesus perceives the unspoken thoughts of the scribes' hearts. In the Old Testament (e.g., 1 Kings 8:39, Jeremiah 17:10), knowing the secret thoughts of men's hearts is an attribute belonging exclusively to Yahweh.
verse_bank.mk_2_10: Jesus uses the title 'Son of Man' (referencing the divine figure of Daniel 7:13-14 who rides the clouds and receives eternal worship) to assert His inherent authority to forgive sins on earth.
verse_bank.mk_2_28: Jesus declares Himself 'Lord even of the Sabbath.' Since Yahweh exclusively instituted the Sabbath law in Genesis 2 and Exodus 20, claiming to be its Lord is a direct claim to be the divine Lawgiver Himself.
verse_bank.mk_13_26: In the Old Testament, 'riding the clouds' is imagery strictly reserved for Yahweh to demonstrate His supremacy over creation (Psalm 104:3, Isaiah 19:1). Jesus applies this exclusive divine action to Himself, referencing the divine figure in Daniel 7:13 who receives eternal dominion and worship.
verse_bank.mk_13_27: Jesus refers to the angels as 'his' angels and the believers as 'his' elect. In the Old Testament (Deuteronomy 30:3-4, Isaiah 43:5-6), it is exclusively Yahweh who commands the hosts of heaven and gathers His scattered chosen people from the ends of the earth.
verse_bank.mk_13_31: Isaiah 40:8 explicitly states that while creation fades, 'the word of our God will stand forever.' Jesus directly claims this eternal, divine attribute for His own words, equating the permanence and authority of His speech with that of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mk_14_24: Covenants in the Old Testament are exclusively initiated by Yahweh (Exodus 24:8, Jeremiah 31:31). Jesus institutes the New Covenant not merely as a prophetic messenger, but upon His own blood, acting in the direct role and authority of Yahweh initiating a covenant with His people.
verse_bank.mk_14_62: Jesus claims He will sit at God's right hand (Psalm 110:1) and come with the clouds of heaven (Daniel 7:13). Because riding clouds is a distinct identifier of Yahweh in the Old Testament, the Jewish High Priest instantly recognized this as a direct claim to deity, immediately declaring it blasphemy and demanding the death penalty.
verse_bank.mk_3_11: Unclean spirits fall down before Jesus and declare Him the 'Son of God', recognizing His divine nature and supreme authority over the spiritual realm.
verse_bank.mk_3_27: Jesus speaks of binding the strong man (Satan) by His own power. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 49:24-25), it is exclusively Yahweh who rescues captives from the mighty and defeats the ultimate tyrant.
verse_bank.mk_4_38: Jesus is depicted sleeping during a raging storm that He will soon calm. This narrative closely parallels Psalm 107:28-29, which explicitly states that Yahweh is the one who calms the storm to a whisper and rescues those at sea.
verse_bank.mk_4_39: Jesus rebukes the wind and sea by His own spoken command, resulting in a great calm. In the Old Testament, rebuking the sea (e.g., Psalm 106:9, Nahum 1:4) is an action strictly reserved for Yahweh's authority over creation.
verse_bank.mk_4_41: The disciples react with great fear, asking 'Who can this be, that even the wind and the sea obey Him!' This rhetorical question highlights for the reader that Jesus is exercising the exclusive prerogative of God over the forces of nature.
verse_bank.lk_21_15: Jesus promises to personally impart wisdom and speech to His followers. In the Old Testament, granting speech and wisdom is an action strictly reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Exodus 4:11-12, Proverbs 2:6). Jesus is claiming a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.lk_21_27: Jesus claims the title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7:13-14, a divine figure who receives eternal dominion and is worshipped. Furthermore, He claims to ride the clouds, which is imagery reserved exclusively for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 104:3, Isaiah 19:1).
verse_bank.lk_21_33: Jesus elevates His own words to an eternal status that will outlast creation itself. In the Old Testament, this eternality is attributed exclusively to the word of Yahweh (Isaiah 40:8).
verse_bank.lk_22_20: Jesus institutes the New Covenant in His own blood. In the Old Testament, it is strictly Yahweh who establishes covenants with His people (Jeremiah 31:31). By initiating a new covenant based on Himself, Jesus exercises a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.lk_22_29: Jesus claims possession of His own kingdom and the authority to grant others the right to judge the twelve tribes of Israel, acting as the supreme divine King over God's people.
verse_bank.lk_22_51: Jesus heals the servant's severed ear by His own inherent authority and touch. This contrasts with Old Testament prophets who had to explicitly pray and cry out to Yahweh for such miraculous healing power.
verse_bank.lk_22_69: Jesus claims He will be seated at the right hand of God, directly applying the prophecy of Psalm 110:1 to Himself. This signifies sharing in God's sovereign rule and authority over the cosmos.
verse_bank.lk_22_70: Jesus affirms the title 'Son of God' in a unique, ontological sense. The religious leaders immediately recognize this as a blasphemous claim to divine equality, which becomes the basis for His condemnation.
verse_bank.lk_19_10: Jesus claims the divine title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7, a figure given everlasting dominion. Furthermore, He states His mission is 'to seek and to save the lost.' This directly fulfills Ezekiel 34:11-16, where Yahweh rebukes human shepherds and declares that He Himself—exclusively—will search for and save His lost sheep.
verse_bank.lk_19_40: When asked to rebuke His followers for praising Him, Jesus refuses, saying the stones would cry out. In the Old Testament, inanimate creation crying out in praise is a response reserved exclusively for the presence and glory of Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 97:7-9 in LXX). Jesus accepts this divine worship as His absolute due.
verse_bank.lk_19_44: Jesus pronounces judgment on Jerusalem because they did not recognize the time of their 'visitation.' In the Old Testament, a 'visitation' (Hebrew 'paqad') that brings ultimate salvation or judgment is an action strictly performed by God coming to His people (e.g., Jeremiah 10:15). By equating His arrival with this event, Jesus claims to be God visiting Israel.
verse_bank.lk_19_46: Jesus forcefully clears the Temple by His own authority. In doing so, He fulfills the prophecy of Malachi 3:1, which states that the Lord (Yahweh) Himself will suddenly come to His temple to purify it. Jesus exercises the exclusive prerogative of the Temple's true owner.
verse_bank.lk_20_18: Jesus describes Himself as the stone that will crush those who reject Him. This imagery directly parallels Isaiah 8:13-14, where it is explicitly the 'LORD of hosts' (Yahweh) who is called a 'stone of offense and a rock of stumbling.' Jesus takes an exclusive description of Yahweh and applies it to Himself.
verse_bank.lk_20_44: Jesus references Psalm 109:1 (LXX) to demonstrate that the Messiah is not merely David's human descendant, but David's 'Lord' (Adonai) who sits at God's right hand. By emphasizing this position of shared cosmic authority and rule with God, Jesus argues that the Messiah possesses a divine nature rather than being just a human king.
verse_bank.lk_5_8: Peter reacts to Jesus's miracle with the same fear and acute awareness of sin as Old Testament prophets who encountered Yahweh (e.g., Isaiah in Isaiah 6:5). He recognizes he is in the presence of the Holy and Divine.
verse_bank.lk_5_13: Jesus heals the leper by His own inherent authority saying, 'I am willing; be cleansed.' Old Testament prophets had to pray to God to heal leprosy (e.g., Moses crying out for Miriam in Numbers 12:13), but Jesus heals by His own direct command.
verse_bank.lk_5_20: Jesus forgives the paralyzed man's sins. In the Old Testament, only God can forgive sins, because all sins are ultimately an offense against God. Jesus claims this exclusive divine prerogative for Himself.
verse_bank.lk_5_24: Jesus proves His divine authority to forgive sins (a power the scribes rightly noted belongs only to God) by miraculously healing the paralytic by His own word, proving His unseen divine claim with a visible divine action.
verse_bank.lk_5_34: Jesus refers to Himself as the 'bridegroom'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is exclusively and repeatedly described as the husband or bridegroom of Israel (e.g., Isaiah 54:5, Hosea 2:16). Jesus appropriates this divine identity for Himself.
verse_bank.lk_6_5: Jesus claims the title 'Lord of the Sabbath'. Since the Sabbath was instituted by Yahweh and is strictly His holy day (Exodus 20:8-11), claiming to be its Lord is claiming to be its divine author.
verse_bank.lk_6_8: Jesus is shown knowing the inner thoughts of the scribes and Pharisees. The Old Testament explicitly states that knowing the hearts and thoughts of men is an exclusive attribute of Yahweh alone (e.g., 1 Kings 8:39, Jeremiah 17:10).
verse_bank.lk_6_19: Power inherently goes out from Jesus to heal the multitudes. Unlike prophets who acted merely as human conduits asking for God's power through prayer, Jesus possesses divine, healing power inherently within Himself.
verse_bank.lk_6_47: Jesus equates hearing and doing *His* words with building a solid foundation for life. In the Old Testament, life and safety are built exclusively upon hearing and obeying the words of Yahweh (e.g., Deuteronomy 11:1). Jesus places His own words on the exact same authoritative level as God's.
verse_bank.lk_7_14: Jesus raises a dead man by his own direct command ('I say to you, arise'). Unlike Old Testament prophets who had to pray and cry out to God to raise the dead, Jesus exercises inherent divine authority over life and death, a prerogative belonging solely to Yahweh (1 Samuel 2:6).
verse_bank.lk_7_22: Jesus points to His miracles of making the blind see and the deaf hear as proof of His identity. This directly fulfills Isaiah 35:4-5, which explicitly prophesies that 'God' Himself will come to save His people, accompanied by these exact, exclusive miraculous signs.
verse_bank.lk_7_27: Jesus applies Malachi 3:1 to John the Baptist, indicating John is the messenger sent to prepare His way. In the original Malachi text, the messenger prepares the way for Yahweh Himself. By placing Himself as the one being prepared for, Jesus identifies Himself as Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_7_48: Jesus explicitly forgives the sinful woman's sins. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 43:25), only Yahweh has the authority to forgive sins, as sins are ultimately committed against Him. Jesus claims and exercises this exclusive divine prerogative by His own authority.
verse_bank.lk_8_24: Jesus rebukes the wind and the raging waves, instantly calming the chaotic storm. In the Old Testament (Psalm 107:29, Psalm 89:9), only Yahweh possesses the sovereign power to subdue the chaotic seas and calm storms. Jesus performs this action by His own inherent word.
verse_bank.lk_8_39: Jesus commands the formerly possessed man to return home and declare how much 'God' has done for him. The text notes that the man immediately departed and proclaimed how much 'Jesus' had done for him, establishing a direct, undeniable textual equivalence between the actions of God and the actions of Jesus.
verse_bank.lk_8_54: Jesus raises Jairus's dead daughter by directly commanding her ('Child, arise'). Contrasted with Old Testament prophets who petitioned Yahweh for resurrection, Jesus demonstrates He inherently possesses the Creator's authority to command and restore life by His mere word.
verse_bank.lk_9_1: Jesus inherently grants power and authority over demons and diseases to His disciples. Old Testament prophets performed miracles by crying out to God, but they did not have inherent authority to delegate supernatural power to others. This demonstrates sovereign divine authority.
verse_bank.lk_9_16: Jesus miraculously provides food for the multitudes in a desolate wilderness place. This directly parallels Exodus 16, where Yahweh alone provides manna supernaturally to feed the Israelites in the wilderness.
verse_bank.lk_9_26: Jesus refers to Himself as the 'Son of Man' who will come in glory with the holy angels. This draws directly from Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man is a divine figure riding the clouds who is given everlasting dominion, glory, and kingship.
verse_bank.lk_9_30: Moses and Elijah appear in glory to commune with Jesus on a mountain. In the Old Testament, Moses (Exodus 24) and Elijah (1 Kings 19) are the specific prophets who climbed a mountain to commune directly with Yahweh. Here, they are communing with Jesus.
verse_bank.lk_9_43: After Jesus heals a demon-possessed boy by His own direct rebuke (unlike prophets who had to cry out to God), the text notes the people were 'astonished at the majesty of God' while marveling at what Jesus was doing, identifying His inherent power directly with God's majesty.
verse_bank.lk_10_19: Jesus grants His followers authority over all the power of the enemy (Satan) and the spiritual realm. Having inherent authority over the spiritual realm and the ability to delegate it to others is a prerogative of God alone.
verse_bank.lk_10_22: Jesus claims absolute sovereignty ('All things have been handed over to me') and exclusive, mutual knowledge with God the Father. This level of incomprehensibility and unique divine relationship is an attribute exclusive to Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_13_12: In Psalm 145:8 (LXX), raising up those who are bowed down is an action explicitly attributed to Yahweh. Jesus performs this healing by His own direct command and touch, exercising divine prerogative rather than crying out to God like a prophet.
verse_bank.lk_13_27: Jesus places Himself in the role of the final eschatological Judge ('master of the house'), banishing the wicked by directly quoting the righteous judgment of Psalm 6:8 ('Depart from me, all you workers of evil!'), assuming an authority reserved for God.
verse_bank.lk_13_34: In the Old Testament (e.g., Ruth 2:12, Psalm 90:4 LXX), sheltering Israel under wings is imagery exclusively reserved for Yahweh. Jesus claims this divine role for Himself, expressing His own sovereign desire to gather Jerusalem under His wings.
verse_bank.lk_14_24: Jesus claims ownership of the eschatological banquet ('my banquet'), stepping out of the parable to address the crowd. In Isaiah 25:6, it is the Lord of hosts who prepares this grand feast for all people, showing Jesus identifying Himself with Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_14_26: Jesus demands absolute allegiance that supersedes all human family bonds. In the Old Testament (e.g., Deuteronomy 33:9), this level of supreme devotion and forsaking of family is exclusively demanded by and reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_15_4: In Ezekiel 34:11-16, Yahweh declares that because the human shepherds failed, He Himself will personally come to seek out and save His lost sheep. When criticized by the Pharisees for receiving sinners, Jesus defends His actions by comparing Himself to a shepherd seeking a lost sheep. By applying this specific metaphor to His own ministry, Jesus is identifying Himself as the Divine Shepherd, taking on a role and action that Yahweh exclusively reserved for Himself.
verse_bank.lk_23_43: Granting entry into paradise and eternal life is a prerogative of God alone. Jesus guarantees this by His own inherent authority without petitioning God.
verse_bank.lk_24_45: In the Old Testament, illuminating the mind to understand divine truth is an action of God (e.g., Psalm 118:18 in LXX). Jesus performs this divine action directly on His disciples.
verse_bank.lk_24_47: Forgiveness of sins is God's exclusive prerogative, and in the Old Testament, it is sought in Yahweh's name. Here, repentance and forgiveness are explicitly proclaimed in Jesus' name.
verse_bank.lk_24_49: In the Old Testament (e.g., Joel 3:1 in LXX), pouring out the Spirit is an action exclusively performed by Yahweh. Jesus claims the divine authority to send this 'power from on high' Himself.
verse_bank.lk_24_52: Worship is strictly reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., Exodus 34:14). Jesus accepts their worship as He ascends to heaven, unlike angels or prophets who refuse worship.
verse_bank.lk_3_4: Quotes Isaiah 40:3, which prophesies a voice in the wilderness preparing the way for Yahweh. Since John the Baptist is explicitly preparing the way for Jesus, the text directly identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_3_16: John the Baptist declares that Jesus will baptize with the Holy Spirit. In the Old Testament (such as Joel 2:28), only Yahweh has the power and prerogative to pour out His Spirit upon people. Jesus is performing a distinctly divine action.
verse_bank.lk_4_34: A demon identifies Jesus as 'the Holy One of God.' In the Old Testament, 'the Holy One' (or 'the Holy One of Israel') is a prominent title exclusively reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_4_35: Jesus casts out a demon by His own direct command ('Be silent and come out of him!'). Unlike Old Testament prophets who had to invoke Yahweh's name or pray to perform miracles, Jesus exercises inherent, supreme authority over the spiritual realm.
verse_bank.lk_4_39: Jesus heals Simon's mother-in-law by directly 'rebuking' the fever. Old Testament prophets like Elijah and Elisha had to cry out and pray to Yahweh for healing miracles, but Jesus commands nature and sickness by His own absolute authority.
verse_bank.lk_4_41: Demons are cast out while crying, 'You are the Son of God!' The spiritual entities recognize His divine nature and shared essence with God, knowing they are subject to His supreme authority.
verse_bank.lk_17_18: A healed leper falls at Jesus' feet thanking Him. Instead of deflecting this honor (as prophets or angels do), Jesus accepts it and equates the man's action of thanking Him to 'giving praise to God', implicitly identifying Himself as God.
verse_bank.lk_17_30: Jesus claims the title 'Son of Man'. In the Old Testament (Daniel 7:13-14), the Son of Man is a divine, heavenly figure who is given an everlasting kingdom and whom all nations serve, prerogatives belonging only to God.
verse_bank.lk_18_8: Jesus uses the divine title 'Son of Man' (from Daniel 7) and questions if He will find faith on earth when He returns. While Old Testament prophets directed people's faith to Yahweh, Jesus makes Himself the direct object of this faith.
verse_bank.lk_18_19: Jesus affirms the Old Testament truth that only God is good. He does not deny His own goodness, but challenges the ruler's casual use of the title to lead him to the logical conclusion: if Jesus is truly good, He must be God.
verse_bank.lk_18_31: Jesus again claims the divine title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7:13-14, asserting that the Old Testament prophets explicitly wrote about Him and His divine mission.
verse_bank.lk_18_42: In Isaiah 35:4-5 (LXX), the opening of the eyes of the blind is a specific sign of God Himself coming to save. Unlike prophets who prayed to God for miracles, Jesus heals the blind man by His own inherent, authoritative command ('Recover your sight'), acting as Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_11_14: In Exodus 4:11 and Isaiah 35:5-6, making the mute speak is an action specifically attributed to Yahweh's power. Jesus performs this miracle by His own inherent authority without calling out to God, doing what only God does.
verse_bank.lk_11_20: Jesus claims to cast out demons by the 'finger of God' (an Old Testament idiom for Yahweh's sovereign power, e.g., Exodus 8:19). Rather than praying for God to act, Jesus wields God's power directly as His own.
verse_bank.lk_11_22: Jesus describes Himself as the 'stronger one' who overpowers the strong man (Satan) to rescue captives. In the Old Testament (Isaiah 49:24-25), only Yahweh is said to have the power to take prey from the mighty and save captives.
verse_bank.lk_12_8: Jesus claims the title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7:13-14, a divine figure given everlasting dominion. Furthermore, He makes a person's eternal standing before God's angels dependent entirely on their allegiance to Him, an ultimate judgment prerogative belonging only to Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_12_40: Jesus uses the divine title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7:13-14, referring to the heavenly figure who shares God's dominion, and asserts He will return to bring ultimate judgment—a role the Old Testament attributes exclusively to Yahweh on the Day of the Lord.
verse_bank.lk_12_43: In this parable, Jesus identifies Himself as the 'Master' (Lord) who will return to judge His servants based on their faithfulness. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the ultimate Master and Judge of His people.
verse_bank.lk_12_49: Jesus states He came to 'cast fire on the earth,' referring to bringing divine judgment. Throughout the Old Testament (e.g., Ezekiel 39:6, Amos 1-2), sending consuming fire upon the earth to judge it is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_1_16: John the Baptist is prophesied to turn the children of Israel to 'the Lord their God' by preparing the way for Jesus. In Malachi 3:1, the messenger prepares the way for Yahweh Himself. Thus, Jesus is identified contextually as the Lord their God.
verse_bank.lk_1_17: John goes before Him to prepare a people for 'the Lord' in the spirit of Elijah. Malachi 4:5-6 prophesies Elijah preceding the coming of Yahweh. John precedes Jesus, equating the arrival of Jesus with the arrival of Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_1_43: Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Spirit, calls the unborn Jesus 'my Lord' (Kurios). In the Greek Old Testament (Septuagint), Kurios is the standard translation used to replace the divine name Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_1_76: Zacharias prophesies that John will go 'before the face of the Lord to prepare His ways.' This directly parallels Isaiah 40:3 and Malachi 3:1, where a messenger prepares the way for Yahweh. Since John prepares the way for Jesus, the text is identifying Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.lk_2_11: The angel announces the birth of a 'Savior, who is Christ the Lord.' In Isaiah 43:11, Yahweh emphatically declares, 'besides Me there is no savior.' Applying the exclusive divine title of Savior and Lord (Kurios) to Jesus equates Him with the God of the Old Testament.
verse_bank.jn_3_13: Jesus claims the title 'Son of Man' (Daniel 7:13-14) and fulfills Proverbs 30:4 (LXX), which states that only God has 'gone up to heaven, and come down.' By claiming heavenly descent, He takes on a divine attribute.
verse_bank.jn_3_29: John the Baptist identifies Jesus as the 'bridegroom.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is exclusively portrayed as the husband and bridegroom of His people (Hosea 2:16-20).
verse_bank.jn_3_31: Jesus is described as coming from heaven and being 'above all,' asserting supreme, universal authority. This exalted status is reserved for God alone (Psalm 96:9 LXX).
verse_bank.jn_4_10: Jesus offers 'living water.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly identifies Himself as the 'fountain of water of life' (Jeremiah 2:13 LXX). Jesus claims to be the divine source of this life.
verse_bank.jn_4_14: Jesus promises that the water He gives becomes a spring welling up to eternal life. Providing eternal life is an action exclusive to God, reinforcing His claim as the divine source of living water.
verse_bank.jn_4_42: The Samaritans identify Jesus as the 'Savior of the world.' This is an exclusive title of Yahweh, who states in Isaiah 43:11, 'I am God; and beside me there is no Saviour.'
verse_bank.jn_4_50: Jesus heals the official's son from a great distance simply by speaking a command. This mirrors Yahweh in Psalm 106:20 (LXX), 'He sent his word, and healed them,' showing Jesus possesses inherent divine authority over sickness.
verse_bank.jn_19_37: John applies Zechariah 12:10 directly to Jesus. In Zechariah, it is Yahweh speaking, saying 'they shall look upon me' who was pierced or mocked. By applying this Old Testament passage where Yahweh is the subject to Jesus being pierced, the text identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_20_22: Jesus physically breathes on his disciples to impart the Holy Spirit. In the Old Testament (e.g., Genesis 2:7), it is God alone who breathes the breath of life. Bestowing the Spirit of God is an exclusive divine prerogative, which Jesus performs inherently by his own breath.
verse_bank.jn_20_23: Jesus delegates the authority to forgive sins to his disciples. In the Old Testament (Isaiah 43:25), forgiving sins against God is an action exclusively reserved for God. By possessing the inherent power to not only forgive sins but also to delegate this divine authority, Jesus exercises a prerogative of Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_20_28: Thomas addresses Jesus as 'My Lord and my God', which are titles exclusively reserved for the Creator (Yahweh) in the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 35:23). Jesus accepts this direct worship and divine title without correcting Thomas, proving He affirms the claim to be God.
verse_bank.jn_13_19: Jesus predicts the future and applies the divine name 'I am he' (ego eimi) to Himself, mirroring Yahweh in Isaiah 43:10 who says 'that you may know, and believe, and understand that I am he.' Additionally, only God can declare the end from the beginning.
