verse_bank.jn_13_31: Jesus claims the title 'Son of Man' and its associated glory. In Daniel 7:13-14, the Son of Man is a divine figure who comes with the clouds, is given an everlasting kingdom, and is served by all nations—prerogatives belonging only to God.
verse_bank.jn_13_34: Jesus issues a 'new commandment' based on His own inherent authority and uses Himself as the ultimate standard of love. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the sole Lawgiver who establishes commandments for His people.
verse_bank.jn_14_1: Jesus commands His disciples to place the exact same faith and trust in Him as they do in God. In the Old Testament, trusting completely in Yahweh is commanded, while placing absolute trust in a mere creature is condemned.
verse_bank.jn_14_6: Jesus claims to be the embodiment of 'the truth' and 'the life.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is identified as the God of truth (Psalm 31:5) and the fountain of life (Psalm 36:9). He also claims exclusive access to the Father.
verse_bank.jn_14_9: Jesus claims that seeing Him is equivalent to seeing the Father. No prophet or angel could ever say that seeing them is seeing God; this asserts a shared divine essence and perfect revelation of Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_14_13: Jesus promises to personally answer and fulfill prayers directed to Him in His name. In the Old Testament, hearing and answering prayers is an action exclusively reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 65:2).
verse_bank.jn_14_14: A reiteration that Jesus Himself, by His own power, will answer prayers ('I will do it'). This is an exercise of divine omnipotence and omniscience, as He must hear all prayers to answer them.
verse_bank.jn_14_23: Jesus promises that He and the Father will spiritually dwell within the believer. This asserts omnipresence and fulfills Old Testament promises where Yahweh Himself says He will dwell among His people (Leviticus 26:11-12, Zechariah 2:10).
verse_bank.jn_14_27: Jesus offers His own supernatural peace to the disciples. In the Old Testament, granting true, lasting peace is a prerogative and blessing of Yahweh alone (Psalm 29:11).
verse_bank.jn_21_6: Jesus miraculously commands an immense catch of fish simply by His own spoken word. In the Old Testament, commanding the creatures of the sea is a sovereign action of Yahweh (e.g., Jonah 1:17). While prophets performed miracles by praying or crying out to God, Jesus exercises this authority inherently, demonstrating His divine power over nature.
verse_bank.jn_21_15: Jesus claims ownership over the believers by calling them 'my lambs'. In the Old Testament (Ezekiel 34:11-31), Yahweh declares that the people are 'my sheep' and that He Himself is their true, ultimate shepherd. By claiming the flock of God as His own, Jesus assumes a title and role exclusively reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_21_17: Peter tells Jesus, 'Lord, you know everything; you know that I love you,' ascribing to Him the knowledge of all things and the secrets of the heart. The Old Testament is clear that uniquely knowing the hearts of all people is an exclusive attribute of God (1 Kings 8:39; Proverbs 24:12). Jesus accepts this statement of His divine omniscience without correction.
verse_bank.jn_21_22: Jesus speaks of His future return ('until I come') as the eschatological event that consummates history. In the Old Testament, the end of the age is brought about by the 'Day of the Lord'—the coming of Yahweh Himself (Zechariah 14). By making His own return the focal point of the end times, Jesus substitutes Himself for Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_1_1: Explicitly identifies the Word (Jesus) as God, existing eternally with God in the beginning.
verse_bank.jn_1_3: The Old Testament states God alone created everything (Isaiah 44:24 LXX). By saying all things were made through the Word (Jesus), He is identified as performing an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_1_4: The Old Testament identifies Yahweh as the sole fountain of life (Psalm 35:9 LXX). Saying life inherently is in Jesus attributes to Him what belongs uniquely to God.
verse_bank.jn_1_10: Reaffirms that the world was made through Him, an action reserved for God who created the world alone (Isaiah 44:24 LXX).
verse_bank.jn_1_14: States the Word became flesh and manifested divine glory, paralleling Yahweh tabernacling with His people.
verse_bank.jn_1_15: John the Baptist asserts Jesus' pre-existence ('he was before me' despite John being born earlier physically), an attribute of eternal existence belonging to God.
verse_bank.jn_1_18: Explicitly refers to Jesus with the divine title 'God the only Son' (or 'the only begotten God'), distinguishing His personhood while affirming His shared divine nature.
verse_bank.jn_1_23: John the Baptist applies Isaiah 40:3 to his own ministry. In the Old Testament, the voice prepares the way for Yahweh. Since John is preparing the way for Jesus, Jesus is identified as Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_1_29: In the Old Testament, it is Yahweh who forgives and redeems from sin. Calling Jesus the one who takes away the sin of the world assigns Him a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.jn_1_34: John bears witness that Jesus is the Son of God, a title affirming His unique divine nature.
verse_bank.jn_1_48: Jesus demonstrates divine omniscience by seeing Nathanael remotely before they had met, an attribute belonging exclusively to God.
verse_bank.jn_1_49: Nathanael recognizes Jesus' divine omniscience and attributes to Him the divine title 'Son of God'.
verse_bank.jn_1_51: Jesus references Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12), claiming to be the very bridge between heaven and earth and the place of divine revelation.
verse_bank.jn_2_11: Yahweh states He will not share His glory with another (Isaiah 42:8 LXX). Jesus manifesting His own inherent glory through the miracle of creation demonstrates His shared divine nature.
verse_bank.jn_2_16: Jesus refers to the Temple as 'my Father's house,' claiming a unique, direct sonship to God which the Jews later recognize as a claim to equality with God.
verse_bank.jn_2_19: In the Old Testament, it is God who gives life and raises the dead (1 Kingdoms 2:6 LXX). Jesus claims He will raise His own body by His own inherent authority.
verse_bank.jn_2_24: Knowing the hearts of all people is an attribute exclusively reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament (3 Kingdoms 8:39 LXX). Jesus is described as possessing this omniscience.
verse_bank.jn_2_25: Reaffirms that Jesus knows the inner thoughts and hearts of all people, an attribute of Yahweh alone.
verse_bank.jn_11_4: In Isaiah 42:8, Yahweh explicitly states He will not share His glory with another. Jesus claims that this miracle is for both the glory of God and the glorification of the Son, claiming a share in the exclusive divine glory.
verse_bank.jn_11_25: In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the sole author of life and resurrection (1 Sam 2:6, Deut 32:39). Jesus does not merely claim the power to resurrect, but inherently identifies Himself as the source of resurrection and life itself.
verse_bank.jn_11_43: While Old Testament prophets like Elijah (1 Kings 17) had to cry out and pray to Yahweh to raise the dead, Jesus inherently holds the authority of life and death, raising Lazarus by His own direct command.
verse_bank.jn_12_32: In Isaiah 66:18, Yahweh declares that He will gather all nations and tongues to Himself. Jesus claims this exact divine prerogative, stating that He will draw all people to Himself.
verse_bank.jn_12_41: John quotes Isaiah 6, where Isaiah sees the glory of Yahweh of Hosts on His throne (Isaiah 6:1-5). John explicitly states that Isaiah was actually seeing the glory of Jesus, directly identifying Jesus as the Yahweh of the Old Testament.
verse_bank.jn_12_45: Jesus claims that seeing Him is synonymous with seeing the Father, implying a shared divine nature and essence that goes beyond mere representation by a human prophet.
verse_bank.jn_12_46: In the Old Testament, Yahweh is explicitly described as the light of His people (Psalm 27:1, Micah 7:8). Jesus takes this exclusive divine attribute for Himself, declaring that He is the light of the world.
verse_bank.jn_17_2: Jesus claims the authority to give eternal life. In the Old Testament, Yahweh alone is the author and giver of life (Deuteronomy 32:39). Jesus exercising this authority is an exclusively divine prerogative.
verse_bank.jn_17_5: Jesus asks to be glorified with the glory He shared with the Father before the world existed. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly states He will not share His glory with another (Isaiah 42:8). Jesus sharing this glory indicates He is of the same divine essence.
verse_bank.jn_17_10: Jesus claims that everything belonging to the Father belongs to Him. Since Yahweh is the sole creator and owner of all things (Psalm 23:1, LXX numbering), claiming co-ownership of everything is a direct claim to equality with God.
verse_bank.jn_17_11: Jesus states that the Father has given Him the divine name. In the Old Testament, the Name of Yahweh represents His supreme authority and presence. For Jesus to possess and protect His disciples in the divine name signifies His equality with God.
verse_bank.jn_17_24: Jesus speaks of His glory and being loved by the Father 'before the foundation of the world,' demonstrating His eternal pre-existence. In the Old Testament, only God is eternal and exists before creation (Psalm 89:2, LXX numbering).
verse_bank.jn_18_5: Jesus identifies Himself with the phrase 'I am he' (Ego eimi in Greek). This echoes the divine name of Yahweh revealed in the Old Testament, specifically in passages like Isaiah 43:10.
verse_bank.jn_18_6: Upon Jesus uttering the divine name 'I am he,' the soldiers draw back and fall to the ground. In the Old Testament, falling to the ground is the standard human reaction to encountering a theophany or the direct presence of God (Ezekiel 1:28).
verse_bank.jn_18_36: Jesus claims a kingdom that is 'not of this world.' In the Old Testament, an everlasting, transcendent kingdom belongs to Yahweh and the divine Son of Man (Daniel 7:14), contrasting with earthly human kingdoms.
verse_bank.jn_9_5: Jesus claims to be the 'light of the world'. In the Old Testament, being the light is a title and attribute of Yahweh (Psalm 26:1 LXX, Isaiah 60:19).
verse_bank.jn_9_7: Jesus opens the eyes of a man born blind. Isaiah 35:4-5 prophesies that God Himself will come to save, and as a sign, 'the eyes of the blind shall be opened.' Jesus exercises this divine prerogative by His own authority.
verse_bank.jn_9_38: The healed man worships Jesus, and Jesus accepts it. In the Old Testament, worship is exclusively reserved for Yahweh (Exodus 34:14). By accepting worship without correcting the man, Jesus affirms His divinity.
verse_bank.jn_10_11: Jesus claims to be the 'good shepherd'. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is explicitly declared to be the shepherd of His people (Psalm 22:1 LXX) and promises to come and shepherd them Himself (Ezekiel 34:11).
verse_bank.jn_10_18: Jesus claims the inherent authority to lay down His life and raise Himself from the dead. Absolute power over life and death and the ability to resurrect inherently belong only to God.
verse_bank.jn_10_28: Jesus claims to give eternal life and says no one can snatch His sheep out of His hand. This directly mirrors Yahweh's exclusive claim in Deuteronomy 32:39 (LXX), 'there is none who shall deliver out of my hands'.
verse_bank.jn_10_30: Jesus explicitly claims 'I and the Father are one', declaring a unity of essence and divine authority with God the Father.
verse_bank.jn_10_33: The Jewish leaders recognize Jesus' words as a direct claim to deity, stating He makes Himself God. Jesus does not correct their understanding, affirming their realization of His claim.
verse_bank.jn_7_37: Jesus invites people to come to Him to drink, claiming to be the source of living water (v. 38). In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly calls Himself the 'fountain of water of life' (Jeremiah 2:13 LXX). Jesus takes this divine role for Himself.
verse_bank.jn_8_12: Jesus claims 'I am the light of the world.' In the Old Testament, being the everlasting light and salvation is a title and role exclusively reserved for Yahweh (Psalm 26:1 LXX, Isaiah 60:19-20).
verse_bank.jn_8_24: Jesus tells the Jews they will die in their sins unless they believe that 'I am he' (ego eimi). This directly parallels Isaiah 43:10, where Yahweh uses this exact phrase to declare His unique deity. He also ties belief in His identity to salvation from sin, a prerogative Yahweh claims exclusively (Isaiah 43:25).
verse_bank.jn_8_28: Jesus says 'then you will know that I am he', perfectly echoing Yahweh's declaration in Isaiah 43:10 ('that you may know... that I am he'). He also refers to Himself as the 'Son of Man', the divine figure who receives everlasting dominion and worship in Daniel 7:13-14.
verse_bank.jn_8_51: Jesus claims that keeping His word grants eternal life and prevents one from seeing death. In the Old Testament, Yahweh alone has the inherent power over life and death (Deuteronomy 32:39).
verse_bank.jn_8_58: Jesus states 'before Abraham was, I am.' This is a direct invocation of the divine name revealed to Moses in Exodus 3:14 ('I AM THE BEING' / 'I AM WHO I AM'). The Jews recognized this as a claim to deity, as they immediately tried to stone Him for blasphemy.
verse_bank.jn_5_17: Jesus claims the right to work on the Sabbath just as God the Father does, asserting the divine prerogative of continuously sustaining creation.
verse_bank.jn_5_18: The Jewish leaders explicitly understood Jesus's claim of divine sonship and Sabbath work as making Himself equal with God.
verse_bank.jn_5_19: Jesus states that whatever the Father does, the Son does likewise, claiming equal power, omnipotence, and identical operation with God.
verse_bank.jn_5_21: Jesus claims the power to raise the dead and give life by His own will, an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh (1 Samuel 2:6).
verse_bank.jn_5_22: Jesus claims the role of the ultimate Judge of all humanity, a role the Old Testament reserves exclusively for Yahweh (Genesis 18:25, Psalm 50:6).
verse_bank.jn_5_23: Jesus demands equal honor and reverence as the Father, whereas Yahweh explicitly states He will not share His glory with another (Isaiah 42:8).
verse_bank.jn_5_26: Jesus claims to possess 'life in himself' (aseity or self-existence), which is the defining uncreated characteristic of God.
verse_bank.jn_5_27: Jesus invokes the title 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7:13-14, claiming the authority of the divine figure who is given everlasting dominion and judgment.
verse_bank.jn_6_19: Jesus walks on the water, visually enacting Job 9:8, which states that God alone treads on the waves of the sea.
verse_bank.jn_6_20: Jesus comforts the disciples saying 'It is I' (Greek: ego eimi, 'I am'), echoing the Divine Name from Exodus 3:14 and Isaiah 43:10.
verse_bank.jn_6_35: While Yahweh provided physical manna in the wilderness (Exodus 16), Jesus claims to BE the ultimate 'bread of life' that gives eternal life.
verse_bank.jn_6_38: Jesus explicitly claims pre-existence in heaven before His incarnation, an attribute of the eternal God.
verse_bank.jn_6_40: Jesus claims that He Himself will personally raise believers from the dead on the final day, exercising a divine prerogative of God.
verse_bank.jn_6_62: Jesus refers to ascending back to where He was before, further affirming His pre-existence and divine origin from heaven.
verse_bank.jn_15_26: Jesus claims the authority to send the Holy Spirit. In the Old Testament, sending or pouring out the Spirit is an action performed exclusively by Yahweh (e.g., Joel 2:28, Ezekiel 36:27). By performing this divine action, Jesus exercises a prerogative reserved only for God.
verse_bank.jn_16_7: Jesus asserts His power to send the Helper (the Holy Spirit). Since only Yahweh pours out His Spirit in the Old Testament, Jesus sending the Spirit demonstrates His divine nature and authority.
verse_bank.jn_16_14: Jesus states that the Spirit of truth will glorify Him. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly declares that He will not give His glory to another (Isaiah 42:8). For Jesus to be the direct recipient of divine glory points to His equality with God.
verse_bank.jn_16_15: Jesus claims that absolutely everything the Father (God) has belongs to Him. Since God does not share His unique divine attributes or sovereign possessions with any created being, claiming co-ownership of all the Father has is a direct claim to equality with Yahweh.
verse_bank.jn_16_30: The disciples recognize Jesus's omniscience, stating 'you know all things'. Perfect knowledge of all things and knowing the hearts of men is an attribute reserved exclusively for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., 1 Kings 8:39). Jesus accepts this attribution of a divine trait without correcting them.
verse_bank.mt_19_28: Jesus claims He will sit on a glorious throne to judge the tribes of Israel. In the Old Testament, sitting on the ultimate heavenly throne of judgment is exclusively Yahweh's prerogative (Psalm 9:7-8). He also claims the divine 'Son of Man' title from Daniel 7.
verse_bank.mt_19_29: Jesus promises eternal reward to those who sacrifice everything 'for my name's sake.' In the Old Testament, requiring absolute devotion and acting for the sake of the divine name is strictly reserved for Yahweh (1 Samuel 12:22, Psalm 23:3).
verse_bank.mt_20_34: Jesus restores sight to the blind by His own inherent touch and authority, rather than praying to God like a prophet. The Old Testament explicitly identifies opening the eyes of the blind as an action unique to Yahweh (Psalm 146:8, Isaiah 35:4-5).
verse_bank.mt_7_21: Jesus claims to be the 'Lord' to whom people will appeal on the Day of Judgment, taking the role of the ultimate Judge of humanity. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the sole Judge of the earth (Genesis 18:25).
verse_bank.mt_7_22: People claim to prophesy and cast out demons specifically in Jesus' name. In the Old Testament, true prophets only spoke and performed miracles in the name of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_7_23: Jesus exercises divine prerogative by personally banishing the wicked on the final Day of Judgment, an authority reserved only for God in the Old Testament (Joel 3:12).
verse_bank.mt_8_2: A leper worships Jesus and states Jesus has the inherent power to cure leprosy if He simply wills it. In the Old Testament, curing leprosy was seen as an act only God could do, as noted by the King of Israel (2 Kings 5:7).
verse_bank.mt_8_3: Jesus cures the leper by His own sovereign will, stating 'I am willing. Be clean!' Old Testament prophets had to pray to God for healing, but Jesus heals by His own inherent divine command.
verse_bank.mt_8_8: The centurion believes Jesus can heal from a distance simply by speaking a word. The Old Testament describes Yahweh as the one who 'sent out his word and healed them' (Psalm 107:20).
verse_bank.mt_8_26: Jesus directly rebukes the winds and the sea to completely calm a storm. In the Old Testament, calming the raging sea is an action exclusively performed by Yahweh (Psalm 89:9, Psalm 107:28-29).
verse_bank.mt_8_27: The disciples marvel that the winds and the sea obey Jesus. The direct obedience of the elements of nature points to Jesus possessing the Creator's inherent authority over creation.
verse_bank.mt_8_29: The demons acknowledge Jesus has the authority to judge and punish them at the final appointed time, an eschatological judgment role belonging exclusively to God.
verse_bank.mt_25_1: Jesus portrays Himself as the Bridegroom in the parable of the ten virgins. In the Old Testament (Isaiah 54:5, Hosea 2:16-19), Yahweh is exclusively described as the husband and bridegroom of His people.
verse_bank.mt_25_31: Jesus claims He will come in glory with His angels to sit on His throne. In the Old Testament (Zechariah 14:5), it is Yahweh who comes with all His holy ones, and the 'Son of Man' reference links to the divine figure in Daniel 7:13-14.
verse_bank.mt_25_32: Jesus describes Himself gathering all nations and judging them, separating sheep from goats. In Ezekiel 34:11-17, Yahweh explicitly states that He Himself will shepherd His flock and judge between sheep and goats.
verse_bank.mt_25_40: Jesus identifies Himself as 'The King' who determines eternal destinies based on how people treated His brethren. In the Old Testament (Isaiah 33:22, Psalm 47:7), Yahweh is the supreme King and ultimate Judge of the earth.
verse_bank.mt_26_28: Jesus institutes the New Covenant with His own blood for the forgiveness of sins. In Jeremiah 31:31-34, it is Yahweh alone who institutes the New Covenant and forgives sins.
verse_bank.mt_26_64: Jesus claims to be the 'Son of Man' sitting at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds. In the Old Testament, riding the clouds is an exclusive attribute of Yahweh (Psalm 104:3, Isaiah 19:1), making this a direct claim to deity.
verse_bank.mt_26_65: The High Priest tears his clothes and accuses Jesus of blasphemy in response to Jesus' statement about coming on the clouds. This demonstrates that the Jewish authorities clearly understood Jesus' use of Old Testament imagery as a direct claim to equality with Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_3_3: Matthew directly applies Isaiah 40:3 ('prepare the way for Yahweh') to John the Baptist, who is preparing the way for Jesus. By identifying Jesus as the one whose way is being prepared, the text identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_3_11: John states that Jesus will baptize with the Holy Spirit. In the Old Testament (such as in Joel 2:28), only Yahweh has the authority and ability to pour out His Spirit upon humanity. Jesus performing this identifies Him with God's unique prerogative.
verse_bank.mt_3_12: Jesus is described as having a winnowing fork in His hand, ready to execute final judgment by gathering the wheat and burning the chaff with unquenchable fire. In the Old Testament, the role of the final, eschatological judge of mankind is strictly reserved for Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_4_10: Jesus directly commands Satan to leave by His own inherent authority ('Be gone, Satan!'). In the biblical tradition, even the greatest angels did not dare rebuke Satan on their own authority, but instead appealed to God, saying, 'The Lord rebuke you' (Zechariah 3:2). Jesus exercises divine authority over the devil.
verse_bank.mt_4_16: Matthew identifies Jesus as the 'great light' dawning on the people, fulfilling Isaiah 9:1-2. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the light of His people (Psalm 27:1), and this exact prophecy in Isaiah continues just a few verses later by explicitly calling this arriving figure the 'Mighty God' (Isaiah 9:6).
verse_bank.mt_4_23: Jesus heals every disease and affliction by His own power. In the Old Testament, Yahweh uniquely declares 'I am the Lord who heals you' (Exodus 15:26) and is the one 'who heals all your diseases' (Psalm 103:3). Unlike Old Testament prophets who had to pray to God for healing miracles, Jesus heals inherently.
verse_bank.mt_17_2: Jesus' face shines like the sun and His clothes become white as light. In Psalm 104:2, Yahweh is described as 'covering Yourself with light as with a garment.' Jesus is manifesting the inherent, unborrowed glory that belongs only to God.
verse_bank.mt_17_3: Moses and Elijah appear and talk with Jesus on the mountain. In the Old Testament, Moses (Exodus 24) and Elijah (1 Kings 19) are the two prophets who climbed a mountain to commune directly with Yahweh. Here, they are communing with Jesus.
verse_bank.mt_17_26: The temple tax was paid to God for the upkeep of His house. By stating that kings collect taxes from strangers but their own 'sons are free,' Jesus claims exemption. He identifies Himself as the unique Son of God, placing Himself above the requirement to pay tribute to Yahweh because He shares in that divine rule.
verse_bank.mt_17_27: Jesus directs Peter to catch a single fish which will have the exact coin needed to pay the tax. This demonstrates divine omniscience and sovereign providence over nature, an attribute reserved for the Creator who knows all creatures (Psalm 50:10-11).
verse_bank.mt_18_12: Jesus teaches that the Son of Man seeks the lost sheep. In Ezekiel 34:11-12, after rebuking the human shepherds of Israel, Yahweh explicitly declares that He Himself will come down to search for His lost sheep and rescue them. Jesus is stepping into the role that Yahweh claimed for Himself.
verse_bank.mt_18_20: Jesus says, 'For where two or three are gathered together in My name, I am there in the midst of them.' Jesus promises His simultaneous presence everywhere in the world where believers gather. This is the divine attribute of omnipresence, perfectly paralleling Yahweh's promise in Exodus 20:24.
verse_bank.mt_15_25: The Canaanite woman kneels and offers worship to Jesus, an act reserved strictly for Yahweh (Exodus 34:14). Jesus accepts this worship and grants her request without rebuke, unlike angels or prophets who reject worship.
verse_bank.mt_15_30: Jesus miraculously heals the blind, lame, crippled, and mute by His own inherent power. In Isaiah 35:4-6, it is prophesied that Yahweh Himself will come to perform these exact healings.
verse_bank.mt_15_32: Jesus miraculously provides bread for a multitude in a desolate wilderness so they won't collapse, directly paralleling Yahweh providing manna for the Israelites in the desert (Exodus 16).
verse_bank.mt_16_16: Peter declares Jesus as the 'Son of the living God.' Jesus affirms this title in the following verse, accepting a unique, uncreated filial relationship that implies sharing the exact divine nature of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_16_18: Jesus declares He will build 'my church' (or assembly). In the Old Testament, the congregation or assembly of believers belongs exclusively to Yahweh (e.g., the 'assembly of the Lord' in Numbers 27:17).
verse_bank.mt_16_19: Jesus exercises supreme divine prerogative by granting Peter the 'keys of the kingdom of heaven.' Only God possesses the authority to grant or deny access to heaven, paralleling God dispensing authority in Isaiah 22:22.
verse_bank.mt_16_27: Jesus claims the divine 'Son of Man' title (Daniel 7:13-14), stating He will come with 'His angels' to judge and repay each person. In the Old Testament (Psalm 62:12, Jeremiah 17:10), judging and repaying mankind is the exclusive right of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_21_3: Jesus refers to Himself absolutely as 'The Lord' (Kyrios), claiming sovereign ownership and authority. In the Greek translation of the Old Testament (the Septuagint), Kyrios is the standard title used to translate God's divine name, Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_21_14: Isaiah 35:4-6 prophesies that God Himself will come to save His people, and as a sign, the eyes of the blind will be opened and the lame will leap. Jesus fulfills this divine prophecy by healing the blind and lame by His own inherent authority.
verse_bank.mt_21_16: When the chief priests become indignant at children praising Jesus, He defends their worship by quoting Psalm 8:2. In the Old Testament, Psalm 8 is entirely about the majesty and praise directed exclusively to Yahweh. By applying this to Himself, Jesus accepts divine worship.
verse_bank.mt_21_19: Jesus commands a fig tree to wither, and it instantly obeys His word. Unlike Old Testament prophets who had to cry out to God to perform nature miracles, Jesus demonstrates absolute, inherent sovereignty over creation—a prerogative of Yahweh alone (e.g., Psalm 33:9).
verse_bank.mt_21_37: In the Parable of the Tenants, Jesus explicitly distinguishes Himself from all the Old Testament prophets (who are merely described as 'servants') by identifying Himself as the unique 'Son' and 'heir' of the Father, claiming a divine filial relationship distinct from humanity.
verse_bank.mt_22_43: Jesus challenges the religious leaders by pointing out that King David, speaking by the Holy Spirit, calls the coming Messiah his 'Lord' (Adonai). This implies the Messiah is pre-existent and holds a divine authority far superior to any earthly human king.
verse_bank.mt_22_44: Jesus applies Psalm 110:1 to Himself ('The Lord said to my Lord...'). To be invited to sit at the 'right hand' of Yahweh is to be granted a full share in God's universal sovereign rule, omnipotence, and glory—a position reserved exclusively for a divine person.
verse_bank.mt_22_45: Jesus concludes His argument by stating that since David calls the Messiah 'Lord', the Messiah cannot merely be a human descendant. He must be a divine figure who existed before David and exercises divine authority over him.
verse_bank.mt_27_45: In Amos 8:9, Yahweh declares He will make the sun go down at noon and darken the earth. The cosmic darkness during Jesus' crucifixion directly parallels this divine action of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_27_51: Earthquakes and splitting rocks are classic Old Testament signs of Yahweh's direct, physical presence (theophany), as seen when God descended on Mount Sinai in Exodus 19:18.
verse_bank.mt_27_52: In Ezekiel 37:12-13, Yahweh explicitly states that He alone is the one who opens graves and raises His people from the dead. Jesus' death inherently triggers this exclusive divine action.
verse_bank.mt_28_9: Worship is strictly and exclusively reserved for God (Yahweh) in the Old Testament (Exodus 34:14). Unlike prophets or angels who immediately reject worship, Jesus accepts it without correcting the women.
verse_bank.mt_28_17: Jesus receives and accepts worship from His disciples on the mountain, an act considered blatant idolatry in Judaism unless the recipient is God Himself.
verse_bank.mt_28_18: Jesus claims absolute, universal authority over all creation. This fulfills Daniel 7:14, where the divine 'Son of Man' is given everlasting dominion, a prerogative and kingship belonging to God alone.
verse_bank.mt_28_19: Jesus commands baptism in the singular 'name' (not plural 'names') of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, equating His own identity and authority directly with God the Father.
verse_bank.mt_28_20: Jesus promises 'I am with you always,' claiming divine omnipresence. In the Old Testament, the promise 'I will be with you' is a signature guarantee given exclusively by Yahweh to His people (e.g., Exodus 3:12).
verse_bank.mt_9_2: Jesus forgives the sins of a paralyzed man. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 43:25), forgiving sins is an exclusive prerogative of Yahweh. Jesus exercises this divine right by His own authority.
verse_bank.mt_9_6: Jesus heals the paralytic specifically to prove He has the authority on earth to forgive sins, definitively claiming and demonstrating a power that belongs only to God.
verse_bank.mt_9_18: A synagogue leader kneels before Jesus (the Greek word 'proskuneo' often denotes worship) and asks Him to bring his dead daughter back to life, attributing the divine power over life and death directly to Jesus. Jesus accepts this reverence without rebuke.
verse_bank.mt_9_25: Jesus raises the dead girl simply by taking her hand. While Old Testament prophets (like Elijah in 1 Kings 17) had to earnestly pray and cry out to Yahweh to raise the dead, Jesus performs the resurrection by His own inherent command and authority.
verse_bank.mt_9_30: Jesus restores the sight of two blind men by His own touch. In the Old Testament, Psalm 146:8 explicitly states that it is Yahweh who opens the eyes of the blind.
verse_bank.mt_9_38: Jesus commands His disciples to pray to the 'Lord of the harvest' to send laborers. Immediately in the following verses (Matthew 10:1-5), Jesus Himself acts as that Lord by personally commissioning and sending out the twelve disciples into the harvest.
verse_bank.mt_10_1: Jesus delegates authority over unclean spirits and diseases to His disciples. Because God alone is the supreme sovereign over the spiritual realm, Jesus acting as the source who dispenses this spiritual authority demonstrates His divine nature.
verse_bank.mt_10_37: Jesus demands that His followers love Him more than their own parents or children. In the Old Testament, this level of absolute, supreme devotion and allegiance is demanded exclusively by Yahweh (Deuteronomy 6:5, the Shema).
verse_bank.mt_13_41: Jesus claims ownership of the angels ('his angels') and the Kingdom ('his kingdom'), and claims authority to execute final judgment. In the Old Testament, the angels belong strictly to God (Psalm 103:20), and God is the judge. Furthermore, the title 'Son of Man' references Daniel 7:13-14, a divine figure who receives an everlasting, worldwide dominion.
verse_bank.mt_14_19: Jesus miraculously multiplies bread to feed the multitudes in a desolate wilderness. This directly mirrors Yahweh providing manna supernaturally to the Israelites in the wilderness in Exodus 16, showing Jesus acting with the power of the Divine Provider.
verse_bank.mt_14_25: Jesus walks on the sea. The Old Testament, specifically Job 9:8, explicitly states that God alone stretches out the heavens and tramples or walks upon the waves of the sea.
verse_bank.mt_14_27: Jesus tells the disciples 'It is I' and 'Do not be afraid.' The Greek translation for 'It is I' is 'ego eimi', which literally means 'I AM'. This is the exact divine name God revealed to Moses in Exodus 3:14. Coupled with 'Do not be afraid' (a common reassurance from God, e.g., Isaiah 41:10), Jesus is applying the divine name to Himself.
verse_bank.mt_14_33: The disciples in the boat worship Jesus, and He fully accepts it. In the Old Testament, worship is strictly and exclusively reserved for God alone (Exodus 20:2-3). By accepting worship without rebuking them (as a prophet or angel would), Jesus assumes the prerogative of God.
verse_bank.mt_11_5: In Isaiah 35:4-5, the prophet declares that God Himself will come to save, accompanied by the blind seeing and the deaf hearing. Jesus performs these exact miracles by His own authority to prove He is that promised coming of God.
verse_bank.mt_11_10: Jesus quotes Malachi 3:1, where Yahweh says a messenger will prepare the way 'before Me.' Jesus applies this to John the Baptist preparing the way for Him, directly placing Himself in the position of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_11_27: Jesus claims that He and the Father have exclusive, exhaustive knowledge of one another. Since only an infinite God can fully comprehend God the Father, claiming this level of mutual knowledge is a claim to equality with God.
verse_bank.mt_11_28: In Exodus 33:14 and Jeremiah 31:25, Yahweh is the one who provides ultimate rest for weary souls. Jesus offers this universal, spiritual rest directly from Himself, assuming a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.mt_12_6: The Temple was sacred exclusively because it housed the presence of Yahweh on earth. To claim to be greater than the Temple is to claim to be the reality to which the Temple pointed: God's presence dwelling among men.
verse_bank.mt_12_8: The Old Testament repeatedly calls the Sabbath 'the Sabbath of Yahweh' (e.g., Exodus 20:10). It is a day belonging exclusively to God. By calling Himself 'Lord of the Sabbath,' Jesus exercises sovereign authority over a divine institution.
verse_bank.mt_12_25: The Old Testament explicitly states that God alone knows the thoughts and hearts of men (1 Kings 8:39, Jeremiah 17:10). Jesus demonstrates this exclusive divine attribute by reading the hidden thoughts of His critics.
verse_bank.mt_12_29: In Isaiah 49:24-25, Yahweh alone promises to bind the mighty tyrant and rescue captives. Jesus fulfills this exclusive divine role by binding Satan and casting out demons by His own inherent power.
verse_bank.mt_23_34: Jesus states 'I am sending you prophets and sages and teachers'. In the Old Testament (e.g., Jeremiah 7:25), it is exclusively Yahweh who sends prophets to Israel. Jesus acts as Yahweh by claiming this divine prerogative as His own.
verse_bank.mt_23_37: Jesus describes Himself wanting to gather Jerusalem's children 'as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings'. In the Old Testament, taking refuge under divine 'wings' is a metaphor exclusively reserved for Yahweh protecting His people (Psalm 91:4, Ruth 2:12). Jesus applies Yahweh's protective nature to Himself.
verse_bank.mt_24_30: Jesus claims He will come 'on the clouds of heaven, with power and great glory'. In the Old Testament, 'riding on the clouds' is an action uniquely describing Yahweh (Psalm 104:3, Isaiah 19:1). Jesus applies the prophecy of Daniel 7:13 to Himself, claiming divine status.
verse_bank.mt_24_31: Jesus says He will send 'his angels' to gather 'his elect'. In the Old Testament, the angels are the heavenly host of Yahweh, and Israel is the chosen elect of Yahweh (Isaiah 45:4). Jesus claims direct divine ownership over both God's angels and God's people.
verse_bank.mt_24_35: Jesus says, 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away.' The Old Testament declares that 'the word of our God endures forever' (Isaiah 40:8). Jesus elevates His own words to the exact same eternal, unfailing status as the words of Yahweh.
verse_bank.mt_5_11: In the Old Testament, true prophets were persecuted for the name and sake of Yahweh (God). Here, Jesus promises divine blessing for those who suffer 'on my account', equating loyalty to Himself with loyalty to God.
verse_bank.mt_5_17: Jesus claims He came to 'fulfill' the Law and the Prophets. In the Old Testament, the Law is given strictly by Yahweh. Only the Divine Lawgiver could claim to be the ultimate embodiment and fulfillment of the entire divine system of revelation.
verse_bank.mt_5_22: While Old Testament prophets always spoke with delegated authority ('Thus says the LORD'), Jesus quotes the divine Law of Moses and expands it using His own inherent authority ('But I say to you'). Only God has the authority to unilaterally redefine or expand God's Law.
verse_bank.mt_5_28: Jesus again uses the formula 'But I say to you' to elevate the Law from physical actions to the heart's intent. He exercises the divine prerogative to legislate and judge the inner thoughts of man, an action the Old Testament reserves exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., 1 Samuel 16:7).
verse_bank.mt_5_32: Jesus continues to modify the Mosaic Law given by God at Sinai, relying solely on His own authority ('But I say to you'). A mere human prophet would not dare alter God's decree without explicitly stating that God commanded the change.
verse_bank.mt_5_34: By issuing new commands regarding oaths with 'But I say to you', Jesus places His own words on the exact same level of absolute authority as the Law given by Yahweh in the Old Testament.
verse_bank.mt_5_39: Jesus redefines the Old Testament law of retaliation (Lex Talionis) with His own authoritative command ('But I say to you'). Only the Divine Lawgiver has the authority to amend the Law He originally instituted.
verse_bank.mt_5_44: Jesus commands love for enemies, overriding previous interpretations of the Law with 'But I say to you'. This consistent pattern throughout the Sermon on the Mount demonstrates Jesus assuming the position and authority of the Divine Lawgiver Himself.
verse_bank.mt_1_21: The angel declares that Jesus will 'save his people from their sins.' In the Old Testament, redeeming Israel from their sins is exclusively the action of Yahweh (Psalm 130:8, Isaiah 43:25). Here, Jesus is portrayed as having the inherent power to forgive and save from sin, an action reserved solely for God.
verse_bank.mt_1_23: Matthew applies the title 'Immanuel' to Jesus, which explicitly translates to 'God with us.' By linking Jesus to the prophecy of Isaiah 7:14, the text claims that Jesus is not merely a human prophet, but the very presence of Yahweh dwelling physically among His people.
verse_bank.mt_2_2: The Magi declare they have come to 'worship' the newborn king. In the Old Testament, religious worship is strictly and exclusively reserved for Yahweh alone (Exodus 34:14). The text presents their desire to worship Him as a righteous and correct action.
verse_bank.mt_2_6: This verse quotes Micah 5:2, prophesying a ruler who will 'shepherd my people Israel.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is explicitly identified as the true Shepherd of Israel (Psalm 23:1, Psalm 80:1). Additionally, the full prophecy in Micah 5:2 states that this ruler's origins are 'from ancient times' or 'from eternity,' an attribute belonging exclusively to the eternal God.
verse_bank.mt_2_11: The Magi fall down and worship the child Jesus. While prophets, apostles, and angels in the biblical text strictly refuse worship and direct it to God alone (Acts 10:25-26, Revelation 19:10), Jesus rightfully receives it here without any divine correction, demonstrating His divine prerogative.
verse_bank.2tm_3_11: Paul states that 'the Lord' (Christ) rescued him from all his afflictions. This mirrors Psalm 34:19 (LXX 33:20), where it is Yahweh who delivers the righteous out of all their afflictions, attributing a divine action to Jesus.
verse_bank.2tm_3_15: The verse identifies salvation as coming through faith in Christ Jesus. In Isaiah 43:11, Yahweh declares that besides Him there is no savior. By making Christ the object of saving faith, He is placed in the exclusive divine role of Savior.
verse_bank.2tm_4_1: Christ is described as the one who will 'judge the living and the dead.' In the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 50:6, 96:13), being the judge of the whole earth is an exclusive prerogative and action of Yahweh.
verse_bank.2tm_4_8: Paul calls Christ (whose 'appearing' Christians await) 'the Lord, the righteous judge.' In the Old Testament, being the righteous judge is a specific title and role reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 7:11).
verse_bank.2tm_4_14: Paul states that 'the Lord' (Christ) will repay Alexander according to his deeds. In Proverbs 24:12 and Psalm 62:12, rendering to a man according to his works is an action explicitly attributed to Yahweh.
verse_bank.2tm_4_17: Paul attributes his rescue 'from the lion's mouth' to the Lord (Christ). This directly parallels prayers and acts of deliverance reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament, such as in Psalm 22:21 (LXX 21:22), where the psalmist cries out to God for salvation from the lion's mouth.
verse_bank.2tm_4_18: Paul offers a doxology ('To him be the glory forever and ever') to the Lord (Christ), the ruler of the heavenly kingdom. In Isaiah 42:8, Yahweh explicitly states He will not share His glory with another. Ascribing eternal glory to Christ is treating Him as God.
verse_bank.2tm_4_22: Paul blesses Timothy by asking that 'the Lord' (Christ) be with his spirit. The ability to be spiritually present with believers everywhere implies divine omnipresence, an attribute belonging solely to God.
verse_bank.2tm_1_2: Paul places Christ on an equal level with God the Father as the fountain of 'grace, mercy, and peace.' In the Old Testament, pronouncing and granting such divine blessing and peace is an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., the Aaronic blessing in Numbers 6:24-26).
verse_bank.2tm_1_9: The verse states that grace was given in Christ 'before the ages began', pointing to Christ's eternal pre-existence before time. In the Old Testament, being eternal and existing before creation is an attribute exclusive to God (e.g., Psalm 90:2).
verse_bank.2tm_1_10: Jesus is called 'Savior' and performs the action of abolishing death. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly states there is no Savior besides Him (Isaiah 43:11). Furthermore, permanently destroying death is an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh (Isaiah 25:8).
verse_bank.2tm_1_18: Paul prays for 'the Lord' (Jesus) to grant mercy from 'the Lord' (the Father) on the Day of Judgment. This echoes Old Testament texts displaying a multi-personal Yahweh, like Genesis 19:24. Additionally, presiding over the Final Judgment is exclusively the role of Yahweh on the 'Day of the Lord' (Joel 2).
verse_bank.2tm_2_7: Paul states that 'the Lord' (Jesus) will give Timothy understanding in everything. In the Old Testament, it is explicitly Yahweh who gives wisdom, knowledge, and understanding (Proverbs 2:6). Jesus is thus performing an action of God.
verse_bank.2tm_2_10: Paul identifies Christ as the source of salvation accompanied by 'eternal glory.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh declares that salvation belongs to Him alone (Jonah 2:9) and explicitly states He will never share His divine glory with another (Isaiah 42:8).
verse_bank.2tm_2_13: This verse attributes absolute immutability to Christ, stating He 'cannot deny himself.' In the Old Testament, being entirely unchanging and perfectly true to one's divine nature is an attribute that belongs uniquely to Yahweh (Malachi 3:6).
verse_bank.2tm_2_19: Paul quotes Numbers 16:5, where Moses states that 'Yahweh will show who is his,' and applies this to 'the Lord' (Jesus) regarding the Church. By taking an Old Testament passage about Yahweh and applying it to Christ, Paul identifies Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.2tm_2_22: Believers are identified as those who 'call on the Lord' (Jesus). In the Old Testament, 'calling on the name of the Lord' is the standard phrase for offering divine worship and prayer to Yahweh (e.g., Joel 2:32). Praying to Jesus means He receives the worship reserved solely for God.
verse_bank.ti_3_6: Jesus is called 'our Savior'. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4), Yahweh explicitly declares that He alone is the Savior and there is no other. Contextually, verse 4 refers to 'God our Savior', and here Paul applies this exact same exclusive divine title to Jesus, identifying Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.ti_1_4: The Old Testament explicitly states that Yahweh is the only Savior and there is no other (Isaiah 43:11). By calling Jesus 'our Savior', Paul applies a title exclusively reserved for Yahweh to Jesus.
verse_bank.ti_2_13: Paul explicitly calls Jesus 'our great God and Savior.' Not only is He directly called God, but He is given the title of Savior, which Yahweh claims exclusively for Himself in the Old Testament.
verse_bank.ti_2_14: Jesus purifies 'for himself a people for his own possession.' In the Old Testament (Deuteronomy 7:6, Exodus 19:5), taking a people as a special possession is an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh. Jesus claiming a people for Himself is a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.col_1_15: Christ is called the 'image of the invisible God.' He is the exact visible manifestation of the invisible God, revealing the divine nature perfectly to creation.
verse_bank.col_1_16: The verse states 'by him all things were created' and 'all things were created through him and for him.' In Isaiah 44:24, Yahweh declares that He created all things alone and by Himself. Ascribing the creation of the universe to Jesus is a direct attribution of Yahweh's exclusive prerogative.
verse_bank.col_1_17: Paul writes that 'he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.' This attributes eternal pre-existence and the divine providence of sustaining the universe to Jesus, an action and authority reserved exclusively for God (e.g., Nehemiah 9:6).
verse_bank.col_1_19: The text declares 'in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell.' This asserts that the entirety of the divine nature and attributes resides in Jesus.
verse_bank.col_2_3: In Christ 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.' Complete and perfect wisdom is an attribute exclusive to Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., Proverbs 2:6, Daniel 2:20-21). Ascribing 'all the treasures' of omniscience to Christ is a claim to a divine attribute.
verse_bank.col_2_9: The text states 'in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily.' This is an explicit, unambiguous statement that the complete essence and nature of God permanently resides in the incarnate Christ. The presence of Yahweh that once filled the Old Testament Temple now dwells fully in the body of Jesus.
verse_bank.col_3_1: Christ is described as being 'seated at the right hand of God,' fulfilling Psalm 110:1. Sharing the divine throne and sovereign rule over the cosmos is a position exclusively reserved for God.
verse_bank.col_3_4: Christ is explicitly called 'your life'. In the Old Testament, being the very life of the believers is a title and attribute explicitly reserved for Yahweh (Deuteronomy 30:20).
verse_bank.col_3_11: Paul states that 'Christ is all, and in all', attributing omnipresence to Him. In the Old Testament, filling all things is an attribute that belongs exclusively to God (Jeremiah 23:24).
verse_bank.col_3_13: Paul commands believers to forgive just as the Lord (Christ) has forgiven them. The authority to forgive sins is an exclusive prerogative of God in the Old Testament (Psalm 130:4, cf. Mark 2:7).
verse_bank.col_3_16: Paul refers to the 'word of Christ' dwelling in believers, a direct parallel to the Old Testament concept of the 'word of Yahweh' (e.g., Jeremiah 1:2), showing Christ's words carry the exact same divine authority.
verse_bank.col_3_17: Believers are commanded to do everything 'in the name of the Lord Jesus'. In the Old Testament, God's people are commanded to walk and act exclusively in the name of Yahweh (Micah 4:5, Zechariah 10:12).
verse_bank.col_3_24: Paul states 'You are serving the Lord Christ.' The Old Testament strictly commands that God's people are to serve Yahweh alone (1 Samuel 7:3, Deuteronomy 6:13). By instructing them to serve Christ, Paul attributes divine status to Him.
verse_bank.col_3_25: The preceding verse states believers serve the Lord Christ, and this verse states He pays back wrongdoers with 'no partiality'. Being the ultimate, impartial judge of mankind is a role reserved only for Yahweh (Deuteronomy 10:17).
verse_bank.col_4_12: Epaphras is called a 'servant [slave] of Christ Jesus'. In the Old Testament, the prophets and believers are exclusively identified as 'slaves of Yahweh' (e.g., Amos 3:7). Calling believers slaves of Christ attributes to Him the ownership over believers that belongs only to God.
verse_bank.1thes_1_1: Paul addresses the church as existing 'in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.' He places Jesus on the exact same level as the Father as the single divine sphere or dwelling place of the believers, a concept reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 90:1).
verse_bank.1thes_1_3: The Old Testament repeatedly emphasizes that a believer's steadfast hope and trust must be placed in Yahweh alone (e.g., Psalm 13:6, 17:2 in the Septuagint). Here, Paul explicitly names 'our Lord Jesus Christ' as the object of their steadfast hope.
verse_bank.1thes_1_8: In the Old Testament, the phrase 'the word of the Lord' refers exclusively to the word of Yahweh. Here, Paul applies this exact divine phrase to the gospel message of Jesus, equating His word with the word of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1thes_1_10: In the Old Testament, God alone is the Savior who delivers His people from divine judgment and wrath (e.g., Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4). Here, Jesus assumes this exclusive divine role as the Savior who delivers believers from the eschatological wrath to come.
verse_bank.1thes_2_19: The Old Testament prophesies the 'Day of the Lord' when Yahweh Himself will come to judge and reign (Zechariah 14:5). Paul applies this eschatological event directly to the 'coming' (Parousia) of the Lord Jesus, identifying Jesus' return with the promised coming of Yahweh.
verse_bank.1thes_5_2: In the Old Testament, the eschatological day of judgment is exclusively the 'Day of Yahweh' (e.g., Joel 2:1, Zephaniah 1:14). Here, Paul refers to this as the 'day of the Lord', which in the context of this letter (e.g., 1 Thess 4:15, 5:23) is the return of the Lord Jesus Christ. Applying the Day of Yahweh to Jesus equates Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.1thes_5_27: Paul puts the Thessalonians under an oath 'by the Lord' (Jesus). In the Old Testament, taking oaths and adjuring others was to be done exclusively in the name of Yahweh (Deuteronomy 6:13, 10:20). Swearing an oath by Jesus treats Him with the reverence and authority reserved for God.
verse_bank.1thes_5_28: Paul bestows divine grace directly from Jesus Christ. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the source of grace and divine blessing (e.g., Numbers 6:24-26, Psalm 84:11). Equating Jesus as the source of divine grace implies His divinity.
verse_bank.1thes_3_11: In the Old Testament, directing a person's path is an action reserved exclusively for Yahweh (Proverbs 16:9). Here, Paul prays to both God the Father and the Lord Jesus to direct their way, treating Jesus as co-equal in divine providence and capable of answering prayer.
verse_bank.1thes_3_12: Paul prays to 'the Lord' (Jesus) to make the Thessalonians increase and abound in love. The internal, spiritual work of transforming the human heart is a divine prerogative, as seen in Deuteronomy 30:6 where Yahweh acts to make His people love Him.
verse_bank.1thes_3_13: Zechariah 14:5 prophesies that 'the Lord my God shall come, and all the saints with him.' Paul directly applies this Old Testament prophecy about Yahweh to 'the coming of our Lord Jesus with all his saints,' explicitly identifying Jesus as Yahweh.
verse_bank.1thes_4_6: Paul warns that 'the Lord is an avenger.' In the Old Testament, vengeance is a role belonging exclusively to Yahweh (Deuteronomy 32:35, Psalm 94:1). By using the title 'Lord' (Kurios), which Paul consistently uses for Jesus in this epistle, he attributes a divine prerogative to Christ.
verse_bank.1thes_4_16: Jesus descends with a cry of command to raise the dead. In the Old Testament, giving life and raising the dead by one's own command is an action of Yahweh (1 Samuel 2:6). Unlike prophets who had to pray to God to raise the dead, Jesus does it by His own inherent authority.
verse_bank.1thes_4_17: In the Old Testament, the ultimate hope and eternal reward of the believer is to dwell in the presence of Yahweh forever (Psalm 23:6). Paul states that the eternal destiny of believers is to 'always be with the Lord' (Jesus), placing Jesus in the ultimate position of God.
verse_bank.heb_7_3: The text states that Melchizedek's lack of a recorded genealogy makes him 'resemble the Son of God,' implying the Son truly has 'neither beginning of days nor end of life.' In the Old Testament, absolute eternality from everlasting to everlasting is an attribute exclusive to Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 90:2, Isaiah 44:6).
verse_bank.heb_7_25: Jesus is described as being 'able to save to the uttermost' (completely). In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly declares that He alone is the Savior and there is no other besides Him (Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4). The inherent power to provide ultimate salvation is a divine prerogative.
verse_bank.heb_7_26: Jesus is described as being 'exalted above the heavens.' In the Psalms, being exalted above the heavens is a specific description of supreme majesty and glory reserved uniquely for Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 57:5, Psalm 113:4). The author applies this exclusive divine status directly to Jesus.
verse_bank.heb_8_1: Jesus is seated 'at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh's throne represents His sovereign rule over the universe, and He declares He will not share His glory with another (Isaiah 42:8). For Jesus to sit on the divine throne alongside God signifies that He shares in Yahweh's sovereign authority.
verse_bank.heb_5_9: In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly declares that He is the only Savior and the sole source of salvation (e.g., Isaiah 43:11, 'besides me there is no savior'). Here, Jesus is declared to be the 'source of eternal salvation,' thereby exercising a role and title exclusively reserved for God.
verse_bank.heb_13_8: Jesus is described as unchanging and eternal ('the same yesterday and today and forever'). In the Old Testament, absolute immutability is an attribute exclusively reserved for Yahweh (e.g., Malachi 3:6, Psalm 101:27 LXX).
verse_bank.heb_13_12: Jesus is said to 'sanctify the people' through His own blood. In the Old Testament, Yahweh explicitly declares Himself as the exclusive sanctifier of His people (e.g., Exodus 31:13, Ezekiel 20:12: 'I am the Lord that sanctifies them').
verse_bank.heb_13_21: The doxology ascribes eternal glory to Jesus Christ ('to whom be glory forever and ever'). In the Old Testament, Yahweh repeatedly states He will not share His glory with another (e.g., Isaiah 42:8, 48:11), meaning Jesus receives worship due only to God.
verse_bank.heb_1_2: In the Old Testament, Yahweh declares He created the world entirely alone (e.g., Isaiah 44:24). The assertion that the world was created through the Son means the Son intimately shares in this exclusive divine prerogative of creation.
verse_bank.heb_1_3: Sustaining all of creation is an ongoing action unique to God (e.g., Nehemiah 9:6). The text states the Son upholds the universe by the word of His power, demonstrating His inherent divine nature and authority to sustain existence.
verse_bank.heb_1_6: The author quotes Old Testament passages (such as Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX or Psalm 97:7) where angels are explicitly commanded to worship Yahweh, but applies the command directly to the Son. Receiving worship from the heavenly host is exclusively a divine right.
verse_bank.heb_1_8: Quoting Psalm 44:7, the Father explicitly addresses the Son with the title 'O God'. This is a direct and undeniable attribution of the Divine title to Christ.
verse_bank.heb_1_10: The preceding verses establish the Son as the subject being spoken of. Here, the author quotes Psalm 101:25-27—a passage originally addressed in the Old Testament to Yahweh as the eternal Creator of heaven and earth—and applies it directly to Jesus, directly portraying Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.heb_1_12: Continuing the quote from Psalm 102 regarding the Son, He is described as eternally unchangeable ('you are the same'). In the Old Testament, absolute immutability and eternality are attributes reserved exclusively for Yahweh (e.g., Malachi 3:6).
verse_bank.heb_11_26: Moses is said to have borne 'the reproach of Christ' centuries before the incarnation. In the Old Testament, Moses served and suffered for Yahweh exclusively. By identifying Moses' loyalty and suffering as being for Christ, the author equates the pre-existent Christ with Yahweh, the God of the Exodus.
verse_bank.heb_12_2: Jesus is described as the 'founder and perfecter of our faith' who is 'seated at the right hand of the throne of God.' Sharing the very throne of God implies equality with God and the exercise of universal divine sovereignty, fulfilling the divine prerogative seen in Psalm 110:1.
verse_bank.heb_12_26: The preceding verses (24-25) identify Jesus as the one speaking and warning from heaven. Verse 26 attributes two exclusive divine actions to Him: His voice shook the earth at Sinai (Exodus 19:18), and He promises to shake the heavens and earth again. In Haggai 2 (v. 6 in Hebrew/English, v. 7 in LXX), it is explicitly 'the Lord Almighty' (Yahweh) who makes this exact promise. Thus, Jesus is acting as Yahweh.
verse_bank.2thes_3_3: Paul states that 'the Lord' (Jesus) will guard them from the evil one. In the Old Testament, guarding and preserving from evil is specifically described as an action of Yahweh (e.g., Psalm 121:7, LXX 120:7). By attributing this divine preservation to Jesus, Paul identifies Him with Yahweh's actions.
verse_bank.2thes_3_6: Paul commands the church 'in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ'. In the Old Testament, prophets delivered commands strictly 'in the name of Yahweh' (e.g., Deuteronomy 18:19). Invoking Jesus' name as the ultimate source of divine authority functionally replaces the name of Yahweh with that of Jesus.
verse_bank.2thes_3_16: Paul calls Jesus 'the Lord of peace' who personally gives peace, and prays 'The Lord be with you all.' In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the source of peace (Psalm 29:11) and the standard divine blessing was 'Yahweh be with you' (Ruth 2:4). Paul substitutes Jesus for Yahweh in both dispensing divine peace and in His omnipresent accompaniment.
verse_bank.2thes_1_7: In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the one who is revealed from heaven accompanied by His angels or holy ones (e.g., Deut 33:2, Zech 14:5). Here, the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven and the angels are described as belonging to Him ('his mighty angels'), assigning Him a role and a heavenly host reserved exclusively for God.
verse_bank.2thes_1_8: Isaiah 66:15 states that Yahweh will come in fire to render His vengeance with a flame of fire. Paul applies this exact divine prerogative to Jesus, describing Him as coming in flaming fire to inflict vengeance on His enemies.
verse_bank.2thes_1_9: This verse directly borrows the exact phrasing 'from the glory of his might' used in the Septuagint translation of Isaiah 2:10, 19, and 21, which repeatedly describes the terror of Yahweh's judgment. Paul uses this Old Testament description of Yahweh's judgment and applies it directly to the Lord Jesus.
verse_bank.2thes_1_10: Psalm 88:7 in the Septuagint (Psalm 89:7 in English) declares that God is glorified in the council of the saints and is marveled at by those around Him. Paul directly applies this Old Testament imagery to Jesus, stating He will come to be 'glorified in his saints, and to be marveled at'.
verse_bank.2thes_1_12: The glorification of the 'name' is an act of worship reserved exclusively for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 86:12). Here, Paul prays that the name of the Lord Jesus may be glorified, directing divine worship toward Christ.
verse_bank.2thes_2_2: The preceding verse establishes the context as the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ. Here, Paul refers to His coming as the 'day of the Lord'. In the Old Testament, the Day of the Lord (Day of Yahweh) is the specific eschatological day of God's judgment (e.g., Joel 2, Amos 5). Identifying Jesus' return with the Day of Yahweh equates Him with God.
verse_bank.2thes_2_8: Destroying the wicked with the 'breath of his mouth' (or breath of his wrath) is an action attributed directly to God in the Old Testament (Job 4:9). Additionally, the word 'appearance' (epiphaneia) used for His coming was a term commonly reserved for the physical manifestation of God.
verse_bank.2thes_2_17: The preceding verse establishes 'our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God our Father' as the combined subject of this prayer. Jesus is co-invoked with the Father to 'comfort your hearts and establish them'. Providing such eternal, spiritual comfort is a divine action reserved for Yahweh in the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 51:12).
verse_bank.2pt_1_1: The grammar explicitly identifies Jesus as both 'God' and 'Savior'. In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 43:11), Yahweh declares He is the only God and emphatically states there is no Savior besides Him. Applying these exclusive titles to Jesus identifies Him as Yahweh.
verse_bank.2pt_1_11: The text describes the 'eternal kingdom' as belonging to Jesus. In the Old Testament (e.g., Psalm 145:13, Daniel 4:3), having an eternal and everlasting kingdom is an attribute strictly reserved for Yahweh's sovereign reign.
verse_bank.2pt_2_1: Jesus is called the sovereign 'Master' who 'bought' (redeemed) them. In the Old Testament, Yahweh is the exclusive Master who 'bought' or redeemed His people (e.g., Deuteronomy 32:6, Psalm 74:2). Ascribing this sovereign act of spiritual purchase to Jesus demonstrates His divine prerogative.
verse_bank.2pt_2_20: Jesus is repeatedly given the title of 'Savior' throughout the epistle. The Old Testament makes it emphatically clear (Isaiah 43:11, Hosea 13:4) that Yahweh is the only Savior, establishing Jesus's divine identity.
verse_bank.2pt_3_2: Jesus is referred to as 'the Lord and Savior' (contextualized by 2 Peter 1:11, 2:20, and 3:18). In the Old Testament (e.g., Isaiah 43:11), Yahweh explicitly declares that He alone is God and there is no savior besides Him. Applying this exclusive title to Jesus equates Him with Yahweh.
verse_bank.2pt_3_8: In the context of the promised return of Jesus ('his coming' in v. 4), Peter states that 'with the Lord one day is as a thousand years,' applying the language of Psalm 90:4 to Christ. In the Old Testament, this transcendence over time is an attribute strictly belonging to Yahweh.
verse_bank.2pt_3_10: Peter refers to the return of Jesus as 'the day of the Lord' (equated with 'the day of God' in verse 12). In the Old Testament prophets (like Joel 2 and Zechariah 14), 'the Day of the Lord' is exclusively the day of Yahweh's eschatological judgment. Peter takes this uniquely divine event and applies it directly to Jesus's return.
verse_bank.2pt_3_18: Peter concludes by ascribing eternal glory to Jesus: 'To him be the glory both now and to the day of eternity.' In the Old Testament (Isaiah 42:8), Yahweh strictly states He will not give His glory to another. Offering an eternal doxology to Jesus is an act of worship reserved only for God.

Ignatius of Antioch.note: Lived c. AD 35 to AD 108. Direct disciple of John the Apostle.

quotes.quote: Look for Him who is above all time , eternal and invisible, yet who became visible for our sakes; impalpable and impassible, yet who became passible on our account; and who in every kind of way suffered for our sakes.
I pray for your happiness for ever in our God , Jesus Christ , by whom continue in the unity and under the protection of God .
I Glorify God , even Jesus Christ , who has given you such wisdom.

You have done well in receiving Philo and Rheus Agathopus as servants of Christ our God , who have followed me for the sake of God , and who give thanks to the Lord in your behalf, because you have in every way refreshed them.
And this will be the case with you if you are not puffed up, and continue in intimate union with Jesus Christ our God, and the bishop, and the enactments of the apostles.
the Church which is beloved and enlightened by the will of Him that wills all things which are according to the love of Jesus Christ our God

to those who are united, both according to the flesh and spirit, to every one of His commandments; who are filled inseparably with the grace of God, and are purified from every strange taint, [I wish] abundance of happiness unblameably, in Jesus Christ our God.
For our God, Jesus Christ, now that He is with the Father, is all the more revealed [in His glory].
Permit me to be an imitator of the passion of my God.
being united and elected through the true passion by the will of the Father, and Jesus Christ, our God
Being the followers of God, and stirring up yourselves by the blood of God, you have perfectly accomplished the work which was beseeming to you.

There is one Physician who is possessed both of flesh and spirit; both made and not made; God existing in flesh; true life in death; both of Mary and of God; first passible and then impassible — even Jesus Christ our Lord.
Let us therefore do all things as those who have Him dwelling in us, that we may be His temples, and He may be in us as our God, which indeed He is, and will manifest Himself before our faces.
For our God, Jesus Christ, was, according to the appointment of God, conceived in the womb by Mary, of the seed of David, but by the Holy Ghost.

Hence every kind of magic was destroyed, and every bond of wickedness disappeared; ignorance was removed, and the old kingdom abolished, God Himself being manifested in human form for the renewal of eternal life.

Since therefore I have, in the persons before mentioned, beheld the whole multitude of you in faith and love, I exhort you to study to do all things with a divine harmony, while your bishop presides in the place of God, and your presbyters in the place of the assembly of the apostles, along with your deacons, who are most dear to me, and are entrusted with the ministry of Jesus Christ, who was with the Father before the beginning of time, and in the end was revealed.

There is one Jesus Christ, than whom nothing is more excellent. Therefore run together as into one temple of God, as to one altar, as to one Jesus Christ, who came forth from one Father, and is with and has gone to one.

For the divinest prophets lived according to Christ Jesus. On this account also they were persecuted, being inspired by His grace to fully convince the unbelieving that there is one God, who has manifested Himself by Jesus Christ His Son, who is His eternal Word, not proceeding forth from silence, and who in all things pleased Him that sent Him.

Clement of Rome.note: Lived c. AD 35 to AD 99. Co-worker of Paul.

quotes.quote: As God liveth, and the Lord Jesus Christ liveth and the Holy Spirit — the faith and hope of the elect, so surely he that performeth

The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you and with all men in all places who have been called by God and through Him, through whom is glory and honour , power and greatness and eternal dominion, unto Him, from the ages past and for ever and ever. Amen .

Polycarp.note: Lived c. AD 69 to AD 155. Direct disciple of John the Apostle.

quotes.quote: To Him all things in heaven and on earth are subject. Him every spirit serves. He comes as the Judge of the living and the dead.

But may the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, and Jesus Christ Himself, who is the Son of God, and our everlasting High Priest, build you up in faith and truth, and in all meekness, gentleness, patience, long-suffering, forbearance, and purity; and may He bestow on you a lot and portion among His saints, and on us with you, and on all that are under heaven, who shall believe in our Lord Jesus Christ, and in His Father, who raised Him from the dead.

Church of Smyrna.note: Written c. AD 155, detailing the martyrdom of Polycarp.

quotes.quote: Wherefore also I praise You for all things, I bless You, I glorify You, along with the everlasting and heavenly Jesus Christ, Your beloved Son, with whom, to You, and the Holy Ghost, be glory both now and to all coming ages. Amen.

it is neither possible for us ever to forsake Christ, who suffered for the salvation of such as shall be saved throughout the whole world (the blameless one for sinners), nor to worship any other. For Him indeed, as being the Son of God, we adore; but the martyrs, as disciples and followers of the Lord, we worthily love on account of their extraordinary affection towards their own King and Master
Jesus Christ being King for ever, to whom be glory, honour, majesty, and an everlasting throne, from generation to generation. Amen.

Mathetes.note: Anonymous writer, late 1st or early 2nd century.

quotes.quote: He did not, as one might have imagined, send to men any servant, or angel, or ruler, or any one of those who bear sway over earthly things, or one of those to whom the government of things in the heavens has been entrusted, but the very Creator and Fashioner of all things — by whom He made the heavens — by whom he enclosed the sea within its proper bounds

As a king sends his son, who is also a king, so sent He Him; as God He sent Him; as to men He sent Him; as a Saviour He sent Him, and as seeking to persuade, not to compel us; for violence has no place in the character of God.

This is He who was from the beginning, who appeared as if new, and was found old, and yet who is ever born afresh in the hearts of the saints. This is He who, being from everlasting, is today called the Son

And further, my brethren: if the Lord endured to suffer for our soul, He being Lord of all the world, to whom God said at the foundation of the world, Let us make man after our image, and after our likeness, understand how it was that He endured to suffer at the hand of men.

Then He manifested Himself to be the Son of God. For if He had not come in the flesh, how could men have been saved by beholding Him? Since looking upon the sun which is to cease to exist, and is the work of His hands, their eyes are not able to bear his rays.

If therefore the Son of God, who is Lord [of all things], and who will judge the living and the dead, suffered, that His stroke might give us life, let us believe that the Son of God could not have suffered except for our sakes.

Justin Martyr.note: Lived c. AD 100 to AD 165. Early Christian Apologist and Martyr.

quotes.quote: Henceforth, you Greeks, come and partake of incomparable wisdom, and be instructed by the Divine Word, and acquaint yourselves with the King immortal; and do not recognise those men as heroes who slaughter whole nations. For our own Ruler, the Divine Word, who even now constantly aids us, does not desire strength of body and beauty of feature, nor yet the high spirit of earth's nobility, but a pure soul, fortified by holiness, and the watchwords of our King, holy actions, for through the Word power passes into the soul.

For they who affirm that the Son is the Father , are proved neither to have become acquainted with the Father , nor to know that the Father of the universe has a Son; who also, being the first-begotten Word of God , is even God.

The Jews , accordingly, being throughout of opinion that it was the Father of the universe who spoke to Moses , though He who spoke to him was indeed the Son of God , who is called both Angel and Apostle, are justly charged, both by the Spirit of prophecy and by Christ Himself, with knowing neither the Father nor the Son.

For at that juncture, when Moses was ordered to go down into Egypt and lead out the people of the Israelites who were there, and while he was tending the flocks of his maternal uncle  in the land of Arabia, our Christ conversed with him under the appearance of fire from a bush, and said, Put off your shoes, and draw near and hear.

But both Him, and the Son (who came forth from Him and taught us these things, and the host of the other good angels who follow and are made like to Him),  and the prophetic Spirit, we worship and adore, knowing them in reason and truth , and declaring without grudging to every one who wishes to learn, as we have been taught.

Our teacher of these things is Jesus Christ , who also was born for this purpose, and was crucified under Pontius Pilate , procurator of Judæa, in the times of Tiberius Cæsar ; and that we reasonably worship Him, having learned that He is the Son of the true God Himself, and holding Him in the second place, and the prophetic Spirit in the third, we will prove.

Jesus Christ is the only proper Son who has been begotten by God , being His Word and first-begotten, and power; and, becoming man according to His will , He  taught us these things for the conversion and restoration of the human race
And the first power after God the Father and Lord of all is the Word, who is also the Son; and of Him we will, in what follows, relate how He took flesh and became man.

For with what reason should we believe of a crucified man that He is the first-born of the unbegotten God , and Himself will pass judgment on the whole human race , unless we had found testimonies concerning Him published before He came and was born as man, and unless we saw that things had happened accordingly

It is the Word of God which he here names the voice, by whom heaven and earth and the whole creation were made, as the divine prophecies of the holy men teach us; and these he himself also paid some attention to in Egypt , and understood that all creation was made by the Word of God

recognise how great are the benefits which she will confer upon you by predicting, as she does in a clear and patent manner, the advent of our Saviour Jesus Christ ; who, being the Word of God , inseparable from Him in power, having assumed man, who had been made in the image and likeness of God , restored to us the knowledge of the religion of our ancient forefathers

And the Word, being His Son, came to us, having put on flesh, revealing both Himself and the Father, giving to us in Himself resurrection from the dead, and eternal life afterwards. And this is Jesus Christ, our Saviour and Lord.

But if our physician Christ, God, having rescued us from our desires, regulates our flesh with His own wise and temperate rule, it is evident that He guards it from sins because it possesses a hope of salvation, as physicians do not suffer men whom they hope to save to indulge in what pleasures they please.

And His Son, who alone is properly called Son, the Word who also was with Him and was begotten before the works, when at first He created and arranged all things by Him, is called Christ, in reference to His being anointed and God's ordering all things through Him

For next to God , we worship and love the Word who is from the unbegotten and ineffable God , since also He became man for our sakes, that becoming a partaker of our sufferings, He might also bring us healing.
He was born of the very substance of the Father, not that this substance was divided, but He proceeds from it as one fire does from another at which it is lit (cxxviii, lxi)
The Word is God (I Apol., lxiii; Dialogue with Trypho 34 , 36 , 37 , 56 , 63 , 76 , 86 , 87 , 113 , 115 , 125 , 126 and 128 ).

That according to which we worship the God of the Christians, whom we reckon to be one from the beginning, the maker and fashioner of the whole creation, visible and invisible; and the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God, who had also been preached beforehand by the prophets as about to be present with the race of men, the herald of salvation and teacher of good disciples. And I, being a man, think that what I can say is insignificant in comparison with His boundless divinity, acknowledging a certain prophetic power, since it was prophesied concerning Him of whom now I say that He is the Son of God.

By the two birds Christ is denoted, both dead as man, and living as God. He is likened to a bird, because He is understood and declared to be from above, and from heaven. And the living bird, having been dipped in the blood of the dead one, was afterwards let go. For the living and divine Word was in the crucified and dead temple [of the body], as being a partaker of the passion, and yet impassible to God.

The goat that was sent away presented a type of Him who takes away the sins of men. But the two contained a representation of the one economy of God incarnate. For He was wounded for our transgressions, and He bore the sins of many, and He was delivered for our iniquities.

Tatian.note: Lived c. AD 120 to AD 180. Apologist.

quotes.quote: God was in the beginning; but the beginning, we have been taught, is the power of the Logos. For the Lord of the universe, who is Himself the necessary ground (ὑπόστασις) of all being, inasmuch as no creature was yet in existence, was alone; but inasmuch as He was all power, Himself the necessary ground of things visible and invisible, with Him were all things; with Him, by Logos-power (διὰ λογικῆς δυνάμεως), the Logos Himself also, who was in Him, subsists. ... For just as from one torch many fires are lighted, but the light of the first torch is not lessened by the kindling of many torches, so the Logos, coming forth from the Logos-power of the Father, has not divested of the Logos-power Him who begot Him.

The Logos, in truth, is the light of God, but the ignorant soul is darkness. ... And the souls that are obedient to wisdom have attracted to themselves the cognate spirit; but the disobedient, rejecting the minister of the suffering God, have shown themselves to be fighters against God, rather than His worshippers.
We do not act as fools, O Greeks, nor utter idle tales, when we announce that God was born in the form of a man.

Irenaeus.note: Lived c. AD 130 to AD 202. Disciple of Polycarp (who was a disciple of John).

quotes.quote: The sacred books acknowledge with regard to Christ, that as He is the Son of man, so is the same Being not a [mere] man; and as He is flesh, so is He also spirit, and the Word of God, and God.
